translations.source: Àtakò kòkòrò bakitéríà sí òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò (AMR) tí wọ́n fi wo àwọn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀, pàápàá jùlọ àtọ̀sí, ti gbèrú sí i púpọ̀ lẹ́nu lọ́ọ́lọ́ọ́ yìí bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ti ṣe àdínkù àwọn àbájáde àṣeyọrí ìtọ́jú. Àwọn àbájáde láti inú Ètò Àmójútó Kòkòrò àrùn àtọ̀sí Alòdìsí-òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò (GASP) fi àwọn ìwọ̀n ńlá hàn sí ìlòdìsí quinolone hàn, tí ó ń mú ìgbèrú bá ìlòdìsí òògùn azithromycin àti àkójọ-gbòòrò cephalosporin alòdìsí-òògùn tí ó ṣẹ̀ṣẹ̀ bẹ̀rẹ̀. Ìfarahàn alékún agbára àtọ̀sí láti rí ìwòsàn tí ó ń dín kù dórí ìgbòòrò àkójọ cephalosporin, àti ìfìdímúlẹ̀ ìlòdìsí pẹnisilíìnì, sulphonamides, tetravyclines, quinolones àti macrolides ní àwọn ìpele ńlá mú kí àtọ̀sí jẹ́ ajọ̀fẹ́ tí ó lòdì sí ọ̀pọ̀ òògùn. AMR fún àwọn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ mìíràn, bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé wọn kò wọ́pọ̀, wọ́n wà, èyí tí ó mú kí ìdènà àti ìtọ́jú kankan ṣe pàtàkì. Láti tọ́jú àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ ní kíkún ó ṣe pàtàkì láti lo àwọn òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò tí ó yẹ, pẹ̀lú ìwọ̀n ìlò àti àsìkò tí ó tọ́ fún ẹ̀yà àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ pàtó láti rí dájú pé ìtọ́jú tàbí ìwòsàn pípé wà, àti láti dènà ìdàgbà ìlòdìsí òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò.
translations.reference: Antimicrobial resistance (AMR) to antibiotics used to treat STIs, in particular gonorrhoea, has increased rapidly in recent years and has reduced successful treatment outcomes. Results from the current Gonococcal AMR Surveillance Programme (GASP) show trends of high rates of quinolone resistance, increasing azithromycin resistance and emerging resistance of extended-spectrum cephalosporins. The emergence of decreased susceptibility of gonorrhoea to extended-spectrum cephalosporins, together with established high-levels of resistance to penicillins, sulphonamides, tetracyclines, quinolones and macrolides make gonorrhoea a multidrug-resistant organism. AMR for other STIs, though less common, also exists, making prevention and prompt treatment critical. To adequately treat STIs it is important to take the appropriate antimicrobials, with the correct dose and duration for the specific STI to ensure adequate treatment or cure, and to prevent the development of antimicrobial resistance.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"The rise of antimicrobial resistance (AMR) to gonorrhea, particularly drug-resistant gonorrhea, has become a significant public health concern this year, and it has also increased the failure rates of treatment. Findings from the Gonorrhea Antimicrobial Susceptibility Testing (GASP) program have revealed a substantial increase in quinolone resistance, which is exacerbating the decline in susceptibility to the drug azithromycin and the emergence of cephalosporin resistance, which begins rapidly. The appearance of highly resistant gonorrhea strains, leading to treatment failure even with cephalosporin combinations, and the decrease in susceptibility to penicillin, sulphonamides, tetracyclines, quinolones, and macrolides at high levels, makes gonorrhea a difficult-to-treat infection with reduced drug options. AMR for other sexually transmitted infections (STIs) exists, although they are less common, which underscores the importance of accurate diagnosis and treatment. To effectively treat gonorrhea, it is crucial to use appropriate antimicrobial drugs, along with careful monitoring and duration of treatment that is suitable for the specific type of gonorrhea to ensure effective treatment or cure, and to prevent the further development of antimicrobial resistance."

translations.source: Sànpọ̀nná Ìsọnísókí: Sànpọ̀nná ni àrùn líle tí máa ń ràn èyí tí kòkòrò àrùn fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà, ọmọ ẹbí fáírọ́ọ̀sì aṣerankoṣènìyàn ń ṣokùnfà. Ó jẹ́ ọ̀kan lára àwọn àìsàn ńlá tí ọmọnìyàn mọ̀ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó fa ẹgbẹ̀lẹ́gbẹ̀ àwọn ikú kí wọ́n tó rẹ́yìn rẹ̀. Ìgbàgbọ́ wà pé ó ti wà fún ó kéré jù ọdún 3000. Abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára sànpọ̀nná, tí Edward Jenner ṣẹ̀dá ní ọdún 1796, ni àṣeyọrí abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára kìíní láti di àgbéjáde.
translations.reference: Smallpox Overview: Smallpox is an acute contagious disease caused by the variola virus, a member of the orthopoxvirus family. It was one of the most devastating diseases known to humanity and caused millions of deaths before it was eradicated. It is believed to have existed for at least 3000 years. The smallpox vaccine, created by Edward Jenner in 1796, was the first successful vaccine to be developed.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Smallpox History: Smallpox is a contagious disease that causes a rash with pustules, a disease that afflicts families, and human populations suffer greatly from it. It is one of the major diseases that caused the deaths of many people before its eradication. It is believed to have existed for at least 3000 years. The smallpox vaccine, created by Edward Jenner in 1796, was the first successful vaccine to be developed.**

Here's a breakdown of some of the key phrases and how they were translated:

*   **Sànpọ̀nná Ìsọnísókí:** Smallpox History
*   **àrùn líle:** contagious disease
*   **kòkòrò àrùn fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà:** a rash with pustules (literally, "the appearance of the smallpox disease that is red")
*   **ọmọnìyàn mọ̀ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó fa ẹgbẹ̀lẹ́gbẹ̀ àwọn ikú:** it caused the deaths of many people (literally, "human populations, it is that which causes a multitude of deaths")
*   **rẹ́yìn rẹ̀:** its eradication
*   **Abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára sànpọ̀nná:** The smallpox vaccine
*   **ṣẹ̀dá:** created
*   **àṣeyọrí abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára kìíní:** the first successful vaccine



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Ó wòye pé àwọn afúnwàrà tí wọ́n ti kọ́kọ́ kó olóde màlúù kò kó sànpọ̀nná bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó fi hàn pé àjẹsára irúfẹ́ kan náà lè ṣe é lò láti dènà sànpọ̀nná fún àwọn ẹlòmíràn. Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ètò gbòòrò láti fòpin sí sànpọ̀nná ní ọdún 1967. Àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára àti àmójútó gbòòrò ní wọ́n ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ káàkiri àgbáyé fún ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ọdún. Ọ̀ràn àìtọọ́dá tí ó gbẹ̀yìn wáyé ní Somalia ní 1977. Ní 1980 WHO kéde òpin àrùn sànpọ̀nná - àrùn tí ó máa ń ràn kan ṣoṣo láti ṣe àṣeyọrí yìí.
translations.reference: He observed that milkmaids who previously had caught cowpox did not catch smallpox and showed that a similar inoculation could be used to prevent smallpox in other people. The World Health Organization launched an intensified plan to eradicate smallpox in 1967. Widespread immunization and surveillance were conducted around the world for several years. The last known natural case was in Somalia in 1977. In 1980 WHO declared smallpox eradicated – the only infectious disease to achieve this distinction.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Yoruba document:

It is known that those who initially contracted measles did not contract smallpox, and this showed that a vaccine of that type could be used to eradicate smallpox for others. The World Health Organization established a smallpox eradication program in 1967. Vaccination campaigns and smallpox eradication efforts were carried out around the world for many years. A major conflict erupted in Somalia in 1977. In 1980, WHO declared the eradication of smallpox – a disease that had to be overcome to achieve this success.

translations.source: Èyí wà lára àwọn àṣeyọrí ìlera àwùjọ tí ó gbajúmọ̀ jùlọ tí ó sì jinlẹ̀ jùlọ nínú ìtàn. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Lára àwọn àmì àrùn sànpọ̀nná tí wọ́n máa ń kọ́kọ́ farahàn ni ibà, ọ̀rẹ̀rẹ̀ àti ẹ̀yìn ríro, àti nígbà mìíràn, inú rírun àti èébì. Ọjọ́ méjì sí 3 lẹ́yìn náà kòkòrò àrùn náà yóò ṣe àbùdá kúrúnà pẹ̀lú àwọn ògòdò tí wọ́n ní àwọn omi tí ó ṣe é rí, èyí tí yóò padà ní ọyún àti nígbẹ̀yìn yóò di kúrúkùsá tí yóò gbẹ yóò sì máa rẹ̀ dànù. Kúrúnà máa ń bẹ̀rẹ̀ láti ojú àti ọwọ́, yóò sì tàn lọ sí àwọn ẹ̀yà ara yòókù. Àwọn ògòdò máa ń bẹ̀rẹ̀ ní àwọn awọ apèsè ikun imú àti ẹnu bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni yóò di egbò láìpẹ́ lẹ́yìn ìṣarajọ.
translations.reference: This remains among the most notable and profound public health successes in history. Symptoms: Early symptoms of smallpox include high fever, fatigue and severe back pain, and less often, abdominal pain and vomiting. Two to 3 days later the virus produces a characteristic rash with bumps full of a clear liquid, which later fill with pus and finally develop a crust that dries and falls off. The rash begins on the face and hands, then spreads to the rest of the body. Lesions develop in the mucous membranes of the nose and mouth and ulcerate soon after formation.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"This is among the most common and most devastating public health successes in history. Symptoms: Among the symptoms of smallpox, the first to appear are fever, chills, and back pain, and sometimes, internal heat and vomiting. Two to three days later, the disease will manifest with pustules filled with fluid, which will then turn into blisters and the skin will become very red and will begin to scab. The scabs will then begin to dry from the face and hands, and will spread to the rest of the body. The pustules will begin in the mouth and throat and will then spread throughout the body after the rash."

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   **Context:** The document describes smallpox, a disease eradicated through vaccination.
*   **Accuracy:** I've aimed for accuracy while maintaining readability in English.
*   **Cultural Nuances:** Some phrases have been adjusted to sound natural in English while preserving the original meaning.
*   **Medical Terminology:** I've used standard English medical terms where appropriate.

translations.source: Sànpọ̀nná máa ń ràn láti ara ènìyàn-sí-ènìyàn nípasẹ̀ àwọn ẹ̀kán omira lásìkò ìfarakínra pẹ̀lú ẹni tí ó ti lùgbàdì tí ó ní àwọn àmì àìsàn náà, tàbí ní àwọn àsìkò mìíràn nípasẹ̀ aṣọ tàbí ibùsùn tí ó ti lùgbàdì. Ó ní àsìkò ìsàba ọjọ́ 7-17 lẹ́yìn ìfaragbá bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni yóò di ìkóràn ní kété tí ibà bá ti bẹ̀rẹ̀. Àwọn ènìyàn máa ń ní ìkóràn títí di àsìkò tí èèpo ìkẹyìn bá ṣí bọ́. Sànpọ̀nná la ikú lọ nínú àwọn ọ̀ràn 30%.Ìwé ìdánimọ̀ pẹlẹbẹ Àjọ Elétò Ìlera àgbáyé. Èsì WHO: Àsìkò láti ìgbà ìfòpinsí ní ó ti ní àríyànjiyàn gígùn tí ó lọ́jú èyí tí ó fojúsun ìparun àwọn àkójọ alààyè fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà tí ó kù.
translations.reference: Smallpox is transmitted from person to person via infective droplets during close contact with infected people who have symptoms of the disease, or in some cases through contaminated clothing and bedding. It has an incubation period of 7–17 days after exposure and only becomes infectious once a fever develops. People remain infectious until the last scabs fall off. Smallpox was fatal in up to 30% of cases.WHO smallpox recognition card WHO response: The period since eradication has been defined by a lengthy and complex debate focussed on the destruction of the last remaining stocks of live variola virus.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba text into English:

"Chickenpox spreads from person to person through blisters during the rash period with someone who has already had it, or at other times through clothing or bedding that they have used. It has an incubation period of 7-17 days after exposure, and then it becomes a rash. People experience a rash until the secondary infection subsides. Chickenpox is a cause of death in 30% of cases. World Health Organization (WHO) information leaflet. WHO: Time from infection to the onset of a severe condition that focuses on the prevention of complications from childhood diseases, including chickenpox, is crucial."

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   **Specificity:** I've tried to maintain the specific medical terminology where appropriate.
*   **Flow:** I've aimed for a natural and readable English flow.
*   **Context:** The text seems to be an informational leaflet about chickenpox, likely from a health organization.

translations.source: Ní ọdún 1996, ní Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé 49, àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ pinnu láti ba àwọn àkójọ yìí jẹ́ ní Oṣù Kẹfà 1999, àti láti fààyè gba àwọn àpẹẹrẹ kékeré láti jẹ́ ìpamọ́ fún ìwádìí ní àwọn láàbù aláàbò méjì tí wọ́n yà sọ́tọ̀ - ọ̀kan ní United States àti ọkàn ní orílẹ̀ èdè Russia. Ìṣàmúlò ìgbà díẹ̀ àwọn àkójọ fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà ni wọ́n ń ṣàmúlò fún ìwádìí pàtàkì síwájú sí i, èyí tí Àjọ Agbani-nímọ̀ràn fún Ìwádìí Fáírọ́ọ̀sì Fàríólà ń darí. Ẹgbẹ́ yìí máa ń pàdé ní ọdọọdún. Ilé iṣẹ́ Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé fún sànpọ̀nná tí ó wà ní olú ilé iṣẹ́ Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ni wọ́n ń darí gbogbo ọrọ̀ tí ó ní ṣe pẹ̀lú àwọn iṣẹ́ mímú òpin bá sànpọ̀nná àti ẹ̀yìn ìfòpinsí. Lára àwọn èyí ni àmójútó àwọn ìwádìí tí wọ́n fààyè gbà pẹ̀lú ààyè fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà fún ìdàgbàsókè àwọn òté ìgbógunti gẹ́gẹ́ bí abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára àti òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò fáírọ́ọ̀sì.
translations.reference: In 1996, at the 49th World Health Assembly, Member States decided to have this stock destroyed in June 1999, and only to allow small samples to be kept for research purposes at two designated secure laboratories – one in the United States and one in the Russian Federation. Temporary retention of the existing stocks of variola virus are used for further essential research, which is overseen by the Advisory Committee for Variola Virus Research. This group meets on an annual basis. The WHO Smallpox Secretariat based in WHO Headquarters coordinates all smallpox eradication-related and post-eradication activities. These include overseeing permitted research with live variola virus for the development of countermeasures such as vaccine and antivirals.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"In 1996, at the 49th World Health Assembly, member states decided to address these issues in September 1999, and to collect small samples for storage for research in two laboratories – one in the United States and one in Russia. The periodic review of the fair and equitable sharing of benefits was being conducted in preparation for research, which was led by the Committee on Fair and Equitable Sharing of Benefits. This committee meets annually. The World Health Organization's Secretariat's office for access to medicines, located within the World Health Organization's Secretariat, leads all matters related to the work of the secretariat on access to medicines and benefit sharing. Among these are the guidelines for research that collect benefits with fair and equitable sharing for the development of essential products such as vaccines and anti-malarial drugs."

translations.source: Láti àsìkò ìfòpinsí, àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára àti ìtọ́jú pàtó ni wọ́n ti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ fún sànpọ̀nná àti àwọn àìsàn tí wọ́n rí bákan náà gẹ́gẹ́ bí sànpọ̀nná. Ibùdó náà tún ń rí sí ìgbáradì pàjáwìrì àti àkóso àkójọpọ̀ pàjáwìrì abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára sànpọ̀nná ní àwọn ìgbà tí èèyàn kò fi ọkàn sí tí àìsàn náà tún yọjú. Ibùdó náà ń mójútó àmójútó ìpamọ́ ẹ̀dá àti ààbò ẹ̀dá àwọn àkójọpọ̀ fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà tí wọ́n fọwọ́sí gẹ́gẹ́ bí Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé ṣe là á kalẹ̀, àti rírí i dájú pé ìjábọ̀ ọdọọdún wà fún Ẹgbẹ́ Adarí Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé. Ibùdó Sànpọ̀nná ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn àwọn Àká Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé láti ṣe ìpamọ́ ìtàn sànpọ̀nná káríayé àti àkọsílẹ̀ àwọn eko tí wọ́n kọ́ láti ara àṣeyọrí rírí ẹ̀yìn sànpọ̀nná. Lára àwọn ojúṣe àti iṣẹ́ Ibùdó Sànpọ̀nná Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ni:
translations.reference: Since the eradication era, safer vaccines and specific treatments have been developed for smallpox and related diseases such as monkeypox. The Secretariat is also responsible for emergency preparedness and manages a smallpox vaccine emergency stockpile in the unlikely event that the disease re-emerges. The Secretariat manages the biosafety and biosecurity inspections of the authorized variola virus repositories as mandated by the World Health Assembly, and ensures annual reporting to WHO Governing Bodies. The Smallpox Secretariat supports WHO Archives in preserving the global history of smallpox and documenting the lessons learned from successful eradication of smallpox. The WHO Smallpox Secretariat's current roles and responsibilities include:
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"From the perspective of epidemiology, prevention strategies and specific interventions have been established for smallpox and diseases that resemble it very much like smallpox. The focus also extends to the development of ring vaccination and the control of ring vaccination around smallpox contacts in situations where people are not immediately aware of the disease. The focus is also on promoting data storage and data sharing of fair and reliable data as recommended by the World Health Organization, and it is expected that a report will be available for the Executive Board of the World Health Organization. The Smallpox Unit supports the World Health Organization's initiatives to document smallpox history globally and to disseminate the lessons learned from its successful eradication. Among the objectives and activities of the Smallpox Unit of the World Health Organization are:"

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   I've aimed for accuracy while maintaining readability in English.
*   Some Yoruba terms related to public health and epidemiology don't have direct, single-word equivalents in English, so I've used phrases to convey the meaning.
*   "Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé" is consistently translated as "World Health Organization."
*   "Sànpọ̀nná" is consistently translated as "smallpox."



Let me know if you have any other documents you'd like me to translate!

translations.source: Ìwádìí fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà àti ìdàgbàsókè àwọn òté agbógunti. ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ìpàdé ọdọọdún Àjọ Agbani-nímọ̀ràn lórí Ìwádìí Fáírọ́ọ̀sì Fàríólà ti Àjọ Elétò Àgbáyé (ACVVR). àkóso àgbéyẹ̀wò àwọn àlàkalẹ̀ ìwádìí fún iṣẹ́ lórí fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà nípasẹ̀ ACVVR àkóso àwọn ìbáṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú àkójọpọ̀ fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà méjì (tí wọ́n tún jé àwọn Ibùdó Àjọṣepọ̀ WHO) àkásílẹ̀, ṣíṣe iṣẹ́ àti àmójútó àwọn ìbéèrè láti ọwọ́ àwọn aṣèwádìí lórí DNA fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà láti inú àkójọpọ̀ 2 lábẹ́ àwọn pàtó.
translations.reference: Variola virus research and development of countermeasures organizing annual meetings of the WHO Advisory Committee on Variola Virus Research (ACVVR) coordinating review of research proposals for work with variola virus through the ACVVR managing relationships with the two variola virus repositories (which are also WHO Collaborating Centres) recording, processing and monitoring requests from researchers for variola virus DNA from the 2 repositories under specific rules
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**The management of Fairground Fever (Faroola) and the monitoring of highly pathogenic avian influenza outbreaks. Implementation of the annual meeting of the Joint Expert Committee on Fairground Fever (Faroola) of the World Health Organization (ACVVR). Coordination of the review of surveillance protocols for work on Fairground Fever (Faroola) through ACVVR, coordination of collaborations with two Fairground Fever (Faroola) joint groups (which are also WHO Collaborative Centres), implementation of work and addressing questions from the investigators on Fairground Fever (Faroola) DNA from the two groups under specific guidelines.**

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   **Fairground Fever (Faroola):** I've kept "Faroola" in parentheses after "Fairground Fever" as it seems to be the Yoruba name for the disease.  Without more context, it's difficult to know if "Fairground Fever" is a descriptive term or a translation of "Faroola."
*   **ACVVR:**  I've kept this acronym as it's likely a specific organization or committee name.
*   **Highly Pathogenic Avian Influenza:** This is a standard term used to describe a severe form of bird flu.
*   **WHO Collaborative Centres:** This refers to institutions that work with the World Health Organization on specific research or public health initiatives.

translations.source: ṣíṣe àmójútó ààbò-ẹ̀dá àti ìdáàbòbò-ẹ̀dá èèkàn ní ọdún méjì àwọn àkójọpọ̀ fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà tí wọ́n káríayé 2. ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìtọ́nisọ́nà àwùjọ lórí ìwádìí lórí DNA fáírọ́ọ̀sì fàríólà àti àwọn ọ̀ràn tí wọ́n jẹmọ́ ara wọn. Ìgbáradì àti ìfèsì pàjáwìrì àkóso àti ìgbáradì ìtọ́nisọ́nà àwùjọ lórí ìdàgbàsókè àti ìlò àwọn òté agbógunti fún àwọn fáírọ́ọ̀sì aṣerankoṣènìyàn. ìgbáradì ìdàgbàsókè àti ọgbọ́n ìfèsì, àti irinṣẹ́ láti ṣàmúlò àwọn òté ìgbáradì
translations.reference: conducting biennial biosafety and biosecurity inspections of the 2 global variola virus repositories developing normative guidance regarding research on variola virus DNA and related matters Emergency preparedness and response coordinating and preparing normative guidance on development and use of medical countermeasures for orthopoxviruses developing preparedness and response strategies, and tools to implement preparedness measures
translations.hypothesis:

"Implementation of genetic engineering and reverse genetic techniques in two generations of *Faidherbia faríólà* trees. Improvement and optimization of control mechanisms and improvement of social network analysis on the basis of propagation and use of improved varieties for *Faidherbia albida*. Improvement of propagation and knowledge optimization, and instructions for processing propagation materials."

translations.source: àkóso àti àmójútó àkójọ abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára sànpọ̀nná lágbàáyé. àkóso ìdàgbàsókè ìṣàṣepọ̀ láàbù fún ìdámọ̀ àti àyẹ̀wò àwọn fáírọ́ọ̀sì aṣerankoṣènìyàn. ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìtọ́nisọ́nà àwùjọ àti àwọn ọgbọ́n ìfèsì fún olóde-ẹdun ènìyàn àti àwọn fáírọ́ọ̀sì aṣerankoṣènìyàn mìíràn. àtìlẹ́yìn agbára ìṣẹ̀dá àti ìṣẹ̀dá àwọn èròjà ìdanilẹ́kọ̀ọ́. ìnípa lórí àwọn ètò ààbò ìlera àgbáyé
translations.reference: maintaining and managing the smallpox global vaccine stockpile coordinating development of a laboratory network for orthopoxvirus detection and diagnostics developing normative guidance and response strategies for human monkeypox and other orthopoxviruses supporting capacity-building and developing training materials contributing to global health security initiatives
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba text into English:

**Management and coordination of global vaccine supply chains. Managing the distribution and monitoring of vaccine doses for human health and humanitarian assistance. Establishing systems for equitable access to vaccines and resources for human education and other humanitarian assistance. Strengthening production capacity and the production of innovative products. Regarding global health programs.**

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   **Context is Key:**  Low-resource language translation often requires making educated guesses about the context. This translation assumes the document is related to global health, vaccine distribution, and humanitarian aid.
*   **Nuance:** Yoruba, like many languages, has nuances that are difficult to capture perfectly in English. I've aimed for accuracy and clarity while trying to preserve the original meaning.
*   **"Àkóso"**: This word has a broad meaning encompassing management, coordination, and control.
*   **"Àmójútó"**: This refers to coordination and collaboration.
*   **"Àkójọ abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára"**: This translates to "supply chain of vaccines."
*   **"Ìdàgbàsókè ìṣàṣepọ̀"**: This refers to the distribution of doses.
*   **"Aṣerankoṣènìyàn"**: This means humanitarian assistance.
*   **"Ìtọ́nisọ́nà àwùjọ"**: This refers to equitable access.
*   **"Olóde-ẹdun ènìyàn"**: This refers to human education.
*   **"Ìṣẹ̀dá àwọn èròjà ìdanilẹ́kọ̀ọ́"**: This refers to the production of innovative products.
*   **"Àwọn ètò ààbò ìlera àgbáyé"**: This refers to global health programs.



I hope this translation is helpful! Let me know if you have any other documents you'd like me to translate.

translations.source: Ìpamọ́ ìtàn sànpọ̀nná lágbàáyé àmójútó àwọn àká Ètò Ìfòpinsí Sànpọ̀nná ní àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú Àká Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé àti ilé ìyáwèékàwé ìdarí lórí àwọn ìjíròrò ìlànà àti ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ mánigbàgbé tí ó ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ìfòpinsí sànpọ̀nná ìjábọ̀ lọ́dọọdún fún Ìgbìmọ̀ Adarí Àjọ Elétò ìlera Àgbáyé àti Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé Ibùdó iṣẹ́ náà ṣe àkójọpọ̀ àti àkóso owó ìrànwọ́ fún àwọn iṣẹ́ wọ̀nyí àti fún àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ nígbà tí ó yẹ nígbà tí ó nílò gẹ́gẹ́ bí igbaninímọ̀ràn àti ìdanilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ ìmọ̀.
translations.reference: Preserving global smallpox history overseeing the Smallpox Eradication Programme archives in collaboration with WHO Archives and the WHO Library leading on policy discussions and commemorative events regarding smallpox eradication reporting annually to the WHO Executive Board and the World Health Assembly The Secretariat also mobilizes and manages funding for these activities and for ad hoc events as required such as technical consultations and training.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"The report details the global situation regarding polio eradication efforts in collaboration with the World Health Organization's Polio Eradication Initiative and the Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria, focusing on challenges and setbacks related to polio eradication programs. The report is submitted to the World Health Organization's Advisory Committee on Polio Eradication and the Global Health Advisory Board. The work of the unit involves coordinating and managing funds for these activities and for emergencies when necessary, such as surveillance and research on knowledge development."

**Explanation of some choices and nuances:**

*   **Ìpamọ́ ìtàn:** Report
*   **sànpọ̀nná:** Polio

*   **ilé ìyáwèékàwé ìdarí:** Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria
*   **ìjíròrò ìlànà àti ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ mánigbàgbé:** Challenges and setbacks
*   **ìfòpinsí sànpọ̀nná ìjábọ̀ lọ́dọọdún:** Polio eradication program report
*   **Ìgbìmọ̀ Adarí Àjọ Elétò ìlera Àgbáyé:** World Health Organization's Advisory Committee on Polio Eradication
*   **Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé:** Global Health Advisory Board
*   **Ibùdó iṣẹ́ náà:** The work of the unit

translations.source: Àìsàn láti ara oró ejò Ìsọnísókí: Àìsàn láti ara oró ejò jẹ́ àìsàn tí ó léwu fún ẹ̀mí tí àwọn oró ẹnu ejò tí ó lóró ń fà. Àìsàn láti ara oró ejò tún lè wáyé nípasẹ̀ oró ejò tí ó fọ́n sí ojú láti ọwọ́ àwọn ẹ̀yà ejò kan tí wọ́n ní agbára láti tu oró gẹ́gẹ́ bí òté ààbò. Àwọn akitiyan àtijọ́ láti ṣàkóso àìsàn láti ara oró ejò ti pèsè àwọn ìwífún-àlàyé ìmọ̀ àrùn tí ó fọ́n ká, tí kò pé. Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn afaragbá kì í lọ sí ibùdó ìlera tàbí ilé ìwòsàn àti dípò bẹ́ẹ̀ wọ́n máa ń gbáralé àwọn ìtọ́jú abẹ́lé.
translations.reference: Snakebite envenoming Overview: Snakebite envenoming is a potentially life-threatening disease caused by toxins in the bite of a venomous snake. Envenoming can also be caused by having venom sprayed into the eyes by certain species of snakes that have the ability to spit venom as a defence measure. Inadequate past efforts to control snakebite envenoming has produced fragmented, inaccurate epidemiological data. Many victims do not attend health centres or hospitals and instead rely on traditional treatments.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Disease from the Mouth of the Mosquito (Malaria):** Disease from the mouth of the mosquito is a disease that threatens the life of those affected by the bites of infected mosquito mouthparts. Disease from the mouth of the mosquito can also arise through mosquito bites that affect the eyes from the hands of certain mosquito species that have the power to transmit very dangerous diseases like typhoid. Traditional measures to control disease from the mouth of the mosquito have hindered scientific medical knowledge, without success. Many people do not go to health centers or hospitals and instead rely on traditional healers.

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   **Specificity:** The Yoruba text uses a very specific and somewhat poetic phrasing ("disease from the mouth of the mosquito") to refer to malaria. I've tried to retain the essence of this phrasing while making it understandable in English.
*   **Cultural Context:** The reference to traditional healers ("àwọn afaragbá") highlights a cultural practice.
*   **Accuracy:** I've aimed for accuracy while ensuring the English is clear and readable.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé, àwọn ìwífún-àlàyé tí ó wà fi hàn pé àwọn ènìyàn 4.5-5.4 ni ejò bù jẹ lọ́dọọdún. Nínú èyí, mílíọ̀nù 1.8-2.7 ní àìsàn bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni 81 000 sí 138 000 kú láti ara àwọn àìsàn. Lára àwọn ọ̀wọ́ tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu jùlọ ni àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ iṣẹ́ àgbẹ̀, darandaran, ọdẹ, àwọn ọmọdé tí wọ́n ń ṣiṣẹ́, àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbé ní ilé tí wọ́n kò kọ́ dáradára àti àwọn tí wọn kò ní àǹfààní sí ẹ̀kọ́ àti ìtọ́jú ìlera. Òjòjò àti ikú máa ń sáábà wáyé láàárín àwọn ọ̀dọ́ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ọmọdé ní ìṣòro ọ̀ràn ikú púpọ̀. Síwájú sí i, àwọn obìnrin máa ń ní ìrírí àwọn ìdènà sí ìṣàmúlò ìtọ́jú ìṣègùn ní àwọn àwùjọ kan bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn aláboyún wà nínú ewu.
translations.reference: However, available data show 4.5–5.4 million people get bitten by snakes annually. Of this, 1.8–2.7 million develop clinical illness and 81 000 to 138 000 die from complications. High-risk groups include rural agricultural workers, herders, fishermen, hunters, working children, people living in poorly constructed houses and those with limited access to education and healthcare. Morbidity and mortality occur most frequently among young people and children suffer higher case fatality. Furthermore, women experience increased barriers to accessing medical care in some cultures and pregnant women are extremely vulnerable.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"It has been revealed by scientific findings that 4.5-5.4 million people suffer from malaria annually. Within this number, 1.8-2.7 million experience severe malaria, resulting in 81,000 to 138,000 deaths due to the illness. Among those at the highest risk are agricultural workers, pregnant women, children who work, people living in poverty who lack education, and those without access to education and healthcare. Malaria and death frequently occur among children, and children are particularly vulnerable during periods of high mortality. Furthermore, women often experience complications related to pregnancy monitoring in certain communities, and orphans are also at risk."

translations.source: Ìṣòro kan tí ó ń lọ lọ́wọ́ tí ó ń dènà àǹfààní sí ìtọ́jú òògùn agbógunti-oró ejò tí ó láàbò, tí ó sì múnádóko ní àwọn ọ̀pọ̀ agbègbè, àti pàápàá jùlọ ní àyíká àgbègbè Sahara Afíríkà, jẹ́ ìdí kan tí ó nípa lórí ewu wíwá ìrànlọ́wọ́ nípasẹ̀ òògùn ìbílẹ̀. Àmì àìsàn: Ẹnu tàbí oró fífọ́n láti ara àwọn ejò olóró lè fa onírúurú àwọn pàjáwìrì ìṣègùn líle tí ó lágbára. Àìsàn láti ara oró ejò láti ara àwọn oríṣìí ejò lè fa onírúurú àmì àìsàn, àwọn kan lágbára jù àwọn mìíràn lọ. Èyí mú kí ìpèsè òògùn agbógunti-oró ejò tí ó tọ́ jẹ́ ìṣòro tí ó ń lọ lọ́wọ́. Àìsàn láti ara oró ejò lè fa rọpárọsẹ̀ líle tí ó lè dènà mímí, èyí tí yóò mú kí ìtọ́jú ìṣègùn ayákánkán ṣe pàtàkì.
translations.reference: An ongoing crisis restricting access to safe, effective antivenom treatment in many regions, and particularly sub-Saharan Africa, is one factor that contributes to the predisposition for seeking help through traditional medicine. Symptoms: Bites or sprayed venom from venomous snakes can cause a range of acute and serious medical emergencies. Envenoming from different types of snakes can cause different symptoms, some more serious than others. This makes the preparation of correct antivenoms an ongoing problem. Envenoming can cause severe paralysis that may prevent breathing, making immediate medical attention critical.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"A disease that spreads rapidly, causing fever and inflammation in the military region of northern Nigeria, which has already appeared in several areas and particularly in the Sahara region of Africa, is a major cause of concern regarding the threat of humanitarian crisis through local warfare. Symptoms: The mouth or nose may bleed from the bodies of infected rodents, leading to various forms of severe hemorrhagic diseases. Diseases originating from rodent feces can cause a variety of symptoms, some more severe than others. This makes the disease of northern rodent-borne warfare a rapidly spreading disease. Diseases originating from rodent feces can cause bleeding in the mouth that can affect people, which will make emergency rescue efforts very critical."

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   **Specificity:** The Yoruba text uses specific terminology related to regions and military areas in Nigeria. I've tried to maintain that specificity in the translation.
*   **"Òògùn agbógunti-oró ejò":** This phrase is quite complex and appears to refer to a disease transmitted by rodents, potentially linked to conflict or warfare. I've translated it as "northern rodent-borne warfare" to capture the essence, but it's a nuanced term.
*   **"Ìtọ́jú òògùn":** This refers to a military region.
*   **"Àwọn pàjáwìrì ìṣègùn líle":** This refers to hemorrhagic diseases.
*   **"Rọpárọsẹ̀ líle":** This refers to bleeding.



I hope this translation is helpful! Let me know if you have any other documents you'd like me to translate.

translations.source: Àwọn ènìyàn lè ní ìrírí ìṣòro ẹ̀jẹ̀ sísun tí ó lè yọrí sí ẹ̀jẹ̀ àsunsínú tàbí akùdé kíndìnrín tí kò ṣe é wò. Ìbàjẹ́ ìṣù ara líle tún lè wáyé, èyí tí ó lè yọrí sí ààbọ̀ ara títí ayé àti ọwọ́ gígé pẹ̀lú. Àwọn ọmọdé nínú ipa ewu ńlá nítorí ara wọn tí ó kéré jù tí àwọn àgbàlagbà lọ. Ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn ikú àti àbájáde láti ara ẹnu ejò ṣe é dènà dáadáa nípa pípèsè àwọn òògùn aporó ejò tí wọ́n wà káàkiri tí wọ́n sì ṣe é ṣàmúlò, pàápàá jùlọ ní àwọn àgbègbè tí ewu pọ̀ sí. Àwọn òògùn aporó ejò alágbára nìkan ni ìtọ́jú pípéye láti dènà tàbí ṣe ìwòsàn ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ipa àwọn oró ejò.
translations.reference: People may also experience bleeding disorders that can lead to fatal haemorrhages or irreversible kidney failure. Severe local tissue destruction may also occur, which can lead to permanent disability and even limb amputation. Children are at higher risk of severe effects due to lower body mass than adults. Most deaths and serious consequences from snake bites are entirely preventable by making safe and effective antivenoms more widely available and accessible, particularly in high-risk areas. High quality snake antivenoms are the only effective treatment to prevent or reverse most of the venomous effects of snake bites.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"People can experience persistent skin itching which can lead to secondary infections or skin lesions without them realizing it. Skin rashes can also develop, which can progress to chronic skin conditions and affect the entire body, including the limbs. Children are in a high-risk group because of their smaller size compared to adults. Many deaths and complications from mouth sores are exacerbated by the scarcity of effective anti-malarial drugs which are available but not utilized, especially in areas where the risk is high. Only potent anti-malarial drugs are considered effective in preventing or treating many of the complications of malaria."

**Notes on the translation:**

*   I aimed for clarity and accuracy while maintaining a natural English flow.
*   I've tried to capture the nuances of the Yoruba language, particularly regarding the severity of the issues being discussed.
*   "Ejò" refers to malaria, and I've used "anti-malarial" throughout to reflect this.
*   "Àgbàlagbà" translates to adults.
*   "Òògùn aporó ejò" translates to anti-malarial drugs.

translations.source: Ìtọ́jú: Àwọn òògùn aporó ejò jẹ́ ìtọ́jú pípé láti dènà tàbí ṣe ìwòsàn ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ipa burúkú oró ejò bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n wà nínú àlàkalẹ̀ àwọn òògùn pàtàkì WHO. Wíwà àti níní àǹfààní àwọn òògùn aporó wọ̀nyí, pẹ̀lú ṣíṣe ìpolongo lórí àwọn ìlànà ìdènà abẹ́lé láàrin àwọn àwùjọ àti àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera, ni àwọn ọ̀nà tí wọ́n dára jùlọ láti ṣe àdínkù àwọn ìṣòro ńlá àti ikú láti ara oró ejò. Lẹ́yìn tí ejò tí ó ṣe é ṣe kí ó lóró bá sán ènìyàn, ẹ tẹ́lẹ̀ àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ wọ̀nyí: Ní kété ẹ kúrò ní agbègbè tí sísán náà ti waye. Ẹ yọ ohunkóhun tí ó bá fún kúrò ní ẹ̀yà ara tí ejò tí náà sán láti yàgò fún ewu bí wíwú bá wáyé.
translations.reference: Treatment: Snake antivenoms are effective treatments to prevent or reverse most of the harmful effects of snakebite envenoming and are included in the WHO list of essential medicines. The availability and accessibility of these antivenoms, along with raising awareness on primary prevention methods among communities and health workers, are the best ways to limit serious consequences and deaths from snakebite envenoming. After a bite by a snake suspected of being venomous, follow these steps: Immediately move away from the area where the bite occurred. Remove anything tight from around the bitten part of the body to avoid harm if swelling occurs.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba text into English:

**Topic: Antivenom Drugs are Essential for Treating Many Serious Snakebite Effects and are Included in the WHO's List of Essential Medicines. Awareness and proper handling of these antivenom drugs, along with the implementation of snakebite treatment protocols among communities and healthcare workers, are the best ways to reduce serious complications and deaths from snakebites. After a snake has bitten someone, follow these steps: First, move away from the area where the bite occurred. Remove anything that may have been pressed against the skin where the snake bit to prevent further harm in case of swelling.**

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   I've aimed for clarity and accuracy while maintaining a natural English flow.
*   "Òògùn aporó" translates to "antivenom drugs."
*   "Ìtọ́jú" translates to "essential" or "topic."
*   "Ẹ" is a Yoruba pronoun and is often omitted in English translation as it's implied by context.
*   I've tried to capture the tone of a public health advisory.

translations.source: Ẹ fi ọkàn afaragbá balẹ̀, nítorí pé kì í ṣe gbogbo oró ejò ni ó ń ikú kíákíá. Ẹ jẹ́ kí ẹni náà wà láìmira pátápátá kí ẹ sì gbé ẹni náà lọ sí ibùdó ìlera ní kíákíá. Fífi ọ̀ǹtẹ̀ sí ojú sísán náà pẹ̀lú èèfún lè jẹ́ ohun tí ó dára ní àwọn ọ̀ràn kan. Ẹ yàgò fún àwọn ìlànà ìtọ́jú ìbílẹ̀ àkọ́gbà tàbí àwọn òògùn ìbílẹ̀. Ẹ lè fún ẹni náà ní Parasitamọ́ fún ìrora abẹ́lé (èyí tí ó lè lágbára).
translations.reference: Reassure the victim, as most venomous snake bites do not cause immediate death. Immobilize the person completely and transport the person to a health facility as soon as possible Applying pressure at the bite site with a pressure pad may be suitable in some cases. Avoid traditional first aid methods or herbal medicines. Paracetamol may be given for local pain (which can be severe).
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Lower the fever with a cold compress, because it is not every illness that causes death repeatedly. It is best for the person to be completely at rest and to take them to the hospital frequently. Applying a cold compress to the forehead with a cloth can be beneficial in some cases. Give traditional healing remedies or herbal medicines. You can give the person Paracetamol for malaria (which can be severe)."

**Explanation of some choices:**

*   **"Lower the fever with a cold compress"**: This accurately conveys the meaning of "Ẹ fi ọkàn afaragbá balẹ̀."
*   **"It is not every illness that causes death repeatedly"**: This captures the nuance of "kì í ṣe gbogbo oró ejò ni ó ń ikú kíákíá."
*   **"Traditional healing remedies or herbal medicines"**: This is a good way to translate "àwọn ìlànà ìtọ́jú ìbílẹ̀ àkọ́gbà tàbí àwọn òògùn ìbílẹ̀."
*   **"Paracetamol for malaria (which can be severe)"**: This clarifies the purpose of the medication and adds context.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Èébì lè wáyé, nítorí náà ẹ mú kí ẹni náà sún pẹ̀lú ẹ̀gbẹ́ rẹ̀ òsì ní ipò ìmúláradá. Ẹ mójútó káà ìmí àti ìmí rẹ̀ kí ẹ sì gbáradì láti jí i bí ó bá ṣe pàtàkì.
translations.reference: Vomiting may occur, so place the person on their left side in the recovery position. Closely monitor airway and breathing and be ready to resuscitate if necessary
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba text into English:

"Trouble may arise, therefore, ensure that the person is accompanied by their family in a supportive role. Carefully consider the matter and its implications so you can advise accordingly as needed."

**Explanation of some of the choices made in the translation:**

*   **"Èébì lè wáyé"**:  This translates to "Trouble may arise" or "Problems might occur."
*   **"nítorí náà ẹ mú kí"**: This means "therefore, ensure that" or "for that reason, make sure that."
*   **"ẹni náà sún pẹ̀lú ẹ̀gbẹ́ rẹ̀"**: This translates to "the person is accompanied by their family."
*   **"òsì ní ipò ìmúláradá"**: This means "in a supportive role" or "as a source of support."
*   **"Ẹ mójútó káà ìmí àti ìmí rẹ̀"**: This translates to "Carefully consider the matter and its implications."
*   **"kí ẹ sì gbáradì láti jí i bí ó bá ṣe pàtàkì"**: This means "so you can advise accordingly as needed."



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Àwọn aràn tí ilẹ̀ ń ṣe ìkóràn Ìsọnísókí: Àwọn àìsàn aràn tí ilẹ̀ ń ṣe àkóràn wà lára àwọn àìsàn tí wọ́n wọ́pọ̀ jùlọ káàkiri àgbáyé tí wọ́n sì ń nípa lórí àwọn àwùjọ tí wọ́n tòṣì jùlọ tí wọ́n kò sì ní àǹfààní jùlọ. Wọ́n máa ń ràn nípa àwọn ẹyin tí ó wà nínú ìgbẹ́ ènìyàn èyí tí ó ń sọ ilẹ̀ di àìmọ́ ní àwọn àgbègbè tí kò ti sí ìmọ́tótó. Àwọn ẹ̀yà gbòógì tí wọ́n máa ń mú ènìyàn ni aràn roboto (Ascaris lumbricoides), aràn kòbókò (Trichuris trichuria) àti kán-ànyàn (Necator americanus àti Ancylostoma duodenale). Aràn inú máa ń pèsè àwọn onírúurú àmì àìsàn tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn ìṣòro inú bí (ìgbẹ́ gbuuru, inú rírun), ìrora àti àìlera gbogbogbò.
translations.reference: Soil-transmitted helminthiases Overview: Soil-transmitted helminth (STH) infections are among the most common infections worldwide and affect the poorest and most deprived communities. They are transmitted by eggs present in human faeces which in turn contaminate soil in areas where sanitation is poor. The main species that infect people are the roundworm (Ascaris lumbricoides), the whipworm (Trichuris trichiura) and the hookworms (Necator americanus and Ancylostoma duodenale). Intestinal worms produce a wide range of symptoms including intestinal manifestations (diarrhoea, abdominal pain), general malaise and weakness.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Diseases the Country Causes to be Worried About: Parasitic diseases are among the most prevalent diseases worldwide, and they affect the most vulnerable populations who do not have adequate access to healthcare. They are caused by worms that exist within the human gut, which make the country vulnerable in areas lacking proper knowledge. The major strains that affect people are roundworm (Ascaris lumbricoides), whipworm (Trichuris trichuria), and hookworm (Necator americanus and Ancylostoma duodenale). Intestinal worms cause various symptoms of illness that lead to complications such as diarrhea, anemia, and general weakness.**

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   I've aimed for clarity and accuracy while maintaining a natural English flow.
*   I've included the scientific names of the worms in parentheses for added precision.
*   The phrasing in the original Yoruba is sometimes a bit indirect, so I've tried to express the meaning as directly as possible in English.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Kán-ànyàn ń fa àdánù ẹ̀jẹ̀ inú líle èyí tí ó lè fa àìlẹ́jẹ̀tó. Àwọn aràn tí ilẹ̀ ń ṣe àkóràn máa ń ràn nípa àwọn ẹyin tí wọ́n máa ń jáde láti ara ìgbẹ́ àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n jẹ́ afaragbá. Àwọn àgbà aràn máa ń gbé nínú ìfun níbi tí wọ́n ti máa ń pèsè àwọn ẹgbẹẹgbẹ̀rún ẹyin lójoojúmọ́. Ní àwọn agbègbè tí wọn kò ní ìmọ́tótó tó tó, àwọn ẹyin wọ̀nyí maat ń sọ ilẹ̀ di àìmọ́. Èyí lè ṣẹlẹ̀ ní àwọn onírúurú ọ̀nà: àwọn ẹyin tí wọ́n lẹ̀ mọ́ ẹ̀fọ́ tí ènìyàn jẹ nígbà tí wọn kò bá ṣe, fọ̀ tàbí bó ẹ̀fọ́ náà dáadáa.;
translations.reference: Hookworms cause chronic intestinal blood loss that result in anaemia. Soil-transmitted helminths are transmitted by eggs that are passed in the faeces of infected people. Adult worms live in the intestine where they produce thousands of eggs each day. In areas that lack adequate sanitation, these eggs contaminate the soil. This can happen in several ways: eggs that are attached to vegetables are ingested when the vegetables are not carefully cooked, washed or peeled;
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"The problem of waste is causing pollution within this area, which can lead to health issues. The stories the land tells are about the refuse that emerges from the bodies of people who are wasteful. Elders of the community live in designated areas where they used to sort through large quantities of waste daily. In areas lacking sufficient waste management, this waste turns the land into a place of ignorance. This can happen in various ways: waste that is known to be food that people eat when they are hungry, rots or if the food is of poor quality."

**Notes on the translation:**

*   **Cultural Context:** The text references "elders of the community" sorting waste. This highlights a traditional practice that might not be common in all cultures. I've tried to convey this nuance.
*   **"Àìmọ́" (Ignorance):** This word carries a deeper meaning than just a lack of knowledge. It implies a state of being disconnected from the land and its well-being due to the overwhelming presence of waste.
*   **Figurative Language:** The phrase "the land tells stories" is a poetic expression in Yoruba. I've translated it to convey the idea that the waste reveals information about the community's habits.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: wọ́n máa ń jẹ ẹyin láti ara àwọn orísun omi tí kò mọ́; àwọn ọmọdé tí wọ́n ń ṣeré ní orí ilẹ̀ tí kò mọ́ máa ń kó ẹyin be sì ni wọ́n yóò fi àwọn ọwọ́ wọn sí ẹnu wọn láìfọ wọ́n. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Àìsàn máa ń ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú iye àwọn aràn tí àgọ̀ gbà. Àwọn ènìyàn tí àìsàn wọn jẹ́ àwọn aràn tí kò pọ̀ (aràn níwọ̀n) kì í sáábà jìyà láti ara àìsàn yìí. Àwọn àìsàn líle lè fa onírúurú àwọn àmì àìsàn tó fi mọ́ àwọn ìfarahàn nínú ìfun (ìgbẹ́ gbuuru àti inú rírun), àìjẹunrekánú, ìrora àti àìlera gbogbogbò, àti àìdàgbàbóṣeyẹ̀ àti àìgbèrú.
translations.reference: eggs are ingested from contaminated water sources; eggs are ingested by children who play in the contaminated soil and then put their hands in their mouths without washing them. Symptoms: Morbidity is related to the number of worms harboured. People with infections of light intensity (few worms) usually do not suffer from the infection. Heavier infections can cause a range of symptoms including intestinal manifestations (diarrhoea and abdominal pain), malnutrition, general malaise and weakness, and impaired growth and physical development.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"They would eat clay from unknown water sources; children playing on the ground who do not know would eat clay and then put their hands in their mouths unknowingly. Symptoms of illness: Illnesses often come with the amount of heat the body absorbs. People whose illnesses are boils that don't heal (boils in the body) rarely recover from this illness. Internal illnesses can cause various symptoms that manifest in the body (black stool and bloody urine), loss of appetite, weakness and general sickness, and confusion and paralysis."

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   **Cultural Context:** The document seems to be describing traditional beliefs or observations about illness and its symptoms, likely within a Yoruba cultural context.
*   **"Ẹyin":** This word refers to clay, which was historically consumed for various reasons, sometimes due to mineral deficiencies or cultural practices.
*   **"Aràn":** This refers to boils or sores.
*   **"Ìfun":** This refers to the body.
*   **Specificity:** I've tried to be as accurate as possible while maintaining readability in English.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Àwọn àìsàn tí wọ́n le lè fa ìdíwọ́ inú tí ènìyàn gbọ́dọ̀ tọ́jú pẹ̀lú iṣẹ́ abẹ. S. stercolaris lè fa àìsàn awọ ara àti òpónà ọ̀fun bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àìjẹunkánú láàrin àwọn ọmọdé. Ní ti àdínkù àjẹsára ẹni tí ó gbàlejò wọn, ajọ̀fẹ́ náà lè fa òòrèkóòrè àìsàn/àpẹẹrẹ àpínká tí ó lágbára bí wọn kò bá tètè tọ́jú wọn bí ó ṣe tọ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó máa léwu bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé ìtọ́jú wà. Ní 2001, àwọn aṣojú ní Àpérò Àgbáyé fẹnukò papọ̀ lórí ọ̀nà àbáyọ (WHA54. 19) tí ó ń rọ àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n ní àjàkálẹ̀ àrùn láti bẹ̀rẹ̀ sí ní kojú aràn, pàápàá jùlọ sikosomíásì àti àwọn aràn tí ilẹ̀ ń ṣe àkóràn. Ọgbọ́n láti ṣàkóso àwọn àìsàn aràn tí ilẹ̀ ń ṣe àkóràn ni láti ṣàkóso ewu àìsàn nípasẹ̀ ìtọ́jú òòrèkóòrè àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu ní àwọn agbègbè àjàkálẹ̀ àrùn.
translations.reference: Infections of very high intensity can cause intestinal obstruction that should be treated surgically. S. stercoralis may cause dermatological and gastro-intestinal morbidity and is also known to be associated with chronic malnutrition in children. In case of reduced host immunity, the parasite can cause the hyperinfection/dissemination syndrome that is invariably fatal if not promptly and properly cured and is often fatal despite the treatment. In 2001, delegates at the World Health Assembly unanimously endorsed a resolution (WHA54.19) urging endemic countries to start seriously tackling worms, specifically schistosomiasis and soil-transmitted helminths. The strategy for control of soil-transmitted helminth infections is to control morbidity through the periodic treatment of at-risk people living in endemic areas.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Diseases that can cause internal bleeding that people should address with care. *S. stercolaris* can cause skin infections and intestinal tract diseases; it is also associated with irritability among children. Furthermore, if a person's immunity is weakened, the infection can cause severe disease/complications if they are not treated properly. In 2001, the World Health Assembly agreed on a resolution (WHA54.19) urging countries with outbreaks of disease to begin with community control, especially parasitic diseases and diseases the country is experiencing an epidemic of. The key to controlling country-occurring diseases is to control the disease risk through the treatment of people who are at risk in outbreak areas."



**Notes on the Translation:**

*   I've aimed for clarity and accuracy while maintaining a natural English flow.
*   I've kept the scientific name *S. stercolaris* as is, assuming it's a specific reference.
*   I've tried to capture the nuances of the Yoruba phrasing as best as possible.

translations.source: Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu ni: àwọn ọmọ jéléósinmi àwọn ọmọ ọjọ́ orí wọn lọ ilé ẹ̀kọ́ àwọn obìnrin tí ọjọ́ orí wọn ti tó bímọ (tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn aláboyún ní ìpele kejì Tai ìkẹta oyún àti obìnrin tí wọ́n ń fún ọmọ lọ́yàn) Àwọn àgbàlagbà tí wọn ń ṣe àwọn iṣẹ́ tí wọ́n léwu bí àwọn olùkórè tíì tàbí awakùsà.
translations.reference: People at risk are: preschool children school-age children women of reproductive age (including pregnant women in the second and third trimesters and breastfeeding women) adults in certain high-risk occupations such as tea-pickers or miners.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Yoruba document:

"The people who are at risk include: young people, children going to school, girls whose age has reached marriageable age (who are also mentors to young people at the second and third levels of Tai Ikẹta), and the elderly who are engaged in risky jobs such as teachers or conductors."



**Explanation of some of the choices made in the translation:**

*   **"Tai Ikẹta"**: This is a specific cultural reference. I've left it as "Tai Ikẹta" and added a note to clarify it refers to levels within a system.  Without more context, it's difficult to translate precisely.
*   **"ọmọ lọ́yàn"**: This refers to young people, particularly those needing guidance. I translated it as "young people" in the context of mentorship.
*   **"àwọn olùkórè tíì tàbí awakùsà"**: This refers to teachers or conductors. I translated it as "teachers or conductors" to maintain the original meaning.

translations.source: Ìtọ́jú Àjọ WHO dábàá àwọn òògùn - albendazole (400mg) àti mebendazole (500mg) - kájúẹ̀, kò wọ́n bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n rọrùn láti lo láti ọwọ́ àwọn ènìyàn tí wọn kì í ṣe òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera (b.a àwọn olùkọ́). Wọ́n ti là ìdánwò ààbò gbòòrò kọjá bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ẹgbẹ̀lẹ́gbẹ̀ àwọn ènìyàn ti lò wọ́n pẹ̀lú ipa ẹ̀yìn níwọ̀n àti èyí tí kò tó nǹkan. Àti albendazole àti mebendazole ni wọ́n máa ń fún àwọn àjọ ìlera ìjọba orílẹ̀ èdè nípasẹ̀ Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ní gbogbo àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n ní àrùn fún ìtọ́jú gbogbo àwọn ọmọdé tí wọ́n wà ní ọjọ́ orí láti lọ sí ilé ẹ̀kọ́. Ifamẹkitíìnì fún àkóso S. stercolaris ni ìrètí wà pé yóò wà ní iye tí kò jara lọ láti ọdún 2021.
translations.reference: TreatmentThe WHO recommended medicines –albendazole (400 mg) and mebendazole (500 mg) – are effective, inexpensive and easy to administer by non-medical personnel (e.g. teachers). They have been through extensive safety testing and have been used in millions of people with few and minor side-effects. Both albendazole and mebendazole are donated to national ministries of health through WHO in all endemic countries for the treatment of all children of school age. Ivermectin for the control of S. stercoralis is expected to be available at affordable price from 2021.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

The WHO recommends medications – albendazole (400mg) and mebendazole (500mg) – stating that they are easy to use by individuals who are not healthcare workers (e.g., teachers). They have undergone extensive testing and the side effects experienced by people who have used them are minimal. Both albendazole and mebendazole will be provided to government health agencies through the World Health Organization for all countries that need them to treat all children from the day they are born to when they go to school. The cost of controlling *Schistosoma stercolaris* is expected to be relatively low from 2021 onwards.

translations.source: Àbíkú Ìsọnísókí: Ọmọ tí ó kú lẹ́yìn ọ̀sẹ̀ méjìdínlọ́gbọ̀n oyún, ṣùgbọ́n síwájú tàbí lẹ́yìn ìbí, ni a mọ̀ sí àbíkú. Ó tó mílíọ̀nù 2 àwọn àbíkú ni ó wà lọ́dọọdún - ọ̀kọ̀ọ̀kan ní ìṣẹ́jú àáyá 16 kan. Ó lé ní ìdá 40% gbogbo àwọn àbíkú ṣẹlẹ̀ ní àsìkò ìbímọ - àdánù tí ó ṣe é dènà pẹ̀lú ìtọ́jú dídára tí ó sì pọ́nnilé lásìkò ìbímọ tí ó fi mọ́ ètò àmójútó àti àǹfààní àsìkò sí ìtọ́jú aláboyún pàjáwìrì nígbà tí ó nílò. Níní ìrírí àbíkú lásìkò ìlóyún tàbí ìbímọ jẹ́ àjálù tí wọ́n kò ṣe àmójútó tí ó tó nínú àwọn ètò, ìlànà àti ìrànwọ́ àgbáyé. Àwọn ìdíyelé wà, pàápàá jùlọ fún àwọn obìnrin àti ẹbí wọn, bí ìbànújẹ́ alábiamọ, ìṣòro owó àti àwọn àbájáde ọrọ̀ ajé, àti ìdẹ́yẹsí àti èèwọ̀ pẹ̀lú. Ní 2014, Àpérò Ìlera Àgbáyé fọwọ́sí Ètò Ọmọ tuntun Kọ̀ọ̀kan, èyí tí a ti rí àfojúsùn àgbáyé 12 tàbí àwọn àbíkú onípele kẹta (pípẹ́) díẹ̀ nínú àpapọ̀ ìbí 1, 000 ní orílẹ̀ èdè kọ̀ọ̀kan ní 2030.
translations.reference: Stillbirth Overview: A baby who dies after 28 weeks of pregnancy, but before or during birth, is classified as a stillbirth.There are nearly 2 million stillbirths every year – one every 16 seconds. Over 40% of all stillbirths occur during labour – a loss that could be avoided with improved quality and respectful care during childbirth including routine monitoring and timely access to emergency obstetric care when required. Experiencing a stillbirth during pregnancy or childbirth is a tragedy insufficiently addressed in global agendas, policies and funded programmes. There are psychological costs, especially to women and their families, such as maternal depression, financial consequences and economic repercussions, as well as stigma and taboo.In 2014, the World Health Assembly endorsed the Every Newborn Action Plan (ENAP), which includes a global target of 12 or fewer third trimester (late) stillbirths per 1,000 total births in every country by 2030.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Abiku Phenomenon: A child who dies after two weeks or months of life, or before or after birth, is known as an Abiku.** Approximately 2 million Abiku are born annually – roughly 16 per minute. 40% of all Abiku deaths occur during childbirth – a statistic exacerbated by a lack of specialized care during pregnancy and a deficiency in emergency and referral systems for critically ill newborns, particularly in resource-limited settings. In cases of Abiku deaths during infancy or childbirth, they are often not included in global programs, policies, and research. The causes are present, especially for women and their families, including postpartum hemorrhage, financial difficulties and related poverty, and stigma and associated shame. In 2014, the Global Health Initiative endorsed the Every Newborn Action Plan, which aims to reduce the third (postnatal) week mortality rate to less than 23 per 1,000 live births in each country by 2030.



**Notes on the Translation:**

*   **Abiku:** I've kept "Abiku" as is, as it's a culturally specific term with no direct English equivalent. It's a concept deeply rooted in Yoruba belief.
*   **Cultural Context:** I've tried to convey the cultural significance of the term and the associated challenges.
*   **Accuracy:** I've aimed for accuracy while maintaining readability in English.

translations.source: Ní 2021, àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè gọbọi àti alábọ́dé èrè 139 gbòógì ti fẹnukò lórí àfojúsùn yìí, ṣùgbọ́n àwọn orílẹ̀ 56 kò ní ṣe àṣeyọrí àfojúsùn Ètò Ọmọ tuntun Kọ̀ọ̀kan ní 2030 bí àwọn ìgbìyànjú síwájú sí i kò bá jẹ́ ṣíṣe. Bí àwọn àṣà ìwòyí bá tẹ̀síwájú, àwọn ìkókó mílíọ̀nù 15.9 yóò jẹ́ àbíkú; ó sún mọ́ ìdajì àwọn wọ̀nyí (mílíọ̀nù 7.7, 48%) yóò ṣẹlẹ̀ ní agbègbè ẹkùn Sahara Afíríkà. Okùnfà: Pẹ̀lú ìtọ́jú ìlera dídára káàkiri àsìkò ìlóyún àti ìbímọ, ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn àbíkú ṣe é dènà. Àwọn okùnfà àbíkú gbòógì ni àwọn ìṣòro oyún àti ìbímọ, oyún ọjọ́ pípẹ́, àìsàn ìyálọ́mọ bí ibà maléríà, sífílì àti KASA, àwọn ìṣòro ìyálọ́mọ pàápàá jùlọ ẹ̀jẹ̀ ríru, àti àtọ̀gbẹ, àti gbèdéke ìwọ̀n títóbi (nígbà tí ọmọ tí wọ́n kò tíì bí kò bá lè dàgbà bí ó ṣe tọ́ nítorí náà ó kéré jù bí ó ṣe yẹ lọ). Àwọn ìṣòro àsìkò ìbí ni wọ́n ṣokùnfà kò tó ìdá 10% àwọn àbíkú ni wọ́n jábọ̀ káàkiri àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè gọbọi bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ibùdó elérè pínnísín kò ní àkọsílẹ̀ nítorí àwọn gbèdéke inú àwọn àyẹ̀wò.
translations.reference: In 2021, 139 mainly high-income and upper middle-income countries had met this target, but 56 countries will not each the ENAP target by 2030 if further efforts are not made. If current trends continue, 15.9 million babies will be stillborn; nearly half of these (7.7 million, 48%) will occur in sub-Saharan Africa. Causes: With quality health care throughout pregnancy and childbirth, most stillbirths are preventable. The major causes of stillbirth include pregnancy and childbirth-related complications, prolonged pregnancy, maternal infections such as malaria, syphilis and HIV, maternal conditions especially hypertension, and diabetes, and fetal growth restriction (when an unborn baby is unable to achieve its growth potential and therefore smaller than it should be). Congenital abnormalities are responsible for less than 10% of stillbirths reported nationally in high-income countries and the exact proportion in low-income settings is unknown due to limitations in diagnostics.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"In 2021, 139 high-burden and fragile states focused on this challenge, but 56 countries did not achieve the child survival targets of the Every New Child 2030 initiative if the current trends continue. As mortality rates progress, 15.9 million deaths will occur; of these, 7.7 million (48%) will occur in the Saharan Africa region. Causes: With the impact of disease, particularly around childbirth and infancy, many deaths are preventable. The main causes of preventable deaths are infectious diseases and childbirth complications, prolonged labor, childhood illnesses such as malaria, sickle cell and HIV/AIDS, severe acute malnutrition, and adolescent pregnancy, and stunted growth (where children who are not born do not grow as they should because they are smaller than they should be). Causes of childbirth complications account for less than 10% of deaths across high-burden countries; furthermore, fragile contexts are not tracked due to limitations within data systems."

translations.source: Àwọn ìdí tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú ọjọ́ orí ìyá àti fífa nǹkan náà lè ṣe àgbéga ewu àìsàn ìyálọ́mọ àti àbíkú. Àwọn àbíkú lè ṣe é dènà nípasẹ̀ ètò ìfètòsọ́mọbíbí láti dènà oyún àìmọ̀dí, ìlera àti ìjẹunrekánú gidi síwájú àti lásìkò ìlóyún, dídára àti ìbọ̀wọ̀fún ìtọ́jú àsìkò ìlóyún àti ìbímọ tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn alámọ̀já òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera tí wọ́n tó dórí àwọn agbẹ̀bí. Ìtọ́jú sífílì lásìkò ìlóyún lè dènà ìṣirò àwọn àbíkú 200 000, nígbà tí àmójútó ìwọ̀n ìmí olẹ̀ àti àmójútó ìbímọ àti ìdásí àsìkò nígbà tí wọ́n nílò ṣe pàtàkì láti dènà àwọn àbíkú 832 000 lásìkò ìbí àti àdínkù àwọn ikú lẹ́yìn ìbí. Wọ́n ní àjọṣepọ̀ wà láàrin àbíkú pẹ̀lú àwọn aṣokùnfà àwùjọ àti ọrọ̀ ajé líle lórí ìlera. Ìdènà àti ìtọ́jú afèsì nílò láti jẹ́ ṣíṣe àmúlò káàkiri ìtẹ̀síwájú ìtọ́jú ìlera ìyálọ́mọ, àti jù bẹ́ẹ̀ lọ.
translations.reference: Factors related to age of the mother and practicing smoking can also increase the risk of maternal disease and stillbirth. Stillbirths can be prevented through family planning to avoid unwanted pregnancies, good health and nutrition prior to and during pregnancy, quality and respectful antenatal and childbirth care including adequate skilled health personnel including midwives. Syphilis treatment in pregnancy could also prevent an estimated 200 000 stillbirths, while fetal heart rate monitoring and labour surveillance and prompt interventions when needed are crucial for preventing 832 000 intrapartum stillbirths and reducing neonatal deaths. Stillbirths are strongly linked to adverse social and economic determinants of health. Prevention and responsive care need to be integrated across the continuum of maternal health care, and beyond.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"The factors associated with maternal mortality and stillbirth can increase the risk of child morbidity and mortality. Stillbirths can be reduced through comprehensive maternal health programs to address ignorance, illness, and genuine lack of care before and during pregnancy, accurate and timely detection of pregnancy complications and childbirth, and training healthcare workers, including community health workers. Prenatal care can reduce stillbirths by 200,000, while interventions for newborn survival and management of illness during infancy and childhood are critical to reduce stillbirths during childbirth and subsequent deaths after birth by 832,000. Collaboration exists between stillbirth prevention and social support and financial assistance programs related to health. Prevention and timely diagnosis are needed to implement comprehensive maternal health care assessment and, even more so."

**Key points about the translation:**

*   **Accuracy:** I've aimed for a translation that accurately reflects the meaning of the Yoruba text.
*   **Clarity:** I've tried to make the English translation clear and easy to understand.
*   **Context:** The text appears to be related to public health and maternal/child health interventions. I've kept that context in mind.
*   **Terminology:** I've used standard English medical and public health terminology.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Lára èyí ni ìtọ́jú alápọ̀n-ọ́nlé àti alátìlẹ́yìn lásìkò ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ ikú. Fífi etí sílẹ̀ sí ìrírí àti ohùn àwọn obìnrin àti àwùjọ wọn ṣe pàtàkì láti ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún kíkojú àwọn ọ̀ràn ìdẹ́yẹsí tí ó ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àbíkú. Èsì WHO: Ohun púpọ̀ ní láti di ṣíṣe sí i láti ṣàmúlò ìdènà àbíkú láàrin ètò àgbáyé àti orílẹ̀ èdè fún ìtọ́jú ìlera ńlá fún àwọn obìnrin, ọ̀dọ́mọdé, àti ìkókó. Nípasẹ̀ ọgbọ́n Àgbáyé fún Ìlera àwọn Obìnrin, Ìkókó àti Ọ̀dọ́mọdé 2016-2030 àti ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn àbá Ètò Iṣẹ́ Ọmọ tuntun Kọ̀ọ̀kan (2014), Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ń ṣiṣẹ́ láti fòpin sí àwọn àbíkú tí wọ́n ṣe é dènà, gẹ́gẹ́ bí ara akitiyan láti mú ìlọsíwájú bá ìlera ìyálọ́mọ, ọmọ tuntun, ọmọdé àti ọ̀dọ́mọdé. Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn àbíkú kì í gba ìwé ẹ̀rí ìbí bẹ́ẹ̀ sit ni wọn kì í forúkọ wọn sílẹ̀.
translations.reference: This includes respectful and supportive care in the event of a death. Listening to the experiences and voices of women and their communities is essential to help address issues of stigma associated with stillbirth. WHO response: More needs to be done to integrate stillbirth prevention within global and national agendas for high quality health care for women, adolescents, and babies. Through the Global Strategy for Women's, Children's and Adolescents Health 2016–2030 and implementing the recommendations of the Every Newborn Action Plan (2014), WHO is working to end preventable stillbirths, as part of efforts to improve maternal, newborn, child and adolescent health. Most stillbirths do not receive a birth certificate and are not registered.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"This concerns the challenges of maternal mortality and morbidity during childbirth. Addressing the grief and mourning of women and their communities is crucial to providing support for the psychological trauma associated with death. WHO's Challenge: A great deal needs to be done to harmonize data on mortality between global organizations and countries for large-scale health assessments of women, children, and adolescents. Through the Global Strategy for Women’s, Children’s and Adolescents’ Health 2016-2030 and the implementation of Every Newborn Action Plan (2014), the World Health Organization is working to identify and address mortality rates, particularly as a key priority to advance health for pregnant women, newborns, children, and adolescents. Many of these deaths go unrecorded, as they do not receive birth certificates."

translations.source: Mímú ìlọsíwájú bá ètò ìjábọ̀ àwọn ìbí àti ikú ẹ̀yìn ìbímọ jẹ́ ọ̀rọ̀ ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn àti àmúyẹ fún mímú àdínkù bá àwọn àbíkú. Ní àfikún, ó nílò kí wọ́n mú ìrólágbára bá àwọn ètò ìròyìn ojoojúmọ́ láti sọ iye àti láti jábọ̀ àwọn àbíkú pérépéré fún àmójútó àti ìlọsíwájú ìtọ́jú ìlera. WHO pèsè àwọn irinṣẹ́ láti ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè láti mú ìlọsíwájú ìwífun-àlàyé wọn lórí àwọn àbíkú àti àwọn ikú ìkókó. WHO tún ní ìtọ́nisọ́nà láti ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè láti ṣàyẹ̀wò àti láti wádìí ikú ẹ̀dá kọ̀ọ̀kan kí wọ́n lè dábàá kí wọ́n sì ṣàmúlò àwọn ọ̀nà àbáyọ láti dènà irúfẹ́ àwọn nǹkan bẹ́ẹ̀ ní ọjọ́ iwájú àti ìpínsọ́wọ̀ọ́ ètò fún àwọn ikú àbíkú (ICD-PM). WHO àti UNICEF ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn ìpè fún iṣẹ́ àjọṣe láti fòpin sí àwọn àbíkú tí wọ́n ṣe é dènà nípa:
translations.reference: Improving systems for reporting births and neonatal deaths is a matter of human rights and a prerequisite for reducing stillbirths. In addition, there is a need to strengthen routine health information systems to accurately capture and report stillbirths for monitoring and improving the quality of care. WHO provides tools to help countries improve their data on both stillbirths and neonatal deaths. WHO also has guidance to help countries review and investigate individual deaths so they can recommend and implement solutions to prevent similar ones in the future and a standard classification system for perinatal deaths (ICD-PM). WHO and UNICEF are supporting a call for collective action to end preventable stillbirths by:

translations.source: mímú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá ìpolongo àti àdínkù ìdẹ́yẹsí ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn àwọn obìnrin àti mọ̀lẹ́bí tí ọ̀fọ́ sẹ̀ ìrólágbára àwọn ètò ìlera láti pèsè ìtọ́jú pípéye àmúlò àwọn àfojúsùn àbíkú ní orílẹ̀ èdè àti lábẹ́lé mímú ìlọsíwájú bá ìdíwọ̀n àwọn àbíkú láti mú ìdàgbàsókè bá ẹ̀rí àti ìmọ̀.
translations.reference: increasing awareness and reducing stigma supporting bereaved women and families strengthening health systems to provide high-quality care nationalizing and localizing stillbirth targets improving measurement of stillbirths to enhance evidence and knowledge.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba text into English, aiming for accuracy and clarity:

"The advancement of women and the empowerment of girls through the implementation and support of programs is crucial to reducing maternal mortality and improving the health of women and families in the country. Furthermore, the advancement of inheritance rights for widows is essential to ensuring their economic stability and well-being through health and knowledge."

**Explanation of Choices & Nuances:**

*   **mímú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá ìpolongo:** "The advancement of women and the empowerment of girls through implementation..."  I combined "ìtẹ̀síwájú" (advancement) and "ìpolongo" (implementation) to capture the full meaning.
*   **àti àdínkù ìdẹ́yẹsí ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn:** "...and the support of programs."  "Àdínkù" means "support" or "addition." "ìdẹ́yẹsí ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn" refers to the support and implementation of programs.
*   **ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn àwọn obìnrin àti mọ̀lẹ́bí:** "...of women and girls." "Obìnrin" means women, and "mọ̀lẹ́bí" means girls.
*   **tí ọ̀fọ́ sẹ̀ ìrólágbára àwọn ètò ìlera:** "...is crucial to reducing maternal mortality..." "Ọ̀fọ́ sẹ̀" signifies importance or cruciality. "ìrólágbára àwọn ètò ìlera" means reducing the power/impact of maternal mortality.
*   **láti pèsè ìtọ́jú pípéye àmúlò àwọn àfojúsùn àbíkú:** "...and improving the health of women and families in the country."  This is a more complex phrase. "ìtọ́jú pípéye" means overall health. "àmúlò àwọn àfojúsùn àbíkú" refers to the health of widows.
*   **ní orílẹ̀ èdè àti lábẹ́lé:** "...in the country and within the family."
*   **mímú ìlọsíwájú bá ìdíwọ̀n àwọn àbíkú:** "Furthermore, the advancement of inheritance rights for widows..." "ìlọsíwájú" means advancement. "ìdíwọ̀n àwọn àbíkú" means inheritance rights of widows.
*   **láti mú ìdàgbàsókè bá ẹ̀rí àti ìmọ̀:** "...is essential to ensuring their economic stability and well-being through health and knowledge." "ìdàgbàsókè" means economic stability. "ẹ̀rí" means well-being, and "ìmọ̀" means knowledge.



I hope this translation is helpful! Let me know if you have any other Yoruba texts you'd like me to translate.

translations.source: Àwọn ohun èlò ìṣègùn ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ. Ìsọnísókí: Ó tó bílíọ́nù méjì àwọn ènìyàn káàkiri àgbáyé ni wọn kò ní àǹfààní sí àwọn òògùn, abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára, irinṣẹ́ ìṣègùn tí wọ́n ṣe pàtàkì tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn irinṣẹ́ àyẹ̀wò àìsàn, àti àwọn ohun èlò ìlera mìíràn, èyí tí ó ń ṣẹ̀dá àlàfo tí ó máa ń sáábà jẹ́ dídí láti ọwọ́ àwọn ohun èlò ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ. Ìṣòro yìí ń dàgbà sí i bí ẹ̀wọ́n ìpèsè káàkiri àgbáyé ṣe ń gbòòrò sí i, tí ó túmọ̀ sí pé àwọn ohun èlò tí wọ́n pèsè ní orílẹ̀ èdè kan lè jẹ́ dídì ní orílẹ̀ èdè kejì tí wọn yóò sì máa pín wọn káàkiri àwọn odi ìlú láti polówó tàbí tà fún swkmt oníbàárà ní ìkẹta. Ìdàgbàsókè okòwò orí ẹ̀rọ tún ń nípa lórí àṣà yìí nípa mímú kí ó rọrùn láti ra àwọn òògùn lórí ẹ̀rọ ayélujára, láti ọwọ́ àwọn orísun tí wọ́n kò ní àṣẹ ní ọ̀pọ̀ ìgbà. WHO ti ṣe ìdámọ̀ ọ̀ràn yìí ọ̀kan nínú àwọn ìṣòro ìlera kíákíá fún ọdún mẹ́wàá tí ó ń bọ̀ nítorí pé ó lé ní ọ̀kan nínú mẹ́wàá àwọn òògùn ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín àti alábọ́dé èrè ni wọ́n ṣírò pé wọ́n jẹ́ ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ.
translations.reference: Substandard and falsified medical products Overview: Up to two billion people around the world lack access to necessary medicines, vaccines, medical devices including in vitro diagnostics, and other health products, which creates a vacuum that is too often filled by substandard and falsified products. This problem is growing as global supply chains become more complex, meaning products manufactured in one country may be packaged in a second country and distributed across borders to be marketed or sold to consumers in a third. The growth of e-commerce also contributes to this trend by making it easier to purchase medicines online, often from unauthorized sources. WHO has identified this issue one of the urgent health challenges for the next decade given that more than one in ten medicines in low- and middle-income countries are estimated to be substandard or falsified.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Counterfeit and Substandard Medicines.** **Background:** Approximately two billion people worldwide lack access to essential medicines, vaccines, diagnostic tests, and other health products, which creates a vulnerability to substandard and counterfeit products. This problem is exacerbated as the prevalence of fake medicines increases globally, meaning that medicines manufactured in one country can be substandard in another, and then distributed across urban areas for sale or to healthcare providers. The rise of online pharmacies also contributes to this issue, making it easier to purchase medicines online from sources that are not always authorized, often. WHO has identified this as one of the most serious public health threats for the next decade, as it is estimated that one in ten medicines in low- and middle-income countries are falsely identified as being substandard and counterfeit.

translations.source: Kò sí orílẹ̀ èdè tí kò tíì faragbá nínú ọ̀ràn yìí, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni WHO gba àwọn ìròyìn òògùn, abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára àti irinṣẹ́ àyẹ̀wò àìsàn ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ ní gbogbo àwọn ẹkùn àgbáyé. Àti àwọn òògùn abéròjàjórúkọ àti àwọn òògùn àtọọ́dá ni wọ́n ṣe é gbélẹ̀rọ, láti orí orí àwọn ohun èlò olówó gọbọi fún jẹjẹrẹ dórí àwọn ohun èlò tí wọ́n kò wọ́n rárá fún ìtọ́jú ìrora. Àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé fẹnukò lórí ọgbọ́n gbòòrò káríayé tí ó fojúsun ìdènà, àyẹ̀wò àti ìfèsì láti tẹ̀síwájú lórí àṣeyọrí níní àǹfààní púpọ̀ sí ohùn èlò ìṣègùn dídára, aláàbò, tí ó múnádóko tí ó sì dára. Ipa: Àwọn ohun èlò ìṣègùn ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ ṣẹ̀dá ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ìṣòro fún ati awon ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń lo àwọn òògùn náà àti fún àwọn ẹbí, àwùjọ gbòòrò, ètò ìtọ́jú ilera àti ẹ̀wọ́n ìpèsè. Nígbà tí àwọn òògùn kò bá ṣiṣẹ́ bí ó ṣe yẹ kí wọ́n ṣiṣẹ́, wọ́n lè kùnà láti ṣèwòsàn tàbí dènà àìsàn, ń dá kún ìlòdìsí òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò, ìfiṣòfò àwọn àlùmọ́ọ́nì iyebíye tí yóò sì yọrí sí àdánù ìgboyà nínú lílo àwọn òògùn àti àìfọkàntán àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú ìlera.
translations.reference: No country remains untouched from this issue, and WHO has received reports of substandard or falsified medical medicines, vaccines and in vitro diagnostics from all regions of the world. Both generic and innovator medicines can be falsified, ranging from very expensive products for cancer to very inexpensive products for treatment of pain. WHO Member States have agreed on a comprehensive global strategy focused on prevention, detection and response to move towards achieving increased access to quality, safe, effective and quality medical products. Impact: Substandard and falsified medical products create many problems for both the individual taking the medicine and for families, the larger society, health care system and supply chain. When medicines do not work the way they should, they can fail to treat or prevent disease, contribute to antimicrobial resistance, waste precious resources and lead to a loss of confidence in medicines and distrust of healthcare providers.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"No country has been spared in this crisis, and the WHO has received reports of counterfeit medicines, substandard medical products, and false tests for serious and life-threatening diseases from all over the world. Both generic and branded medicines are being produced illegally, from simple household items to those not intended for the treatment of illness. Member states of the World Health Assembly are concerned about the global trend focusing on production, testing, and certification to advance success in providing significant benefits to quality medical products, safe, effective, and affordable. Impact: Counterfeit and substandard medical products create numerous problems for and for people who use these medicines, families, global communities, health screening programs, and healthcare systems. When medicines do not work as they should, they can fail to treat or cure diseases, leading to antimicrobial resistance, undermining the credibility of quality assurance, and ultimately leading to a decline in the effectiveness of medicines and the frustration of healthcare workers."

translations.source: Èyí lè ní ipa ajẹmọ́ àwùjọ òun ọrọ̀ ajé òdì, níní ipa lórí àdánù tí ó ní í ṣe nínú ìpèsè àti ṣíṣe àfikún àwọn owónàá fún ènìyàn náà àti ètò ìlera orílẹ̀ èdè. Ó ṣe é ṣe kí wọ́n kàn sí àwọn aláìsàn ní àwọn ipò níbi tí ìdènà àǹfààní ti wà sí àwọn ohun èlò ìṣègùn dídára àti aláàbò, ìdarí tí kò dára àti ipa ìmọ̀ tí kò lágbára. Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé ọ̀ràn yìí ń nípa lórí gbogbo orílẹ̀ èdè, àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè níbi tí wọ́n kò ti ètò ìdarí aláìlágbára tàbí tí kò sí ti ń rú àjàgà púpọ̀ àwọn ohun èlò ìṣègùn ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ. Ní ayé tí ó ti lajú sí i, kò sí orílẹ̀ èdè tí ó ní àwọn èròjà tí ó tó àti agbára láti gbógun ti ọ̀ràn náà ní àdáwà. Nípasẹ̀ àwọn ètò àti ìgbésẹ̀ àgbáyé bí Ètò Àkóso àti Àmójútó Káríayé ẹgbẹ́ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé pẹ̀lú ọ̀ràn yìí ní àdáwà.
