"This can have a significant impact on community health, particularly on financial resources, within the scope of prevention and the implementation of additional methods for people and national health systems. It is necessary for them to respond to patients in positions where the availability of medical equipment is limited and inadequate, poor management, and weak knowledge. Although this issue affects all countries, those countries without strong management systems or that do not have sufficient access to advanced and modern medical equipment are particularly vulnerable. In the world we live in, there is no country with resources that are sufficient and powerful enough to combat this issue effectively. Through global programs and initiatives such as the Governance and Coordination Program of the World Health Organization, this issue is being addressed."

translations.source: Àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ ń ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ láti dènà, ṣàyẹ̀wò àti fèsì sí àwọn ohun èlò ìṣègùn ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ.
translations.reference: Member States are working together to prevent, detect and respond to substandard and falsified medical products.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Yoruba document:

"Countries within the community are working together to develop, review, and agree on sustainable development and environmental protection tools."



**Explanation of Choices:**

*   **"Countries within the community"** -  "Àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́" literally means "countries that are members of the group/community."
*   **"are working together"** - "ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀" means "working together."
*   **"to develop, review, and agree on"** - "dènà, ṣàyẹ̀wò àti fèsì sí" translates to "to develop, review, and agree to."
*   **"sustainable development and environmental protection tools"** - "àwọn ohun èlò ìṣègùn ayédèrú àti àgbélẹ̀rọ" refers to "tools of sustainable development and environmental protection."

translations.source: Ìdènà ìgbẹ́míaraẹni Ìsọnísókí: Ó lé ní àwọn ènìyàn 700 000 ni wọ́n kú nípa ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni lọ́dọọdún. Fún ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni kọ̀ọ̀kan, ìṣirò àwọn ìgbìyànjú láti gbẹ̀mí ara ẹni tí ó ju 20 ló wà. Ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni lè wáyé ní èyíkéyìí ìpele ìgbésí ayé àti ní gbogbo ẹkùn àgbáyé. Ní 2019, ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ni asíwájú okùnfà ikú kẹrin láàrin àwọn ọmọ ọdún 15-29 káàkiri àgbáyé, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni 77% gbogbo àwọn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ṣẹlẹ̀ ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín àti àti elérè gọbọi.
translations.reference: Suicide prevention Overview: More than 700 000 people die by suicide every year. For each suicide, there are an estimated more than 20 suicide attempts. Suicide can occur at any stage of life and in all regions of the world. In 2019, suicide was the fourth leading cause of death among 15–29-year-olds globally, and 77% of all suicides happened in low- and middle-income countries.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Yoruba document:

**Suicide Statistics:** Approximately 700,000 people die each year from suicide. For every suicide, there are more than 20 attempts to take one's own life. Suicide can occur at any stage of life and in every region of the world. In 2019, suicide was the fourth leading cause of death among 15-29 year olds worldwide, and 77% of all suicides occur in low- and middle-income countries.

translations.source: Ó fẹ́rẹ̀ẹ́ tó 20% àwọn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni lágbàáyé ṣẹlẹ̀ láti ara mímu májèlé òògùn apako. Nígbà tí àjọṣepọ̀ láàrin ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àti ìṣòro ìlera orí (pàápàá jùlọ, ìbànújẹ́ ọkàn àti ìṣòro ọtí mímu) bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn ìgbìyànjú gbẹ̀mí ara ẹni tẹ́lẹ̀ fẹsẹ̀ múlẹ̀ ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè gọbọi, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni máa ń ṣẹlẹ̀ lásìkò ìṣòro. Àwọn okùnfà ewu fún ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ni ìrírí àdánù, ìdáwà, ẹ̀yàmẹ̀yà, ìyapa ibáraẹniṣepọ̀, ìṣòro owó, ìrora ńlá àti àìsàn, ìwà ipá, àti ìjà tàbí àwọn pàjáwìrì ajẹmọ́ ọmọnìyàn mìíràn. Ìdẹ́yẹsí tí ó yí ìṣòro ilerat orí ká àti ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni túmọ̀ sí pé ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń ronú nípa ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni kò wá ìrànlọ́wọ́. Àwọn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àti ìgbìyànjú láti gbẹ̀mí ara ẹni ní agbára tí ó ń nípa lórí ẹbí, ọ̀rẹ́, alábàáṣiṣẹ́pọ̀, àwùjọ àti ìlú.
translations.reference: Almost 20% of global suicides were due to pesticide self-poisoning. While the link between suicide and mental health conditions (in particular, depression and alcohol use disorders) and a previous suicide attempt is well established in high-income countries, many suicides happen impulsively in moments of crisis. Further risk factors for suicide include experience of loss, loneliness, discrimination, a relationship break-up, financial problems, chronic pain and illness, violence, abuse, and conflict or other humanitarian emergencies. Stigma around mental health conditions and suicide means many people thinking about suicide do not seek help. Suicides and suicide attempts have a ripple effect that impacts on families, friends, colleagues, communities and societies.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"It is estimated that about 20% of dementia cases worldwide are due to vascular damage from stroke. While the association between dementia and neurological disorders (most commonly, Alzheimer's disease and vascular dementia) is well-established, many cases of dementia occur during or after a stroke. Risk factors for dementia include high blood pressure, diabetes, obesity, depression, financial difficulties, severe illness, behavioral problems, and violence or other traumatic events. The link between neurological disorders and dementia suggests that many people concerned about dementia do not receive help. Dementia and its consequences have a significant impact on family, friends, colleagues, community, and the city."

translations.source: Ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni: àwọn òtítọ́ àti iye káàkiri àgbáyé Ìròyìn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni káàkiri àgbáyé fún 2019 GBÉ AYÉ: ṣíṣe àmúlò ìtọ́nisọ́nà funt ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè Ìdènà: Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ nǹkan lè jẹ́ ṣíṣe ní àwọn ìpele ẹ̀dá kọ̀ọ̀kan, àwùjọ àti orílẹ̀ èdè láti dènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àti ìgbìyànjú gbẹ̀mí ara ẹni. Ìlànà GBÉ AYÉ àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé dábàá àwọn ìpèsè mẹ́rin pàtàkì tí wọ́n múnádóko, adálórí-ẹ̀rí èyí tí ó gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ fífi sí ara ìtọ́jú orílẹ̀ èdè kọ̀ọ̀kan dórí ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni:
translations.reference: Suicide: facts and figures globally Suicide worldwide in 2019 report LIVE LIFE: an implementation guide for suicide prevention in countries Prevention: Much can be done at individual, community and national levels to prevent suicide and suicide attempts. WHO’s LIVE LIFE approach recommends four effective, evidence-based key interventions which should be included in every national response to suicide:
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Sustainability: Facts and Figures Worldwide**

**Global Report: Implementing the Sustainability Agenda for 2019**

**GLOBAL:** Implementing a sustainability agenda for environmental sustainability can be achieved at each level – individual, community, and national – through various actions to promote sustainability and personal well-being.

The GLOBAL plan by the World Health Organization outlines four key pillars that must be prioritized and adapted to the specific context of each country to achieve environmental sustainability:

translations.source: fífi gbèdéke sí àǹfààní sí àwọn ọ̀nà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni níní àjọsọ pẹ̀lú ilé-iṣẹ́ ìròyìn fún ìròyìn tí ó tọ̀nà lórí ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ìgbèrú àwọn ìmọ̀-ọ́nṣe ìgbésí ayé àwùjọ ajẹmọ́ ìmọ̀lára láàrin àwọn ọ̀dọ́mọdé ìdámọ̀ àsìkò, ìgbéléwọ̀n, àkóso àti ìtẹ̀lé ẹnikẹ́ni tí ó ní ìpalára nípa àwọn ìwà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni. Àwọn òpó ìpìlẹ̀ ìdásí náà ni àlàyé ipò, àjọṣepọ̀ ọlọ́pọ̀ ẹ̀ka, ṣíṣe ìpolongo, ìṣẹ̀dá agbára, ṣíṣe ètò ìṣúná, àmójútó, àkóso àti ìgbéléwọ̀n.
translations.reference: limit access to the means of suicide interact with the media for responsible reporting of suicide foster socio-emotional life skills in adolescents early identify, assess, manage and follow up anyone who is affected by suicidal behaviours. The foundational pillars of the interventions are situation analysis, multisectoral collaboration, awareness raising, capacity building, financing, surveillance, monitoring and evaluation.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba text into English, aiming for accuracy and clarity:

"To provide feedback to the community on ethical practices within the media, in collaboration with a news agency, for accurate reporting on ethical issues concerning the life experiences of diverse social scientists, moral philosophers among young people, addressing issues of addiction, violence, control, and the tracking of anyone with concerns about ethical values. The key components of this process include situation analysis, multi-sectoral collaboration, policy implementation, empowerment, the development of intervention programs, advocacy, management, and monitoring."

**Explanation of some choices and nuances:**

*   **"àǹfààní sí àwọn ọ̀nà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni"**: This is a complex phrase. I've interpreted it as "provide feedback to the community on ethical practices."  "Àǹfààní" implies a response or feedback. "Ọ̀nà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni" refers to ethical practices or ways of behaving ethically.
*   **"ilé-iṣẹ́ ìròyìn"**:  Directly translates to "news agency."
*   **"ìgbésí ayé àwùjọ ajẹmọ́ ìmọ̀lára"**: This refers to the life experiences of diverse social scientists and moral philosophers.
*   **"ìdámọ̀ àsìkò, ìgbéléwọ̀n, àkóso àti ìtẹ̀lé"**: These are common terms: addiction, violence, control, and tracking.
*   **"òpó ìpìlẹ̀ ìdásí"**:  This refers to the "key components of this process."
*   The rest of the terms are fairly standard and translated directly.



I hope this translation is helpful! Let me know if you have any other Yoruba texts you'd like me to translate.

translations.source: Àwọn akitiyan ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni pè fún àkóso àti àjọṣepọ̀ láàrin àwọn ọ̀pọ̀ ẹ̀ka, tí ó fi mọ́ ìlera, ẹ̀kọ́, iṣẹ́, iṣẹ́ àgbẹ̀, okòwò, ìdájọ́, òfin, òṣèlú àti iṣẹ́ ìròyìn. Ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni kì í sáábà jẹ́ wíwá ojútùú sí nítorí ìdẹ́yẹsí àti àìsí ìpolongo pé ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni jẹ́ ìṣòro ìlera àwùjọ ńlá. Àwọn àwùjọ náà ń kó ipa pàtàkì nínú ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni. Irinṣẹ́ àmúlò ìdanilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ àwùjọ ti Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ń ṣe ìtọ́nisọ́nà àwọn àwùjọ láti ṣe ìdámọ̀ àwọn nǹkan pàtàkì àti ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn iṣẹ́ gẹ́gẹ́ bí àwọn ọ̀gangan ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ abẹ́lé. Èsì WHO: Ìpòǹgbẹ láti ṣiṣẹ́ dènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ni wọ́n ti ṣe ìdámọ̀ tí wọ́n sì ti sọ di pàtàkì ní àwọn ìpele gíga jùlọ.
translations.reference: Suicide prevention efforts require coordination and collaboration among multiple sectors, including health, education, labour, agriculture, business, justice, law, defence, politics and the media. Suicide prevention is often not adequately addressed because of stigma and lack of awareness that suicide is a major public health problem. Communities also play a crucial role in preventing suicide. The WHO community engagement toolkit guides communities in identifying priorities and implementing activities according to local contexts. WHO response: The urgency to act to prevent suicides has been recognized and prioritized at the highest levels.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"The determinants of health are linked to governance and collaboration among various sectors, including health, education, work, agriculture, transportation, justice, law, social welfare, and journalism. Health equity does not exist due to assumptions and a lack of recognition that health equity is a major public health issue. Communities play a crucial role in health equity. The framework for action on social determinants of health by the World Health Organization's Global Health Council provides guidance to communities to identify key issues and implement actions, such as early childhood interventions. WHO's focus: A platform for action on health equity has been established and prioritized at the highest levels."



I've aimed for accuracy and clarity while maintaining the original meaning. Let me know if you'd like any adjustments or further clarification!

translations.source: Àdínkù ìwọ̀n ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè jẹ́ ìtọ́ka nínú àwọn Àfojúsùn ti UN (ìtọ́ka fún ìlera orí kan ṣoṣo), Ètò Gbogbogbò Iṣẹ́ ti Àjọ Elétò ìlera Àgbáyé àti Ètò Iṣẹ́ Ìlera orí Gbòòrò 2013-2030. Ìròyìn ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àgbáyé kìíní ti Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé, Ìdènà Ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni: ìmọ̀ abágbàmu káríayé, tí wọ́n tẹ̀ jáde ní 2014, tí ó ní àfojúsùn láti mú ìgbéga bá ìpolongo pàtàkì ìlera àwùjọ ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àti àwọn ìgbìyànjú gbẹ̀mí ara ẹni àti sísọ ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni di pàtàkì ìlera àwùjọ. Wọ́n gba àwọn ìjọba ní ìyànjú láti ṣàmúlò àgbékalẹ̀ GBÉ AYÉ ti Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ti àwọn ìdásí pàtàkì pípé àti adálórí-ẹ̀rí fún ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àti láti mú ìdàgbàsókè tàbí ìrólágbára bá àwọn ọgbọ́n ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni àgbáyé gbígbòòrò nínú ìlànà ìlera àwùjọ ọlọ́pọ̀ ẹ̀ka. Ìdàrí àti àkóso àgbáyé lórí ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ni wọ́n túbọ̀ ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún nípa àtẹ̀jáde àwọn ọgbọ́n ìdènà ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni orílẹ̀ èdè: ìlọsíwájú, àwọn àpẹẹrẹ àti ìtọ́ka. Ṣíṣe ìdámọ̀ ìwífún àlàyé àti ìṣirò hebt fún ìkéde àti ṣíṣe ìpinnu, àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé tẹ Ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni jákèjádò àgbáyé ní 2019: Àwọn Ìṣirò Ìlera Àgbáyé.
translations.reference: The reduction of suicide rates in countries is an indicator in the UN Sustainable Development Goals (the only indicator for mental health), WHO’s General Programme of Work and WHO’s Comprehensive Mental Health Action Plan 2013–2030. The first WHO world suicide report, Preventing suicide: a global imperative, published in 2014, aimed to increase awareness of the public health significance of suicide and suicide attempts and make suicide prevention a public health priority. Governments are encouraged to implement WHO’s LIVE LIFE package of effective and evidence-based key interventions for suicide prevention, and to develop or strengthen comprehensive national suicide prevention strategies in a multisectoral public health approach. National leadership and coordination on suicide prevention are further supported by the publication of National suicide prevention strategies: progress, examples and indicators. Recognizing that data and statistics are necessary for advocacy and decision-making, WHO published Suicide worldwide in 2019: Global Health Estimates.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"The increasing burden of non-communicable diseases (NCDs) (focusing on cardiovascular disease specifically), the Global Action Plan for Prevention and Control of Non-communicable Diseases of the World Health Assembly, and the Global Target for NCDs 2013-2030. The World Health Organization's first global monitoring report on NCDs, "Global Monitoring Framework: A universal response, released in 2014, contains recommendations to accelerate progress on the national targets for NCDs and risk factors for premature mortality and to make NCDs a priority for national health policies. Governments are urged to implement the World Health Organization’s global action plan for the most effective and cost-effective interventions for NCDs and to strengthen the capacity of global NCD communities within a multi-sectoral health approach. Global leadership and governance on NCDs need to be further strengthened, particularly regarding the development of national NCD profiles: progress, examples, and indicators. To facilitate evidence-based policy-making and decision-making, the World Health Organization released Global NCD Targets 2019: Progress Report."



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Àfojúsùn àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé ni ayé tí ikú nípa ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni kò ti síwájú okùnfà ikú àti níbi tí àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń gbìyànjú tàbí ronú nípa ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni ti lè rí ìrọ̀rùn láti béèrè fún ìrànlọ́wọ́ èyí tí wọ́n ń pèsè nínú ipá àti ọ̀wọ̀ fún gbogbo ènìyàn.
translations.reference: WHO’s vision is a world where death by suicide is no longer a leading cause of death and where people who are attempting or thinking about suicide feel comfortable seeking help which is provided capably and respectfully to all.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba text into English:

"The World Health Organization's public health advisory states that the global death rate from non-communicable diseases has not improved, and that people who are suffering or concerned about these diseases are able to seek help, which is being provided within the body and limbs for all people."

**Explanation of some choices:**

*   **"Àfojúsùn àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé"** - Translated as "World Health Organization's public health advisory." This accurately conveys the meaning of an official announcement or guidance.
*   **"ìgbẹ̀míaraẹni"** - Translated as "non-communicable diseases." This is the standard English term for illnesses that aren't spread from person to person.
*   **"ń gbìyànjú tàbí ronú"** - Translated as "suffering or concerned." This captures the nuance of experiencing hardship or having worries about the diseases.
*   **"pèsè nínú ipá àti ọ̀wọ̀"** - Translated as "provided within the body and limbs." This is a slightly unusual phrasing in Yoruba, and the translation aims to convey the idea of help being accessible and available to individuals.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése Ìsọnísókí: Ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése ni ọ̀rọ̀ ìperí gbòòrò láti ṣàpèjúwe àwọn ìlànà, iṣẹ́ àkànṣe àti ìdókòwò tí ó ń pèsè àwọn àǹfààní lónìí láì kọlu ìlera àyíká, àwùjọ àti ti ara ẹni lọ́jọ́ iwájú. Àwọn ìlànà wọ̀nyí ni wọ́n sáábà máa ń ṣàpèjúwe gẹ́gẹ́ bí àìléwu nítorí pé wọ́n fojúsùn mímú àdínkù bá ipa ìdàgbàsókè lórí àyíká. Àmọ́ sá, àwọn àǹfààní ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése ń jẹ́ rírí káàkiri àwọn onírúurú ẹ̀ka ìlera àti àlàáfíà ọmọnìyàn, tí ó fi mọ́ àdínkù abayíkájẹ́- àti àìsàn adálórí àyíká, àwọn àbájáde ìlera tí ó lọ síwájú àti àdínkù ìṣòro. Bí ìṣòro àyípadà ojú ọjọ́ ṣe ń ṣe pàtàkì sí i, àwọn ọgbọ́n àmúṣiṣẹ́ fún àdínkù ìbàjẹ́, ìtẹ̀síwájú ìlera àti díndín iyeó kù ń pèsè oríṣiríṣi àǹfààní fún àwọn onírúurú àwọn àjọ.
translations.reference: Sustainable development Overview: Sustainable development is a broad term to describe policies, projects and investments that provide benefits today without sacrificing environmental, social and personal health in the future. These policies are often described as green because they focus on limiting the impact of development on the environment. However, the benefits of sustainable development are also felt across a wide cross section of human health and well-being, including reductions in pollution- and environment-related disease, improved health outcomes and decreased stress. As the threats of climate change become increasingly important, win-win strategies for mitigation, health improvement and cost savings offer a range of advantages for various stakeholders.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba text into English:

**Elderly Care and Geriatrics: Elderly care is a broad field dedicated to describing the methods, functional interventions, and support systems that serve the needs of the elderly today in order to prevent chronic disease, disability, and personal decline in the future. These methods are generally described as preventative because they focus on maximizing the impact of elderly care on longevity. Indeed, the benefits of elderly care are significant across various areas of health and well-being, including preventing falls and chronic diseases, advancing health outcomes, and increasing resilience. Considering the importance of age-related eye diseases, healthcare professionals focused on improving damage, promoting health advancement, and reducing mortality are addressing various benefits for diverse communities.**

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   **Specificity:** I've tried to maintain the specific terminology used in the Yoruba text, particularly regarding healthcare and elderly care.
*   **Flow:** I've aimed for a natural and readable English flow while staying true to the original meaning.
*   **Context:** The text seems to be an introduction or overview of geriatric care, so the translation reflects that.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Fún àpẹẹrẹ, àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìtọ́jú ìlera kánrinkése lè ṣẹ̀dá àwọn àǹfààní ìlera aláìsàn àti òṣìṣẹ́ nígbà tí wọ́n tún ń pa agbára mọ́, ṣe àdínkù ewu ojú ọjọ́ àti ìṣẹ̀dá àdínkù iyeó ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́. Àjọ Elétò Ìlera àgbáyé ti pinnu láti lépa ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése nínú gbogbo iṣẹ́ rẹ̀ láti ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ dènà àwọn ènìyàn ọjọ́ iwájú lọ́wọ́ àwọn ewu ìlera ọjọ́ òní tí wọ́n ń dàgbà. Ipa: Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ọgbọ́n ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése lè pèsè àwọn àṣeyọrí pàtàkì fún ìlera, ojú ọjọ́ àti àyíká, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àǹfààní rẹ̀ lè jẹ́ rírí lẹ́sẹ̀kẹsẹ̀. Fún àpẹẹrẹ, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àfojúsùn ìlera àti ìdàgbàsókè lè jẹ́ àṣeyọrí papọ̀ nípa gbígbógun ti ìbàfẹ́fẹ́jẹ́, èyí tí ó ń ṣokùnfà ìṣirò mílíọ̀nù 7 ikú àìtọ́jọ́ lọ́dọọdún. Lára àwọn ohun abàyíkájẹ́ ni àwọn abojú-ọjọ́-jẹ́ alágbára ọlọ́jọ́ díẹ̀ (SLCPs) – pàápàá jùlọ èédú dúdú, òyì àrá àti gáàsì mẹtíìnì– tí wọ́n léwu púpọ̀ fún ìlera ṣùgbọ́n àsìkò ìsẹ̀mí wọn kì í pẹ́.
translations.reference: For example, greener health care operations can generate patient and worker health benefits while also saving energy, mitigating climate risks and creating long-term cost savings. The World Health Organization is committed to pursuing sustainable development in all its work to help protect the people of tomorrow from the health growing health risks of today. Impact: Many sustainable development strategies can offer significant wins for health, climate and the environment, and the benefits can be seen almost immediately. For example, many health and development goals can be achieved simultaneously by tackling air pollution, which is responsible for an estimated 7 million premature deaths annually. Polluting emissions include powerful short-lived climate pollutants (SLCPs)—particularly black carbon, ozone and methane—which are very harmful to health but have only brief atmospheric lifetimes.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"For example, healthcare workers can contract serious illnesses and diseases while also experiencing increased eye strain and the development of long-term vision problems. The World Health Organization has decided to incorporate infection prevention into all of its work to help protect people from the health risks of today while facing the dangers of tomorrow. Impact: Many infection prevention measures can achieve significant successes for health, vision, and longevity, and their impact can be immediate. For example, many health and prevention interventions can be effective in combating infectious diseases, which cause approximately 7 million preventable deaths annually. Among the critical threats are severe, low-dose ocular toxins (SLCPs) – primarily lead, arsenic, and cadmium – which pose a significant health risk but have relatively short latency periods."

translations.source: Àwọn ìlànà ìgbòkègbodò ọkọ̀ àti ìdókòwò tí wọ́n ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ìgbòkègbodò ọkọ̀ àwùjọ mímọ́, pẹ̀lú ìrìn àti kẹ̀kẹ́ gígùn, lè ní ipa rere lórí dídára afẹ́fẹ́. Èyí lè ṣe àdínkù àwọn ewu ìlera lásìkò kan náà, tí ó fi mọ́ ẹ̀gbà, àìsàn ọkàn, àìsàn ẹ̀dọ̀ fóóró àti àwọn jẹjẹrẹ kan. Wọ́n tún lè ṣe àdínkù ìṣirò àwọn ikú mílíọ̀nù 1.25 ọdọọdún láti ara ìfarapa òpópónà, nígbà tí ó ń mú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá àǹfààní sí àwọn iṣẹ́ ìlera, pàápàá jùlọ ní àwọn àgbègbè tí àwọn ènìyàn pọ̀ sí. Àwọn ìlànà ilégbèé ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése lè túbọ̀ ṣe àdínkù àwọn ìtújáde nípasẹ̀ àwọn ìpinnu bí ilẹ̀ ilé àti ìlò ilẹ̀, àwọn àṣàyàn ohun èlò ìkọ́lé, àwọn àbùdá àgbékalẹ̀ àti afẹ́fẹ́ òhun agbára. Èsì WHO: Iṣẹ́ WHO ń gbà ìtọ́nisọ́nà láti ọwọ́ àwọn Àfojúsùn Ìdàgbàsókè Kánrinkése, pàápàá jùlọ Àfojúsùn 3: "Ẹ rí i dájú pé ẹ ní ìgbé ayé ìlera àti ìgbéga àlàáfíà fún gbogbo ọjọ́ orí."
translations.reference: Transport policies and investments that favour clean public transport, along with walking and cycling, can have a dramatic impact on air quality. This can reduce multiple health risks at the same time, including stroke, heart attack, lung disease and some cancers. They can also reduce the estimated 1.25 million deaths annually from traffic injury, while also improving access to health services, particularly in densely populated areas. Sustainable development housing policies can further reduce emissions through considerations such as building siting and land use, choices of construction materials, design features and ventilation and energy. WHO response: WHO’s work is guided by the Sustainable Development Goals, particularly Goal 3: “Ensure healthy lives and promote well-being for all at all ages.”
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Strategies for controlling malaria and mosquito vectors that support community-wide malaria prevention, including nets and insecticides, can have a positive impact on reducing severe anemia. This can increase the risk of certain illnesses, such as pneumonia, heart disease, and severe diarrhea, and can also increase the mortality rate of 1.25 million children annually, while straining healthcare systems, especially in densely populated areas. Integrated disease control interventions can further increase progress through measures such as household sanitation and water management, choices of household cooking fuels, community needs, and strong ventilation. WHO Statement: WHO receives guidance from the Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria, especially Fund 3: "Ensure a healthy life and well-being for all at all ages." "



**Notes on the Translation:**

*   I've aimed for accuracy while maintaining readability in English.
*   Some phrases required interpretation to convey the intended meaning clearly.
*   "Àfojúsùn" is translated as "Fund" in this context, referring to the Global Fund.

translations.source: Àwọn Àfojúsùn náà mú àwọn iṣẹ́ àkànṣe àti àwọn ìlànà káàkiri Àjọ wà, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn ìtọ́nisọ́nà lórí ilégbèé, ìdàgbàsókè ìlú, àwọn ètò ìgbòkègbodò ọkọ̀ àti àgbékalẹ̀ ìtọ́jú ìlera. Fún àpẹẹrẹ, àwọn ilégbèé WHO àti ìtọ́nisọ́nà ìlera, tí wọ́n kọ́kọ́ tẹ̀ jáde ní 2018, ṣe ìdámọ̀ ipa àwọn ìṣòro ilégbèé tí ó ń pọ̀ sí i lórí ìlera ọmọnìyàn nítorí ìdàgbàsókè ìlú, ojú ọjọ́ àti àwọn àyípadà iye ènìyàn. Àwọn ìtọ́nisọ́nà ń pèsè àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n jẹ́ ọmọ ẹgbẹ́, àwọn alábàáṣiṣẹ́pọ̀ àti àwùjọ pẹ̀lú àwọn àbá adálórí-ẹ̀rí tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àìtó ààyè ìgbé (ọ̀pọ̀ èèyàn), àwọn ìwọ̀n ìgbóná kékeré àti gọbọi inú ilé, àwọn ewu ìfarapa nínú ilé, àti níní àǹfààní sí ilégbèé fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ní ìṣòro iṣẹ́ ṣíṣe. Ètò Ìlera Ìgboro (EII) tún fojúsun àwọn ọ̀nà fún ìlú láti fààyè gba ìlera rere nípa ṣíṣe àyàṣe iṣẹ́ tí ó múnádóko lórí ìbàfẹ́fẹ́jẹ́ ìlú àti àwọn abàyíkájẹ́ ojú ọjọ́ ọlọ́jọ́ péréte (SLCPs) – gbígba ẹ̀mí là nípa mímú àjọṣepọ̀ bá ìlera, àyíká àti ìdàgbàsókè kánrinkése. Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé tún ṣe àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú Àgbájọ Afẹ́fẹ́ àti Ojú ọjọ́ mímọ́ lórí Ìpolongo Ayé Ìmí, èyí tí ó fojúsun mímú àdínkù bá ìbàfẹ́fẹ́jẹ́ àti mímú àdínkù bá mílíọ̀nù 7 ikú àìtọ́jọ́ lọ́dọọdún tí ó ń fà.
translations.reference: The Goals inform projects and policies across the Organization, including guidelines on housing, urban development, transportation systems and health care delivery. For example, the WHO Housing and health guidelines, first published in 2018, highlight the increasing impact of housing conditions on human health in light of urban growth, climate and demographic changes. The guidelines provide Member States, partners and the public with evidence-based recommendations relevant to inadequate living space (crowding), low and high indoor temperatures, injury hazards in the home, and accessibility of housing for people with functional impairments. The Urban Health Initiative (UHI) also focuses on ways for cities to enable good health by catalysing effective action on urban air pollution and short-lived climate pollutants (SLCPs)—saving lives by linking health, environment and sustainable development. WHO also collaborates with the Climate and Clean Air Coalition (CCAC) on the Breathe Life campaign, which aims to cut air pollution and reduce the 7 million premature deaths annually that it causes.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"The experts highlighted the policies and strategies around the Assembly concerning issues of health, urban development, transport safety programs, and the strengthening of health surveillance systems. For example, the WHO guidelines and health surveillance indicators, initially released in 2018, assessed the impact of household challenges that are increasing due to urban development, air pollution, and population decline. The indicators address countries that are members, partners, and communities with leaders who deal with mortality rates (high number of deaths), childhood illnesses and domestic violence, household accidents, and the impact on households for people with disabilities. The Community Health Program (CHP) also focuses on ways for cities to improve health by addressing work-related risks that impact urban air quality and sources of pollution (SLCPs) – reducing mortality through collaboration with health, sanitation, and urban development initiatives. The Global Health Electors Council also collaborates with the Global Alliance for Air and Health on the Agenda for Life, which focuses on increasing action on air pollution and preventing the approximately 7 million preventable deaths that occur annually."

translations.source: Sífílì Ìsọnísókí: Sífílì jẹ́ àìsàn àrùn ìbálòpọ̀ tí kòkòrò bakitéríà ń tàn ká láti ọwọ́ Treponema pallidum èyí tí ó máa ń fa ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ewu àìsàn àti ikú, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ṣe é wòsàn. Sífílì máa ń ràn ká nípasẹ̀ ìfarakínra ìbálòpọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn ògòdò àrùn, nípasẹ̀ ìfàjẹ̀sínilára tàbí láti ara aláboyún obìnrin sí ọmọ inú rẹ̀. Ìkóràn sífílì láti ara ìyá-sí-ọmọ sáábà máa ń le púpọ̀ fún ọlẹ̀ ní àwọn ọ̀ràn tí wọ́n kò dá mọ̀ tàbí ṣe ìtọ́jú tó níbẹ̀rẹ̀ ìlóyún. Sífílì ìyálọ́mọ, nígbà tí kò bá rí ìtọ́jú, wọ́n pẹ́ tọ́jú rẹ̀ tàbí wọn kò tọ́jú rẹ pẹlu pẹnisilíìnì, ń yọrí sí àwọn àbájáde ìbí líle nínú ìṣirò àwọn ọ̀ràn 50-80%, dá lórí ìpele sífílì.
translations.reference: Syphilis Overview: Syphilis is a bacterial sexually transmitted infection (STI) caused by Treponema pallidum which results in substantial morbidity and mortality, and it is curable. Syphilis is transmitted through sexual contact with the infectious lesions, via blood transfusion or from a pregnant woman to her fetus. Mother-to-child transmission of syphilis is usually devastating to the fetus in cases that were not detected or treated sufficiently early in pregnancy. Maternal syphilis, when untreated, treated late or not treated with penicillin, results in adverse birth outcomes (ABOs) estimated in 50−80% of cases, depending on the stage of syphilis.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Syphilis: Explanation** Syphilis is a sexually transmitted disease caused by the bacterium Treponema pallidum, which can lead to many health problems and death if left untreated. Syphilis is transmitted through sexual contact with infected individuals, through blood transfusions, or from a pregnant woman to her fetus. Syphilis contracted from mother to child (congenital syphilis) can be particularly severe, causing illnesses that are often undiagnosed or inadequately treated, leading to long-term complications. Treatment for syphilis, whether it is diagnosed early or later, is typically with penicillin, which leads to favorable outcomes in approximately 50-80% of cases, depending on the stage of syphilis.

translations.source: Àwọn àbájáde ìbí líle sáábà máa ń tí ó fi mọ́ àbíkú (lọ́pọ̀ ìgbà), ikú ìkókó, àìgbó, ìwọ̀n ìwúwo ìbí pínnísín, àti àwọn ìkókó tí àìsàn sọ di abiùn. Sífílì lè ṣe é dènà nípasẹ̀ àwọn àṣà ìbálòpọ̀ aláàbò tí ó fi mọ́ ìlò ọ̀rá ìdáàbòbò bí ó ti tọ́ àti déédéé. Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn pẹ̀lú sífílì kò ní èyíkéyìí àmì àìsàn kankan tàbí ní àwọn àmì àìsàn kékeré tí wọ́n kì í ṣàkíyèsí. Ṣíṣe ìdámọ̀ àìsàn nípasẹ̀ àwọn àyẹ̀wò láàbù àti ìtọ́jú àwọn ọ̀ràn àìsàn yóò dènà ìkóràn síwájú àti àbájáde ìlóyún líle tí ó fi mọ́ àbájáde àrùn sífílì. Àyẹ̀wò lè ṣe é ṣe nípa ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn àyẹ̀wò ayákánkán tí wọ́n lè pèsè àbájáde ní kò tó ìṣẹ́jú 20, tí ó ń fi ààyè gba ìtọ́jú àsìkò.
translations.reference: These ABOs are often severe and include stillbirth (most commonly), neonatal death, prematurity, low birth weight, and congenitally infected infants. Syphilis can be prevented through safer sex practices including the correct and consistent use of condoms. Many people with syphilis do not have any symptoms or have only minor symptoms that go unnoticed. Identifying infection through laboratory tests and treatment of positive cases will prevent further transmission and adverse pregnancy outcomes including congenital syphilis. Screening can be done using rapid tests that can provide results in less than 20 minutes, allowing for immediate treatment.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba text into English:

"Postpartum complications during childbirth can lead to death (in many cases), sudden death, infections, postpartum hemorrhage, and complications that result in disability. Syphilis can contribute to this through traditional birth practices that lead to unsafe abortions and complications. Many people with syphilis do not have obvious symptoms of the disease or have minor symptoms that are overlooked. Early detection of the disease through thorough examinations and evaluation of medical history will prevent future complications and postpartum illnesses associated with syphilis. Examinations can be done by performing a series of tests that can provide results in less than 20 minutes, allowing for timely assessment."

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   I've aimed for clarity and accuracy while maintaining a professional tone.
*   Some Yoruba terms related to traditional practices are difficult to translate directly without more context, so I've used descriptive phrases.
*   The text emphasizes the importance of early detection and proper medical practices to prevent complications related to syphilis during and after childbirth.

translations.source: Nígbà tí kò bá rí ìtọ́jú, sífílì yóò lọ ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ọdún bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni yóò la onírúurú ìpele kọjá. Ìtọ́jú fún sífílì máa ń jẹ́ ṣíṣe pẹ̀lú òògùn pẹnisilíìnì benzathine tí ó ṣe é gún bí abẹ́rẹ́, pẹ̀lú ìlò 1 sí 3 èyí tí ó dá lórí ìpele àìsàn náà. Pẹnisilíìnì nìkan ló lè dènà àwọn sífílì àsìkò ìbí. Ìtọ́jú pẹ̀lú pẹnisilíìnì ti ní ìdènà láti ọwọ́ ọ̀wọ́ngógó òòrèkóòrè àti ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́ ní àwọn onírúurú orílẹ̀ èdè, tí ó fi mọ́ àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè gọbọi, pínnísín àti alábọ́dé èrè. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn pẹ̀lú sífílì kì í ṣàfihàn àwọn àmì àìsàn tàbí wọn kì í ní ìmọ̀lára wọn.
translations.reference: When untreated, syphilis lasts many years and goes through different stages. Treatment for syphilis is done with injectable penicillin benzathine, with 1 to 3 doses depending on the stage of the disease. Only penicillin can prevent congenital syphilis. Treatment with penicillin has been hampered by periodic and prolonged shortages of benzathine penicillin in numerous countries, including high-, low- and middle-income countries. Symptoms: Many people with syphilis do not present symptoms or do not perceive them.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"When syphilis is not treated, it will progress for many years and pass through various stages. Treatment for syphilis is done with the drug benzathine penicillin, which is given as an initial dose, with 1 to 3 injections depending on the severity of the disease. Penicillin alone can cure congenital syphilis. Treatment with penicillin has been effective against syphilis from health workers and long-term doctors in various countries, including eradicating endemic, epidemic, and pandemic syphilis. Symptoms: Many people with syphilis do not show symptoms or are unaware of them."

translations.source: Sífílì, àìtọ́jú lo ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ọdún bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó pín sí onírúurú àwọn ìpele:. Ìkóràn ìbálòpọ̀ máa ń ṣẹlẹ̀ lásìkò ìbẹ̀rẹ̀pẹ̀pẹ̀, ìyẹn ni pé, fún bí ọdún méjì lẹ́yìn àrùn. Lásìkò ìpele ìsáájú (sífílì ìpìlẹ̀), èyí tí yóò lo bí ọjọ́ 21, egbò àdáwà, aláìnírora roboto tí ó sáábà máa ń le kan (egbò) yóò farahàn ní agbègbè ìgbàsára (ìfarakanra), ní ọ̀pọ̀ ìgbà nínú òbò, okó tàbí fùrọ̀ (ṣùgbọ́n ó tún lè jẹ́ àfikún ojú ara) bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè lọ láìfarahàn. Ògòdò náà yóò gba ìwòsàn pẹ̀lú tàbí láìsí ìwòsàn láàrin ọ̀sẹ̀ 3 tàbí 10. Bí kò bá rí ìtọ́jú, àìsàn náà yóò tẹ̀síwájú sí ìpele àtẹ̀lé.
translations.reference: Untreated, syphilis lasts many years and is characterized into different stages. Sexual transmission typically occurs during early stages, i.e., up to 2 years after infection. During the initial phase (primary syphilis), which lasts on average 21 days, a solitary, painless, usually hard and round sore (chancre) appears at the site of inoculation (contact), often in the vagina, penis or anus (but it may also be extra-genital) and may go unnoticed. The chancre heals with or without treatment within 3 to 10 weeks. If untreated, the disease progresses to the secondary stage.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba text into English:

"Syphilis, without treatment, progresses through various stages:. Primary infection usually occurs at the beginning, that is, within two years after the disease. During the early stage (primary syphilis), which lasts for about 21 days, a chancre (a painless sore) may appear in the genital area (or other areas of the body), sometimes in the mouth, throat, or anus (it can also be an eyelid lesion), and it may heal without scarring. The disease will either receive treatment with or without treatment within 3 to 10 weeks. If there is no improvement, the disease will progress to the secondary stage."

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   **Medical Terminology:** I've used standard English medical terms (e.g., "chancre") to accurately convey the meaning.
*   **Clarity:** I've rephrased some sentences slightly to improve readability in English while staying true to the original meaning.
*   **Context:** The text appears to be about the stages of syphilis, a sexually transmitted infection.

translations.source: Sífílì àtẹ̀lé ní máa ń ní àbùdá kúrúkùsá tí ó lè jẹ́ onírúurú kí ó sì sé ìsìnjẹ àwọn ìṣòro àrùn tàbí àìsàn mìíràn, ṣùgbọ́n ó sáábà máa ń mú àtẹ́lẹwọ́ àti àtẹ̀lesẹ̀. Kúrúkùsá náà kì í húnni bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè kéré láti jẹ́ àfojúfò. Ní àwọn ẹ̀yà ara tí wọ́n gbóná tí wọ́n sì ní omi, bí ihò ìdí àti ẹnu, ògòdò títóbi funfun tàbí ewú lè dàgbà ní ọ̀gangan egbò ìpìlẹ̀ (condyloma lata). Àwọn àpẹẹrẹ àti àmì sífílì àtẹ̀lé máa ń sàn fúnra rẹ̀, kódà láìsí ìtọ́jú. Láìsí ìtọ́jú, ìpele afarasin bẹ̀rẹ̀.
translations.reference: Secondary syphilis is characterized by a rash that can vary widely and mimic other infectious or non-infectious conditions, but characteristically affects the palms and soles. The rash is non-itchy and can be minimal enough to be overlooked. In warm and moist areas of the body, such as the anus and labia, large white or grey raised lesions may develop at the location of the primary chancre (condyloma lata). The symptoms and signs of secondary syphilis spontaneously resolve, even without treatment. Without treatment, the latent stage starts.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Syphilis has a wide range of symptoms that can be varied and can manifest as sores or other illnesses, but it can also cause fatigue and weakness. The sores may not always be present and can be small and easily missed. In areas of the body that are moist, such as the genitals, anus, and mouth, large, cauliflower-like growths can develop in the genital area (condyloma lata). Examples and signs of syphilis can appear suddenly, even without noticeable symptoms. Without treatment, the latent stage begins."

**Notes on the translation:**

*   **Condyloma lata:** This term is kept as is, as it's a specific medical term.
*   **"Ìtọ́jú"** is translated as "noticeable symptoms" to convey the meaning of visible signs.
*   I've aimed for clarity and accuracy while maintaining a natural English flow.

translations.source: Sífílì afarasin kì í ṣàfihàn àwọn àmì àìsàn àti àpẹẹrẹ kankan bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ìtọ́jú rẹ̀ yàtọ̀ sí ìpele ìbẹ̀rẹ̀. Lẹ́yìn àwọn ọdún tàbí ẹ̀wádún láìsí ìtọ́jú, àwọn àìsàn ajẹmọ́ ọ̀páyìnmọ́pọlọ àti àwọn àìsàn ọkàn àti àwọn mìíràn lè wáyé. Ìtọ́jú: Àyẹ̀wò ìtọ́jú sífílì máa ń dá lórí ìtàn, àyẹ̀wò ojúkojú, àyẹ̀wò láàbù ènìyàn àti nígbà mìíràn àyẹ̀wò ẹ̀rọ afẹ́wẹ́lẹ́, nítorí àmì àìsàn kò wọ́pọ̀ tàbí farahàn. Ìdámọ̀ àrùn aláìlámì àìsàn nípasẹ̀ àwọn àyẹ̀wò láàbù àti ìtọ́jú àwọn ọ̀ràn àrùn yóò dènà ìkóràn síwájú sí i àti àwọn àbájáde oyún líle, tí ó fi mọ́ sífílì amútinúwá. Àwọn àyẹ̀wò láàbù fún sífílì tí ó fi mọ́ ìdámọ̀ tààrà T. pallidum tàbí ìdámọ̀ tààrà bí àyẹ̀wò sẹ́rọ́ọ̀mù inú ẹ̀jẹ̀.
translations.reference: Latent syphilis presents no clinical symptoms or signs and the treatment is different from the early phase. After years or decades without treatment, neurological and cardiovascular diseases among others may occur. Treatment: Syphilis diagnosis is based on the person’s history, physical examination, laboratory testing and sometimes radiology, as symptoms are not common or noticeable. Identifying asymptomatic infection through laboratory tests and treatment of positive cases will prevent further transmission and adverse pregnancy outcomes, including congenital syphilis. Laboratory tests for syphilis include direct detection of T. pallidum or indirect methods such as serology.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Syphilis does not manifest symptoms or any signs initially. However, its progression differs from the initial stage. After years or decades without treatment, complications such as neurosyphilis, cardiac syphilis, and others can arise. Diagnosis: Syphilis diagnosis is based on history, physical examination, laboratory tests, and sometimes advanced technological tests, because symptoms may not be apparent or obvious. Detecting latent infections through laboratory tests and the evaluation of disease patterns will lead to early intervention and better outcomes, which will benefit syphilis patients. Laboratory tests for syphilis can detect early *T. pallidum* infection or detect it as a serological test in the blood."

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   I've aimed for accuracy while maintaining clarity in English.
*   I've used terms like "neurosyphilis" and "cardiac syphilis" as they are standard medical terminology.
*   "*T. pallidum*" is the scientific name for the bacterium that causes syphilis and is left in its original form.
*   The phrasing in the original Yoruba is sometimes a bit complex, so I've tried to simplify it for better readability in English.

translations.source: Àwọn àgbéyẹ̀wò àti àyẹ̀wò kánkán tún wà bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni wọ́n lè pèsè àwọn àbájáde ní kò tó ìṣẹ́jú 20, tí yóò sì mú ìbẹ̀rẹ̀ ìtọ́jú kánkán wá. Àjọ Elétò ìlera àgbáyé dábàá pé kí sífílì máa jẹ́ títọ́jú nípa ṣíṣe àmúlò abẹ́rẹ́ pẹnisilíìnì (benzathine penicillin G) pẹ̀lú ìlò 1 tàbí mẹ́ta, dá lórí ìpele àìsàn náà. WHO ti ṣàlàyé àwọn ìlànà ìtọ́jú fún sífílì, àti ọkàn pàtàkì fún àyẹ̀wò àti ìtọ́jú sífílì nínú oyún. Sífílì amútinúwá Kò sí àyẹ̀wò kánkán báyìí fún àwọn sífílì amútinúwá.
translations.reference: Rapid screening and diagnostic tests are also available and can provide results in less than 20 minutes, facilitating immediate treatment initiation. WHO recommends syphilis to be treated with injectable penicillin (benzathine penicillin G) with 1 or 3 doses, depending on the stage of the disease. WHO has detailed treatment guidelines for syphilis, and one specifically for the screening and treatment of syphilis in pregnancy. Congenital syphilis There are currently no diagnostic tests for congenital syphilis.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Yoruba document:

"Screening and confirmatory tests can also be done to detect cases in less than 20 minutes, which will lead to the start of confirmatory diagnosis. The World Health Organization (WHO) has stated that syphilis should be treated with a single intramuscular dose of benzathine penicillin G, either one or three doses, depending on the severity of the disease. WHO has outlined diagnostic algorithms for syphilis, and key considerations for screening and diagnosis of syphilis in children. Congenital Syphilis There is currently no confirmatory test for congenital syphilis."

translations.source: Gbogbo alààyè tàbí àbíkú ìkókó àwọn obìnrin tí wọ́n ní sífílì gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ àgbéyẹ̀wò fún ẹ̀rí sífílì amútinúwá. Àwọn ìkókó àbíyè gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ yíyẹ̀wò àti àyẹ̀wò lásìkò ìbí láti sọ òté ìtọ́jú. Nígbà tí wọ́n bá tọ́ka sí i, bákan náà ní fún àlàfo oṣooṣù fún oṣù 3 títí àyẹ̀wò sẹ́rọ́ọ̀mù inú ẹ̀jẹ̀ ìkókó, tí ó sì wà, láìsí àmì àìsàn, nítorí àwọn àjẹsára ìyá lè ran ọmọ náà lásìkò oyún láìṣeṣẹ́.
translations.reference: All live or stillborn infants of women with syphilis should be examined for evidence of congenital syphilis. Live-born infants should be examined and tested at birth to define treatment requirement. When indicated, also at monthly intervals for 3 months until the infant’s serology are, and remain, negative, as maternal antibodies can be passively transmitted to the baby during pregnancy.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"All pregnant or postpartum women with pre-eclampsia must be monitored for pre-eclampsia severity. Postpartum cases must be assessed and monitored during the delivery period to identify complications. When indicated, they should receive monthly follow-up for 3 months until the postpartum internal examination of the uterus is normal, without any signs of illness, so that healthcare providers can support the child during the breastfeeding period without complications."

**Explanation of some choices:**

*   **Alààyè tàbí àbíkú:** Translated as "pregnant or postpartum women" to accurately convey the meaning.
*   **Sífílì:** Translated as "pre-eclampsia" as it is the medical term for the condition.
*   **Àgbéyẹ̀wò fún ẹ̀rí sífílì amútinúwá:** Translated as "monitored for pre-eclampsia severity" to capture the nuance of careful observation and assessment.
*   **Ìtọ́jú:** Translated as "complications" to reflect the potential issues being referred to.
*   **Àlàfo oṣooṣù:** Translated as "monthly follow-up" for clarity.
*   **Sẹ́rọ́ọ̀mù inú ẹ̀jẹ̀ ìkókó:** Translated as "postpartum internal examination of the uterus" to be precise.
*   **Àjẹsára ìyá:** Translated as "healthcare providers" to be more general and understandable.
*   **Oyún láìṣeṣẹ́:** Translated as "breastfeeding period without complications" to accurately convey the meaning.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Taeníásí àti sisikasósì Ìsọnísókí: Àìsàn pẹ̀lú aràn pẹlẹbẹ atara-ẹrankosẹ̀ lè fa àwọn ìṣòro ọ̀tọ̀ọ̀tọ̀ méjì: àrùn taẹniásì àti sisikakósì. Taeníásí jẹ́ àrùn ìfun pẹ̀lú aràn pẹlẹbẹ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó máa ń wáyé nígbà tí ènìyàn bá jẹ ẹlẹ́dẹ̀ tútù tàbí tí kò jinná, tàbí ẹlẹ́dẹ̀ tí ó ní àìsàn. Taeníásí kò ní ipa gbòógì kankan lórí ìlera ẹ̀dá. Àmọ́ sá, àwọn ẹyin aràn pẹlẹbẹ tí ẹni tí ó ní aràn pẹlẹbẹ nínú yóò jáde bọ́ síta lára ìgbẹ́ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àìsí ìmọ́tótó tí ó tọ́ lè mú àyíká di àìmọ́.
translations.reference: Taeniasis and cysticercosis Overview: Infection with the tapeworm Taenia solium can result in two distinct conditions: taeniasis and cysticercosis. Taeniasis is the intestinal infection with the tapeworm and it occurs when a person eats raw or undercooked, infected pork. Taeniasis has no major impact on human health. However, tapeworm eggs pass in the faeces of the tapeworm carrier and in the absence of a proper sanitation may contaminate the environment.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Tapeworm and Cysticercosis: An illness with larval tapeworm can cause two serious conditions: taeniasis and cysticercosis. Taeniasis is an infection with an adult tapeworm and occurs when a person eats undercooked meat or fish, or meat from an infected animal. Taeniasis has no significant impact on health. However, the larval tapeworm eggs of the person with taeniasis will be released into the environment and can cause cysticercosis, and a lack of awareness of this fact can lead to misinformation.**

**Explanation of some choices:**

*   **Taeníásí:** Translated as "Taeniasis" - this is the standard English medical term.
*   **Sisikakósì:** Translated as "Cysticercosis" - the standard English medical term.
*   **Aràn pẹlẹbẹ:** Translated as "larval tapeworm" - accurately describes the stage of the parasite being referred to.
*   **Ìsọnísókí:** Translated as "cysticercosis" - the standard English medical term.
*   The phrasing was adjusted to be clear and natural in English while maintaining the meaning of the original Yoruba text.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Nígbà tí aràn pẹlẹbẹ lè mú ẹlẹ́dẹ̀ ṣàìsàn, ẹyin T.solium náà lè mú kí ènìyàn ṣàìsàn bí ènìyàn bá jẹ wọn (nípasẹ̀ ọ̀nà ìgbẹ́-sí-ẹnu, tàbí nípa jíjẹ oúnjẹ tàbí omi tí ó ti di àìmọ́). Jíjẹ ẹyin aràn pẹlẹbẹ máa ń fa àìsàn pẹ̀lú ajọ̀fẹ́ bí ìdin nínú àwọn ìṣù ara bí isan, awọ ara, ojú àti ètò òpónà ọpọlọmẹ́yìn àárín gbùngbùn (sisikakósì ènìyàn), pẹ̀lú ipá líle lórí ìlera. Nígbà tí àwọn ògòdò paali bá dàgbà nínú ọpọlọ, àìsàn náà ni wọ́n ń pè ní nirosisikakósì. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Taeníásí sáábà máa ń ní àbùdá abọ́ọ́dé àti àwọn àbùdá aláìsọpàtó tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àìsàn ìfun (inú rírun, àyà rínrìn, ìgbẹ́ gbuuru, abbl.). Àwọn àmì àìsàn wọ̀nyí lè tẹ̀síwájú títí aràn pẹlẹbẹ náà yóò fi kú lẹ́yìn ìtọ́jú, bí bẹ́ẹ̀ kọ́ ó lè wà láyé fún ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ọdún.
translations.reference: While tapeworm eggs are infective for pigs, T. solium eggs may also infect humans if they are ingested by a person (via the fecal-oral route, or by ingesting contaminated food or water). Ingestion of tapeworm eggs causes infection with the larval parasite in the tissues such as muscles, skin, eyes and the central nervous system (human cysticercosis), with possible devastating effects on health. When cysts develop in the brain, the condition is referred to as neurocysticercosis. Symptoms: Taeniasis is usually characterized by mild and non-specific symptoms associated to an intestinal infection (abdominal pain, nausea, diarrhoea, etc.). These symptoms may continue until the tapeworm dies following treatment, otherwise it may live for years.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"When tapeworms are able to cause nutritional deficiencies, *Taenia solium* can cause humans to become ill like humans being infested by them (through the oral-fecal route, or by consuming contaminated food or water). The ingestion of tapeworm eggs often causes illness with symptoms such as itching, skin rashes, eye problems, and neurological disorders in the central nervous system (human cysticercosis), with a significant impact on health. When the larvae mature in the body, the illness is called neurocysticercosis. Symptoms of the illness: Individuals with taeniasis may experience abdominal pain and non-specific symptoms associated with malabsorption (nausea, large appetite, bad breath, etc.). These symptoms can persist until the tapeworm dies after treatment, or they can exist for many years."

**Key Notes on the Translation:**

*   **Technical Terms:** I've tried to use accurate medical terminology (e.g., "neurocysticercosis," "taeniasis," "cysticercosis") to reflect the content.
*   **Cultural Context:** The Yoruba text refers to the "oral-fecal route," which is a direct and understandable way to describe transmission.
*   **Clarity:** I've aimed for clarity and readability in English, while staying true to the meaning of the original Yoruba.
*   **"Aràn pẹlẹbẹ"**: This refers to tapeworms.
*   **"T.solium"**: This is *Taenia solium*, a specific type of tapeworm.
*   **"Neurocysticercosis"**: This is a serious condition where the larvae of the tapeworm form cysts in the brain and other parts of the nervous system.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Fún sisikakósì, àsìkò ìsàba rẹ̀ máa ń yàtọ̀, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn tí wọ́n ní àìsàn náà lè máa ní àmì àìsàn kankan fún ọ̀pọ̀ ọdún. Ní àwọn àgbègbè tí àrùn yìí tí wọ́pọ̀, àwọn tí wọ́n ń ṣe àìsàn yìí lè ní àwọn ògòdò tí ó hànde tàbí ńlá (kókó tàbí ògòdò kékeré líle) tí ó wà nísàlẹ̀ awọ. Nirosisikakósì ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àwọn onírúurú àmì àti àpẹẹrẹ tí ó dá lórí iye, ìtóbi, ìpele àti ibi àwọn àyípadà ara àti èsì àjẹsára aláìsàn àti àbùdá ajọ̀fẹ́ náà pẹ̀lú, ṣùgbọ́n ó tún lè má ní àmì àìsàn ìlera kankan. Àwọn àmì àìsàn lè ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àwọn orí fífọ́ líle, ẹ̀fọ́jú, gìrì (wárápá bí wọ́n ba jẹ́ òòrèkóòrè), ọta orí, yínrùnyínrùn, ìgbàgbé àti àwọn àmì àìsàn tí àwọn ògòdò tí wọ́n gba àwọn ààyè ètò àkóso ibùdó ara ń fà. Ìtọ́jú: Ìtọ́jú taeníásí láti ọwọ́ Taenia solium ṣe pàtàkì láti dènà nirosisikakósì àti gẹ́gẹ́ bí irinṣẹ́ láti ṣàkóso tàbí dènà ìtànká ajọ̀fẹ́.
translations.reference: For cysticercosis, the incubation period is variable, and infected people may remain asymptomatic for years. In some endemic regions, infected people may develop visible or palpable nodules (a small solid bump or node which is solid that can be detected by touch) beneath the skin. Neurocysticercosis is associated with a variety of signs and symptoms depending on the number, size, stage and location of the pathological changes as well as the host’s immune response and the parasite’s genotype, but it can also be clinically asymptomatic. Symptoms may include chronic headaches, blindness, seizures (epilepsy if they are recurrent), hydrocephalus, meningitis, dementia and symptoms caused by lesions occupying spaces of the central nervous system. Treatment: The treatment of taeniasis by Taenia solium is important to prevent neurocysticercosis and as a tool to assist in controlling or stopping the parasite transmission cycle.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"For neurocysticercosis, the clinical presentation can vary, and those with the condition may not have any symptoms for many years. In areas where this disease is common, those who are ill may have small or large cysts (sometimes small cysts in the liver or small cysts in the muscles) that are visible in the eyes. Neurocysticercosis is associated with various lesions and patterns that depend on the number, location, stage, and viability of the parasitic cysts and the inflammatory response and the need for affection with it, but it can also have no symptoms of illness. Symptoms can include headaches, fever, dizziness (sometimes with seizures, if they are a symptom), neck stiffness, weakness, and symptoms that the cysts cause when they invade the body's control system. Diagnosis: Diagnosis is essential from the eggs of Taenia solium to determine neurocysticercosis and especially as a guide to control or eliminate the inflammatory response."

**Key Notes on the Translation:**

*   **Medical Terminology:** I've used standard English medical terminology to accurately convey the meaning.
*   **Nuance:** Yoruba can be very nuanced, and some phrases are difficult to translate directly. I've aimed for the most accurate and understandable English equivalent.
*   **Context:** The document appears to be about neurocysticercosis, a parasitic disease.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Ìtọ́jú lè jẹ́ ṣíṣe lára ẹni kọ̀ọ̀kan, tàbí Àkóso Òògùn gbogbogbò èyí tí ó dá lórí àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ abẹ́lé àti àwọn ìlànà ìṣàkóso tí wọ́n ń ṣàmúlò. Taeníásí lè jẹ́ títọ́jú nípa lílo àwọn òògùn agbógunti-ajọ̀fẹ́ (Praziquantel, Niclosamide, Albendazole). Nínú nirosisikakósì, níwọ̀n ìgbà tí bíba àwọn ògòdò líle jẹ́ lè fa èsì ìrẹ̀dòdò, ìtọ́jú pàtàkì àìsàn tí ó ń ṣọṣẹ́ nílò bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú àwọn iṣẹ́ ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́ pẹ̀lú àwọn ìlò òògùn agbógunti-ajọ̀fẹ́, àti ìwòsàn alátìlẹ́yìn pẹ̀lú corticosteroids àti/tàbí òògùn agbógunti-wárápá, àti bóyá iṣẹ́ abẹ. Ìlò àti àsìkò ìtọ́jú lè yàtọ̀ dáadáa bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè dá lórí iye, ìwọ̀n, agbègbè, àti ìpele ìdàgbàsókè oyún líle, ìrẹ̀dòdò àyíká ọṣẹ́, ìṣòro àti bí àwọn àmì àìsàn tàbí àmì ṣe lè tó.
translations.reference: The treatment can be done on an individual bases, or as Mass Drug Administration depending on the local circumstances and the control approaches being implemented. Taeniasis can be treated using anthelmintics (Praziquantel, Niclosamide, Albendazole). In neurocysticercosis, since the destruction of cysts may lead to an inflammatory response, specialised treatment of active disease is required and may include long courses with high doses of anthelmintic, as well as supporting therapy with corticosteroids and/or anti-epileptic drugs, and possibly surgery. The dosage and the duration of treatment can vary greatly and depend mainly on the number, size, location and developmental stage of the cysts, their surrounding inflammatory edema, acuteness and severity of clinical symptoms or signs.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba text into English:

"Schistosomiasis can be a consequence for each individual, or the state's overall health management system, which is based on the occurrence of schistosomiasis and control measures that are implemented. Treatment can be achieved through the use of anthelmintic drugs (Praziquantel, Niclosamide, Albendazole). In neuroschistosomiasis, when the eggs of the parasite can cause neurological damage, severe disease treatment that requires it may involve long-term care with anthelmintic drugs, and supportive treatment with corticosteroids and/or anti-inflammatory drugs, and possibly surgical intervention. The use and duration of treatment can vary greatly and may depend on the cost, weight, region, and severity of the parasite load, overall health status, symptoms, and how the disease or symptoms manifest."

**Key Notes on the Translation:**

*   **Schistosomiasis:** This is the disease being discussed, also known as bilharzia.
*   **Anthelmintic drugs:** These are drugs used to treat parasitic worm infections.
*   **Neuroschistosomiasis:** A severe form of schistosomiasis affecting the nervous system.
*   **Context:** The text is clearly a medical or public health document discussing the management and treatment of schistosomiasis.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì Ìsọnísókí: Tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì jẹ́ àìsàn líle tí ènìyàn máa ń kó láti ara ìfaragbá ọmọ àwọn bakitéríà, Clostridium tetani, èyí tí ó ń gbé nínú ilẹ̀, itọ́, eruku àti ìgbẹ́. Bakitéríà náà lè wọ inú ara nípasẹ̀ egbò ọgbẹ́ jínjìn, àwọn ìfarapa tàbí àpá iná tí wọ́n ń nípa lórí ètò àkóso ara. Àìsàn náà máa ń yọrí sí àwọn ìsúnkì isan ara, pàápàá jùlọ àgbọ̀n àti isan ọrùn, beet sì ni s mọ̀ ọ́n sí "ìwayínpọ̀". Gbogbo àwọn ènìyàn pátá lè ní tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ṣùgbọ́n àìsàn náà wọ́pọ̀ ó sì lè láàrin àwọn ọmọ tuntun àti àwọn ìyá wọn nígbà tí ìyá náà kò bá ní ààbò láti ara tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì nípasẹ̀ abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára, tetanus toxoid.
translations.reference: Tetanus Overview: Tetanus is a serious illness contracted through exposure to the spores of the bacterium, Clostridium tetani, which live in soil, saliva, dust and manure. The bacteria can enter the body through a deep cuts, wounds or burns affecting the nervous system. The infection leads to painful muscle contractions, particularly of the jaw and neck muscle, and is commonly known as “lockjaw”. People of all ages can get tetanus but the disease is particularly common and serious in newborn babies and their mothers when the mother is unprotected from tetanus by the vaccine, tetanus toxoid.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Yoruba document:

**Tetanus: The Disease of Clostridium Tetani**

Tetanus is an infectious disease that humans contract from the bacteria Clostridium tetani, which lives in the soil, dust, rust, and manure. The bacteria can enter the body through deep wounds, injections, or burns that disrupt the body's control system. The disease often leads to muscle spasms, most commonly in the jaw and neck, and it can be referred to as "lockjaw." All people are susceptible to tetanus, but the disease is more common among newborns and their mothers if the mother did not receive tetanus protection through vaccination, tetanus toxoid.

translations.source: Tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì tí ó ń farahàn lásìkò oyún tàbí láàrin ọ̀sẹ̀ 6 ní ìgbẹ̀yìn oyún náà ni a ń pè ní tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ìyálọ́mọ, nígbà tí tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì tí ó farahàn láàrin ọjọ́ 28 lẹ́yìn ìbí ni tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ìkókó. Àìsàn náà jẹ́ ìṣòro ìlera àwùjọ pàtàkì ní ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ẹ̀ka àgbáyé, ṣùgbọ́n paapapat jùlọ ní àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè elérè pínnísín tàbí àdúgbò, níbi tí abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára ti kéré tí àwọn àṣà ìbímọ aláìní ìmọ́tótó pọ̀ sí. Àjọ Elétò ìlera Àgbáyé ṣírò pé ní 2018 (ọdún lọ́ọ́lọ́ọ́ tí àwọn ìṣirò rẹ̀ wà nílẹ̀), àwọn ìkókó 25 000 kú láti ara tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ìkókó, àdínkù 88% sí ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ ọdún 2000. Àwọn àmì àìsàn: Ó fẹ́rẹ̀ẹ́ jẹ́ pé gbogbo ọ̀ràn tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì ń ṣẹlẹ̀ láàrin àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n kò tíì gba abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára tàbí àwọn àgbàlagbà tí wọn kò tíì ṣe ìmúdójú-ìwọ̀n àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àgbàkún wọn. Àwọn àpẹẹrẹ àti àmì àìsàn tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì máa farahàn lásìkò kí àsìkò láti ọjọ́ 3 sí 21 lẹ́yìn tí bakitéríà tẹ́tánọ́ọ̀sì bá ti wọ inú ara nípasẹ̀ egbò kan.
translations.reference: Tetanus occurring during pregnancy or within 6 weeks of the end of pregnancy is called maternal tetanus, while tetanus occurring within the first 28 days of life is called neonatal tetanus. The disease remains an important public health problem in many parts of the world, but especially in low-income countries or districts, where immunization coverage is low and unclean birth practices are common. WHO estimates that in 2018 (the latest year for which estimates are available), 25 000 newborns died from neonatal tetanus, 88% reduction from the situation in 2000. Symptoms: Nearly all cases of tetanus occur in people who have never been vaccinated or in adults who have not kept up to date on their booster shots. Signs and symptoms of tetanus appear anytime from 3 to 21 days after tetanus bacteria enter the body through a wound.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"The fever that occurs during the yellow fever season or within 6 months of vaccination is called yellow fever vaccine-induced disease, while the fever that occurs between 28 days and 12 weeks after birth is called post-vaccination fever. This disease is a significant public health problem in many parts of the world, but it is particularly prevalent in low-income countries or regions, where vaccination coverage is low and traditional birth practices are common. The World Health Organization reported that in 2018 (the year its data was available), 25,000 deaths occurred due to yellow fever vaccine-induced disease, an increase of 88% compared to the year 2000. Symptoms of the disease: It is generally observed that all cases of yellow fever occur in people who have not received vaccination or in elderly people who have not undergone booster shots of their vaccines. Examples and symptoms of yellow fever fever usually occur during the acute phase, from 3 to 21 days after the yellow fever bacteria has entered the body through a bite."

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   I've aimed for accuracy while maintaining readability in English.
*   Some terms like "ìmúdójú-ìwọ̀n àwọn abẹ́rẹ́ àgbàkún wọn" were translated to convey the meaning of booster shots.
*   I've tried to preserve the original structure and flow of the Yoruba text as much as possible.

translations.source: Ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn ọ̀ràn máa ń ṣẹlẹ̀ láàrin ọjọ́ 14. Àmì àìsàn lè jẹ́: ìwayínpọ tàbí àìlágbára láti yanu ẹ̀gbọ̀n àwọn iṣu ara pàápàá jùlọ lẹ́yìn, ní inú àti àwọn òpin ara. ìnira àwọn ẹ̀gbọ̀n ìṣù ara lójijì èyí tí ariwo òjìji máa ń fà
translations.reference: Most cases occur within 14 days. Symptoms can include: jaw cramping or the inability to open the mouth muscle spasms often in the back, abdomen and extremities sudden painful muscle spasms often triggered by sudden noises

translations.source: ìṣòro gbígbé nǹkan mi gìrì ẹ̀fọ́rí ibà àti òógùn lílà àyípadà nínú ìru ẹ̀jẹ̀ tàbí ìwọ̀n ọkàn tí ó ń sáré.
translations.reference: trouble swallowing seizures headache fever and sweating changes in blood pressure or fast heart rate.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba text into English:

"Household distress, my concern, the consequences of illness and medicine, complications arising within a type of discharge or mental instability that is spreading."

**Explanation of some of the terms and nuances:**
