translations.source: Yáàsì (Endemic treponematoses) Ìsọnísókí: Yáàsì jẹ́ àrùn awọ ara ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́ tí ó ní àbùdá ògòdò (ògòdò aláìní jẹjẹrẹ) àti àwọn egbò. Bakitéríà Treponema pallidum subspecies pertenue ni ó ń fà á, eyit tí ó wà ọ̀wọ́ kan náà pẹ̀lú bakitéríà tí ń fa àwọn sífílì ìbálòpọ̀. Yáàsì máa ń sáábà mú àwọn ọmọdé lábẹ́ ọjọ́ orí ọdún 15 tí wọ́n ń gbé ní àwọn ìlú ooru, ọlọ́pọ̀ omi nínú kùrukùru àti àwọn ìlú ijù ipa òòrùn agbègbè Áfríkà, Látìn Amẹ́ríkà àti àwọn erékùṣù Pàsífíìkì. Púpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn tí àìsàn náà ń ṣe ń gbé ní àwọn agbègbè ìgbèríko, tí wọ́n jìnnà sí ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera.
translations.reference: Yaws (Endemic treponematoses) Overview: Yaws is a chronic skin infection characterized by papillomas (noncancerous lumps) and ulcers. It is caused by the bacterium Treponema pallidum subspecies pertenue, which belongs to the same group of bacteria that causes venereal syphilis. Yaws primarily affects children aged under 15 years who live in poor communities in warm, humid and tropical forested areas of Africa, Asia, Latin America and the Pacific islands. The majority of affected populations live rural areas, far from health services.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Yoruba text:

**Yaws (Endemic Treponematoses) Explanation:** Yaws is a chronic skin disease with a predilection for the tropics (particularly tropical rainforests) and coastal areas. It is caused by the bacterium *Treponema pallidum* subspecies *pertenue*, which is closely related to the bacterium that causes syphilis. Yaws commonly affects children under the age of 15 living in warm, humid climates and impoverished communities in tropical regions of Africa, Latin America, and the Pacific Islands. Many of the people affected by this disease live in rural areas, which limits their access to healthcare services.

translations.source: Òsì, àwọn ipò àwùjọ-mọ́rọ̀-ajé àti ìmọ́tótó ara ẹni tí kò dára dákun ìtànká yáàsì. Bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé àwọn ọ̀ràn yáàsì 80 000 wà ní ọdọọdún, àwọn alámọ̀já gbàgbọ́ pé àìsàn náà lè ṣe é ṣàkóso kí ó sì di ìfòpinsí pátápátá nítorí onírúurú àwọn ìdí. Ní àkọ́kọ́, ó maat ń ṣe àwọn ẹ̀dá ènìyàn nìkan, kì í ṣe ẹranko, tí ó mú mi àkóso rẹ̀ rọrùn dáadáa. Ó tún rọrùn láti tọ́jú pẹ̀lú àwọn òògùn tí wọ́n wà nílẹ̀ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè kan, tí ó fi mọ́ India ti rẹ́yìn rẹ̀. Àwọn àlàfo àrùn yáàsì tí ó kù, bí ó tilẹ̀ jẹ́ pé ó wọ́pọ̀ ní àwọn ìgbèríko, túmọ̀ sí pé ìtànká kò ṣe é ṣe kó wáyé pẹ̀lú àmójútó tí ó yẹ àti àwọn òṣùwọ̀n ìṣàkóso.
translations.reference: Poverty, low socio-economic conditions and poor personal hygiene facilitate the spread of yaws. Although there are over 80 000 cases of yaws each year, experts believe the disease can be controlled and ultimately eradicated for several reasons. First, it only occurs in humans, not animals, making control much easier. It is also easy to treat with readily available drugs and has already been eliminated in some countries, including India. The remaining pockets of yaws infection, although usually in remote places, means further spread is less likely with proper surveillance and control measures.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Weakness, social and economic conditions, and poor self-care are factors contributing to tuberculosis. Although there are 80,000 cases of tuberculosis annually, experts believe the disease can be controlled and eradicated completely due to various reasons. Initially, it only affected humans, not animals, which made it easier to control. It is also easy to treat with drugs available locally, as seen in some countries, including India, which has made significant progress. The relatively low number of side effects from tuberculosis treatment, although common in some communities, suggests that the disease does not develop with the proper support and control measures."

translations.source: Àmì àìsàn: Ìpele méjì àrùn yáàsì ni ó wà:. Ìpele ìbẹ̀rẹ̀pẹ̀pẹ̀ ni nígbà tí aláìsàn bá kó àrùn náà tí ó sì lè kó àìsàn náà ran àwọn ẹlòmíràn. Ní ìpele yìí, ògòdò kan (kókó tí ó ń dàgbà kan, aláìní jẹjẹrẹ) yóò dàgbà níbi àrùn náà. Ògòdò náà kún fún àwọn ajọ̀fẹ́ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè wà níbẹ̀ fún oṣù 3-6 lẹ́yìn ìtọ́jú aláìtọ́ọ́dá. Láìsí ìtọ́jú, ohun tí yóò tẹ̀lé èyí ni ìtànká àwọn ogido káàkiri ara.
translations.reference: Symptoms: There are two stages of yaws infection. The early stage is when the patient is infectious and can spread the disease to others. In this stage, a papule (a noncancerous, outward-growing lump) develops at the site of infection. This papule is full of the organisms and may persist for 3–6 months followed by natural healing. Without treatment, this is followed by disseminated skin lesions over the body.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Disease Stage:** The disease, tuberculosis, is in two stages. The initial stage is when a patient contracts the disease and can then transmit it to others. In this stage, a single nodule (a growing, whitish lump) will appear in the area of the disease. This nodule is harmless and can remain there for 3-6 months after the initial infection. Without treatment, the next thing that will happen is the spread of nodules throughout the body.

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   I aimed for clarity and accuracy while maintaining a natural English flow.
*   "Àwọn ẹlòmíràn" is translated as "others" to reflect the meaning of transmitting the disease.
*   "Ìtọ́jú aláìtọ́ọ́dá" is translated as "initial infection" for better understanding.
*   "Ìtànká àwọn ogido káàkiri ara" is translated as "the spread of nodules throughout the body" to convey the progression of the disease.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Ìrora eegun àti kókó inú eegun lè wáyé. Ìpele kejì ni aláìní àrùn bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni yóò farahàn lẹ́yìn ọdún márùn-ún àrùn. Ó ní àbùdá sísọ àwọn ojúṣe imú, egungun àti fífa palma/plantar hyperkeratosis (ara yíyi). Lẹ́yìn àyẹ̀wò lẹ́nu ìtọ́jú dá lórí àwọn àmì àìsàn wọ̀nyí, aisant náà lè jẹ́ jíjẹ́rìí nípa ṣíṣe àyẹ̀wò àpẹẹrẹfáyẹ̀wò láti inú ògòdò awọ ara lábẹ́ oríṣìí máíkírósíkòòpù pàtó kan (àyẹ̀wò agbọn dúdú). Àwọn àyẹ̀wò ẹ̀jẹ̀ kì í sáábà jẹ́ lílò nítorí pé yáàsì tan pẹ̀lú bakiteria tí wọ́n ń fa sífílì bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni àwọn àìsàn méjèèjì náà yóò ṣàfihàn èsì kan náà.
translations.reference: Bone pain and bone lesions may also occur. The second stage is noninfectious and typically appears five years after infection. It is characterized by disabling consequences of the nose, bones and palmar/plantar hyperkeratosis (thickening). After a field diagnosis based on these symptoms, the disease can be confirmed by examining a sample from a skin legion under a special type of microscope (darkfield examination). Blood tests are not typically used because yaws is closely related to the bacterium that causes syphilis and the two diseases will show the same result.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba text into English:

"Skin lesions and internal skin growths can develop. The second stage of this disease typically appears after a year of the initial infection. It is characterized by the presence of nodules, warts, and palm/plantar hyperkeratosis (thickened skin). Following a thorough examination of these symptoms, a diagnosis can be confirmed by performing a microscopic examination of a sample taken from the affected skin under a specific microscope (a darkfield examination). Urine tests are not usually helpful because the disease is caused by bacteria that cause syphilis; both conditions will manifest the same symptoms."

**Explanation of some choices and nuances:**

*   **"Skin lesions and internal skin growths"**:  "Ìrora eegun àti kókó inú eegun" is a bit broad. I've tried to capture the meaning of both external and internal skin issues.
*   **"Nodules, warts, and palm/plantar hyperkeratosis"**: This is a direct translation of the listed symptoms.
*   **"Darkfield examination"**:  "Agbọn dúdú" refers to a darkfield microscopy technique.
*   **"Urine tests are not usually helpful"**: This conveys the meaning of "àwọn àyẹ̀wò ẹ̀jẹ̀ kì í sáábà jẹ́ lílò."
*   **"Both conditions will manifest the same symptoms"**: This clarifies the final sentence, explaining why urine tests are not helpful in distinguishing the diseases.



I hope this translation is helpful! Let me know if you have any other documents you'd like me to translate.

translations.source: Ìtọ́jú: Kò sí abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára fún ìtọ́jú yáàsì báyìí. Ẹ̀kọ́ ìlera àti ìdàgbàsókè ìmọ́tótó ara ẹni jẹ́ àwọn ọgbọ́n tí wọ́n ṣe pàtàkì jùlọ fún ìdènà. Òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò méjì lè ṣe é lò láti tọ́jú yáàsì. Àtẹnugbà ìlò azithromycin kan (30mg/kg, ó pọ̀ jù 2g) ni wọ́n fọwọ́ sí fún ìtọ́jú. Benzathine penicillin (ìlò àgbàsínú-isan kan) ní àwọn ìwọ̀n mílíọ̀nù 0.6 (àwọn ọmọdé lábẹ́ ọjọ́ orí 10) àti àwọn ìwọ̀n mílíọ̀nù 1.2 (fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ti ju ọdún 10 lọ) lè ṣe é lò fún àwọn aláìsàn pẹ̀lú afurasí ìtọ́jú tí ó kùnà lẹ́yìn azithromycin, tàbí àwọn aláìsàn tí wọ́n kò ṣe é tọ́jú pẹ̀lú azithromycin.
translations.reference: Treatment: There is currently no vaccine for yaws. Health education and improved personal hygiene are the most important tactics for prevention. Two antibiotics can be used to treat yaws. A single oral dose of azithromycin (30 mg/kg, maximum 2 g) is the recommended treatment. Benzathine penicillin (single intramuscular dose) at 0.6 million units (children aged under 10 years) and 1.2 million units (people aged over 10 years) can be used for patients with suspected clinical treatment failure after azithromycin, or patients who cannot be treated with azithromycin.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Yoruba document:

**Title: No alternative treatment for this fever.** Health education and accurate self-assessment are the most important factors for recovery. Two doses of paracetamol can be used to treat the fever. A single dose of azithromycin (30mg/kg, up to 2g) is administered for treatment. Benzathine penicillin (a single intramuscular injection) with 0.6 million units (for children under 10 years old) and 1.2 million units (for people over 10 years old) can be used for patients with persistent fever after azithromycin, or for patients who have not been treated with azithromycin.

translations.source: Ìtọ́jú yìí yoo yọrí sí 95% ìwòsàn pátápátá ṣùgbọ́n àwọn aláàárẹ̀ gbọ́dọ̀ jẹ́ ṣíṣe àtúnyẹ̀wò lẹ́yìn ọ̀sẹ̀ 4 tí wọ́n ti gba òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò. Àtúnyẹ̀wò àwọn ìwé ìtàn láti àwọn ọdún 1950 fi hàn pé ó lé ní orílẹ̀ èdè àti agbègbè 90 ni wọ́n ní àjàkálẹ̀ àrùn yáàsì. 14 péré nínú àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè àti agbègbè 90 ni wọ́n ní ìwífun-àlàyé abágbàmu lórí yáàsì dá lórí ètò àmójútó àgbékalẹ̀; àmọ́ sá, àwọn iye wọ̀nyí lè jẹ́ ìtọ́ka wíwà àìsàn náà tí kì í sì ṣe gbogbo wíwàa rẹ̀. Ghana, Papua New Guinea àti Solomon Islands jábọ̀ ó lé ní àwọn ọ̀ràn 10 000 lọ́dún. Ecuador àti India dà bí ẹni pé wọ́n ti rẹ́yìn ìkóràn.
translations.reference: This treatment will result in complete clinical healing in 95% of cases but patients should be re-examined 4 weeks after receiving the antibiotics. A review of the historic documents from 1950s shows that over 90 countries and territories were endemic for yaws. Only 14 out of the 90 countries and territories have recent data on yaws based on the routine surveillance system; however, these figures may just be an indication of the presence of the disease and not its full extent. Ghana, Papua New Guinea and the Solomon Islands report over 10 000 cases per year. Ecuador and India appear to have interrupted transmission.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"This treatment will lead to 95% recovery, but follow-up checks must be done after 4 weeks of taking the anti-malarial drug. Historical records from the 1950s show that it was prevalent in 90 countries and regions. 14 percent of those 90 countries and regions had extensive documentation on malaria based on community health programs; however, these figures could be indicators of the disease's prevalence and may not represent the entire picture. Ghana, Papua New Guinea, and the Solomon Islands reported over 10,000 cases annually. Ecuador and India appear to have contained the outbreak."

translations.source: Àìsàn ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀ Ìsọnísókí: Àìsàn ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀ jẹ́ àìsàn fáírọ́ọ̀sì tí ó máa ń ran ènìyàn nípasẹ̀ ẹnu ẹ̀fọn tí ó ti lùgbàdì. Ó ṣe é ṣe kí ó di àjàkálẹ̀ àrùn bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ṣe é dènà nípasẹ̀ abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára. Àwọn ẹ̀yà ẹ̀fọn ajẹni lóòjọ́ láyìíká ilé (abẹ́lé), nínú igbó tàbí ijù (aginjù), tàbí ní àwọn ibi méjèèjì (kòṣabẹ́lé-kòṣejù). Ibà apọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀ jẹ́ àìsàn onípa ńlá, tí ó léwu púpọ̀, pẹ̀lú ewu ìtànká káàkiri àgbáyé, èyí tí ó jẹ́ ewu fún ààbò ìlera àgbáyé.
translations.reference: Yellow fever Overview: Yellow fever is a viral disease that is transmitted to humans by the bites of infected mosquitoes. It is prone to epidemics and is preventable with a vaccine. These day-biting mosquitoes breed around houses (domestic), in forests or jungles (wild), or in both habitats (semi-domestic). Yellow fever is a high-impact, high-threat disease, with a risk of international spread, representing a potential threat to global health security.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Communicable Diseases of Public Health Concern: Communicable diseases are infectious diseases that spread through the mouth of an infected person. They can also be transmitted through respiratory droplets. The agents of the disease are found in the home (dwelling), in the forest or bush (wilderness), or in both places (neither home nor bush). Communicable diseases are a major public health problem, highly deadly, with a global pandemic risk, which poses a threat to global health.**

Here's a breakdown of some of the key terms and how they were translated:

*   **Àìsàn ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀:** Communicable diseases (literally, "disease that spreads widely")
*   **Ìsọnísókí:** Public Health Concern
*   **fáírọ́ọ̀sì:** Infectious
*   **ẹnu ẹ̀fọn:** Mouth of an infected person
*   **lùgbàdì:** Infected
*   **àjàkálẹ̀ àrùn:** Pandemic
*   **abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára:** Respiratory droplets
*   **ajẹni:** Agent (of the disease)
*   **ilé (abẹ́lé):** Home (dwelling)
*   **igbó tàbí ijù (aginjù):** Forest or bush (wilderness)
*   **kòṣabẹ́lé-kòṣejù:** Neither home nor bush
*   **onípa ńlá:** Major problem
*   **léwu púpọ̀:** Highly deadly
*   **ìtànká káàkiri àgbáyé:** Global pandemic
*   **ààbò ìlera àgbáyé:** Global health



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Ìpele ìkóràn 3 ló wà: Sylvatic (tàbí ijù) ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀ máa ń ràn nígbà tí àwọn ẹ̀fọn ijù bá jẹ àwọn ọ̀bọ tí wọ́n kó fáírọ́ọ̀sì náà ran àwọn ọ̀bọ àti ènìyàn mìíràn. Ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀ agbedeméjì máa ń tàn nígbà tí àwọn ẹ̀fọn kògbélé-kògbéjù bá kó àìsàn náà ran àwọn ọ̀bọ àti ènìyàn. Èyí ni oríṣìí ẹ̀yà ìbújáde tí ó wọ́pọ̀ ní Áfíríkà. Ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀ ìgboro lè fa àjàkálẹ̀ àrùn ńlá nígbà tí àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n bá lùgbàdì àrùn náà bá kó fáírọ́ọ̀sì náà ran àwọn àgbègbè tí àwọn ènìyàn pọ̀ sí pẹ̀lú ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ẹ̀fọn àti níbi tí àjẹsára àwọn ènìyàn kò ti tó nǹkan. Ní ìgbà mìíràn, àwọn arìnrìn-àjò tí wọ́n ti lùgbàdì àìsàn náà tí kó àwọn ọ̀ràn wọ àwọn orílẹ̀ èdè tí wọ́n kò ti ní ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀.
translations.reference: There are 3 types of transmission cycles: Sylvatic (or jungle) yellow fever spreads when monkeys are bitten by wild mosquitoes that pass the virus on to other monkeys and humans. Intermediate yellow fever spreads when semi-domestic mosquitoes infect both monkeys and people. This is the most common type of outbreak in Africa. Urban yellow fever can cause large epidemics when infected people introduce the virus into heavily populated areas with high mosquito density and where people have little immunity. Occasionally, infected travellers have exported cases to countries that are free of yellow fever.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba text into English:

Level 3 epidemic: Sylvatic (or jungle) fever often occurs when jungle mosquitoes transmit the disease to livestock that have been infected. Double-epidemic fever often spreads when biting midges transmit the disease to livestock and humans. This is the pattern of epidemic that is common in Africa. Urban epidemic can cause major outbreaks when people who have contracted the disease transmit it to areas with a high population of mosquitoes and where human immunity is low. At other times, travelers who have contracted the disease carry it to countries that do not have jungle fever.

translations.source: Àmọ́ sá, àìsàn yìí lè tàn ká ní ìrọ̀rùn wọ orílẹ̀ ede tuntun bí àwọn oríṣìí ẹ̀fọn tí wọ́n lè ṣe ìtànká rẹ̀, ipò ojú ọjọ́ pàtó, àti ẹranko ajọ̀fẹ́ tí ó nílò láti gbé e ka bá wà nìkan. Àmì àìsàn: Ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀ lè ní àwọn onírúurú àmì àìsàn àti ọwọ́ líle onírúurú. Ní kété tí ènìyàn bá ti kó o, fáírọ́ọ̀sì ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀ yóò sàba nínú ara fún ọjọ́ 3-6. Ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ènìyàn kì í ní ìrírí àmì àìsàn, ṣùgbọ́n nígbà tí àwọn wọ̀nyí bá ṣẹlẹ̀, àwọn tí ó wọ́pọ̀ jùlọ ni ibà, isan ríro pẹ̀lú ẹ̀yìn dídùn, orí fífọ́, àìlèjẹun, àti àyà rínrìn tàbí èébì. Fún ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ọ̀ràn, àmì àìsàn máa ń pòórá lẹ́yìn bí ọjọ́ 3-4.
translations.reference: However, the disease can only spread easily to a new country if there are mosquito species able to transmit it, specific climatic conditions, and the animal reservoir needed to maintain it. Symptoms: Yellow fever can present with a wide range of symptoms and severity. Once contracted, the yellow fever virus incubates in the body for 3–6 days. Many people do not experience symptoms, but when these do occur, the most common are fever, muscle pain with prominent backache, headache, loss of appetite, and nausea or vomiting. In most cases, symptoms disappear after 3–4 days.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"The child is sick, this illness can resolve easily in a new language like various types of fever that can be treated, specific eye conditions, and a loving pet that needs to be taken care of alone. Symptoms: A high fever can have various symptoms and affect different parts of the body. After a person contracts it, the symptoms of a high fever will subside within the body for 3-6 days. Many people do not have any symptoms, but when they do occur, the most common are fever, chills with sweating, headache, fatigue, and severe pain or dizziness. For many cases, the symptoms of the illness will improve after about 3-4 days."

translations.source: Ìdá kékeré àwọn aláàárẹ̀ máa ń wọnú ìpele kejì, tí ó lè gan-an láàrin wákàtí 24 tí wọ́n bá ti padà bọ̀ sípò láti ara àwọn àmì àìsàn ìsáájú. Ibà líle yóò padà bẹ́ẹ̀ sit ni yóò nípa lórí àwọn onírúurú ètò ara, lọ́pọ̀ọ̀gbà ẹ̀dọ̀ àti kíndìnrín. Ní ìpele yìí, ó ṣe é ṣe kí àwọn ènìyàn ní ibà pọ́njúpọ́nra (ara àti ojú pípọ́n, nítorí náà ni orúkọ rẹ̀ ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀), ìtọ̀ pípọ́n, àti inú rírun pẹ̀lú èébì. Ẹ̀jẹ̀ lè sẹ́ láti ẹnu, imú, ojú, tàbí ikùn. Ìdajì àwọn aláàárẹ̀ wọ̀nyí ń kú láàrin ọjọ́ 7-10, ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀ ṣòro láti ṣàyẹ̀wò, pàápàá jùlọ ní àwọn ìpele ìbẹ̀rẹ̀pẹ̀pẹ̀.
translations.reference: A small percentage of patients enter a second, more toxic phase within 24 hours of recovering from initial symptoms. High fever returns and several body systems are affected, usually the liver and kidneys. In this phase, people are likely to develop jaundice (yellowing of the skin and eyes, hence the name yellow fever), dark urine, and abdominal pain with vomiting. Bleeding can occur from the mouth, nose, eyes, or stomach. Half of these patients die within 7–10 days.Yellow fever is difficult to diagnose, especially during the early stages.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Minor ailments often progress to a second stage, which can occur within 24 hours after appearing as initial symptoms of illness. The condition will manifest on various body systems, including the skin, mucous membranes, and internal organs. In this stage, it is characterized by a widespread rash (affecting the skin and eyes, hence the name widespread rash), fever, and internal burning with chills. Discharge may come from the nose, mouth, eyes, or rectum. These minor ailments typically result in death within 7-10 days, and widespread rash is difficult to diagnose, especially in the early stages."

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   **"Ìdá kékeré àwọn aláàárẹ̀"**: This is translated as "minor ailments." The original phrase refers to small or less severe illnesses.
*   **"ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀"**: This is a key phrase and is translated as "widespread rash." It's a descriptive term for the rash's appearance.
*   **Context is Important:** Without more context, it's difficult to be absolutely certain about the precise meaning of some terms. This translation aims to be as accurate as possible based on the provided text.
*   **Medical Terminology:** I've used common English medical terms where appropriate.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Àwọn ọ̀ràn líle púpọ̀ lè rújú ní ìdámọ̀ pẹ̀lú maléríà líle, lẹ́pitosipirósì, àrùn fáírọ́ọ̀sì jẹ̀dọ̀jẹ̀dọ̀ (pàápàá jùlọ àwọn ẹ̀yà òjìji), àwọn ibà ajẹmẹ́jẹ́ mìíràn, àrùn pẹ̀lú àwọn fáírọ́ọ̀sì ẹbí Flaviviridae mìíràn (bí ibà oríike inú ẹ̀jẹ̀) àti májèlé jíjẹ. Ẹ̀wọ́n Ìṣesí Ploymerase (EIS) àyẹ̀wò nínú ẹ̀jẹ̀ àti ìtọ̀ lè ṣe ìdámọ̀ fáírọ́ọ̀sì náà ní àwọn ìpele ìbẹ̀rẹ̀pẹ̀pẹ̀ àìsàn náà. Ní àwọn ìpele ìgbẹ̀yìn, àyẹ̀wò láti ṣe ìdámọ̀ àwọn ẹ̀sọ́ ètò ara ṣe pàtàkì (IgM). Ìtọ́jú: Àrùn ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀ lè fa àìsàn líle bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó lè gbẹ̀mí ní ti àwọn ọ̀ràn líle. Àtìlẹ́yìn ìtọ́jú kíákíá ní àwọn ilé ìwòsàn ń mú ìlọsíwájú bá ìwọ̀n yíyè.
translations.reference: More severe cases can be confused with severe malaria, leptospirosis, viral hepatitis (especially fulminant forms), other haemorrhagic fevers, infection with other flaviviruses (such as dengue haemorrhagic fever) and poisoning. Polymerase chain reaction (PCR) testing in blood and urine can sometimes detect the virus in early stages of the disease. In later stages, testing to identify antibodies is needed (IgM). Treatment: Yellow fever infections can cause serious illness and can be fatal in severe cases. Early supportive treatment in hospitals improves survival rates.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Many viral infections can manifest with malaria-like symptoms, severe jaundice, viral hepatitis (especially in children), other hemorrhagic conditions, diseases with other Flaviviridae viruses (such as congenital infections), and severe complications. Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) testing in blood and tissue can detect the virus at the early stages of the disease. In the recovery phase, testing to detect antibodies (IgM) is crucial. Conclusion: Acute viral infections can cause fever and can be confused with viral infections. Strong diagnostic evidence in hospitals is advancing this knowledge."

**Notes on the translation:**

*   I aimed for accuracy while maintaining readability in English.
*   Some terms (like "EIS" - Enzyme Immunoassay) are kept as they are commonly used in medical contexts.
*   The phrasing has been adjusted to flow naturally in English.
*   "Ìtọ́jú" is translated as "Conclusion" as it seems to be the intended meaning in this context.

translations.source: Kò sí òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò fáírọ́ọ̀sì kankan pàtó báyìí fún ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀, ṣùgbọ́n ìtọ́jú àyàsọ́tọ̀ láti tọ́jú omi ara gbígbẹ, ibà, àti ìkùnà ẹ̀dọ̀ àti kíndìnrín ń ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún àwọn àbájáde. Àwọn àrùn bakitéríà tí wọ́n ní í ṣe pẹ̀lú rẹ̀ lè ṣe é tọ́jú pẹ̀lú òògùn agbógunti-kòkòrò bakitéríà. Aláàárẹ̀ ní latit dúró lábẹ́ àpò ẹ̀fọn lásìkò ojú mọmọ láti ṣe àdínkù ewu ṣíṣe ìtànká sí àwọn mìíràn nípa àwọn ẹnu ẹ̀fọn. Abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára nìkan ni òṣùwọ̀n pàtàkì jùlọ fún ìdènà ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀. Ìdènà àwọn ìbújáde lè jẹ́ àṣeyọrí bí ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn bá ní àjẹsára.
translations.reference: There is currently no specific anti-viral drug for yellow fever, but specific care to treat dehydration, fever, and liver and kidney failure improves outcomes. Associated bacterial infections can be treated with antibiotics. Patients need to stay under mosquito nets during the day to limit the risk of spreading to others through mosquito bites. Vaccination is the single most important measure for preventing yellow fever. The prevention of outbreaks can only be achieved if the majority of the population is immunized.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"There is currently no specific drug to treat malaria, but remedies derived from water, herbs, and traditional practices for fever and chills can be helpful for relief. Bacterial infections that accompany it can be treated with antibacterial drugs. It is important to remain under mosquito nets for at least one hour to reduce the risk of spreading malaria to others through mosquito bites. Artemisinin is the most important drug for the treatment of malaria. The success of treatment can be high if many people have access to artemisinin."

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   **Cultural Context:** The text references "traditional practices" which is a common approach in many cultures, including Yoruba culture, for managing illness.
*   **Artemisinin:** This is a key ingredient in many modern malaria treatments, and the text correctly highlights its importance.
*   **Accuracy:** I've aimed for accuracy while maintaining readability in English.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀ dára kò sì wọ́n, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ìlò ẹ̀ẹ̀kan péré yóò pèsè àjẹsára títí ayé fún àìsàn náà. Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n gba ìwòsàn láti ara ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀ náà ti ní ààbò títí ayé. Kà sí i nípa abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀. Àkóso ẹ̀fọn náà lè ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ láti dènà ibà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀ bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni ó ṣe pàtàkì ní àwọn ìgbà tí ìpèsè abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára bá kéré tàbí abẹ́rẹ́ àjẹsára kò tètè wà nílẹ̀. Lára àwọn àkóso ẹ̀fọn ni mímú àwọn ibi tí àwọn ẹ̀fọn ti lè bímọ kúrò àti pípa àwọn àgbà ẹ̀fọn àti ọ̀dọ́ wọn nípa lílo àwọn òògùn apakòkòrò ní àwọn àgbègbè tí ẹ̀fọn pọ̀ sí.
translations.reference: The yellow fever vaccine is safe and affordable, and a single dose provides life-long immunity against the disease. People who recover from yellow fever infection are also protected for life. Read more about the yellow fever vaccine. Mosquito control can also help to prevent yellow fever and is vital in situations where vaccination coverage is low or the vaccine is not immediately available. Mosquito control includes eliminating sites where mosquitoes can breed and killing adult mosquitoes and larvae by using insecticides in areas with high mosquito density.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"The initial symptoms of cerebrospinal meningitis are not always obvious, and consequently, a single case of illness can spread the meningitis throughout the entire community. People who contract the disease from cerebrospinal meningitis suffer from it for their entire lives. Let's talk about the initial symptoms of cerebrospinal meningitis. Control measures can help prevent the spread of cerebrospinal meningitis and are especially crucial when the initial symptoms of meningitis are mild or when the initial symptoms of meningitis are not clearly present. These control measures include identifying areas where mosquitoes can breed and educating elders and young people about the use of mosquito repellents in areas where mosquitoes are prevalent."

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   **Cerebrospinal Meningitis:** I've consistently used this term as it's the standard English medical term for "ìbà pọ́njúpọ́ntọ̀."
*   **Context:** The document appears to be discussing the prevention and awareness of cerebrospinal meningitis, particularly focusing on mosquito control as a preventative measure.
*   **Nuance:** I've tried to capture the tone and meaning of the Yoruba text as accurately as possible, while ensuring the English translation is clear and understandable.

translations.source: Ìdásí àwùjọ nípa àwọn iṣẹ́, bí nínu àwọn kòtò ilé àti bíbo àwọn koto omi níbi tí àwọn ẹ̀fọn lè dàgbà sí jẹ́ ọ̀nà pàtàkì tí ó sì múnádóko láti ṣàkóso àwọn ẹ̀fọn.
translations.reference: Community involvement through activities, such as cleaning household drains and covering water containers where mosquitoes can breed is an important and effective way to control mosquitoes.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba text into English:

"Community efforts regarding work, both within homes and around water bodies, are a crucial and effective way to control pests."



**Explanation of some of the choices made in the translation:**

*   **Ìdásí àwùjọ:** "Community efforts" - This captures the sense of collective action.
*   **àwọn iṣẹ́:** "regarding work" - This refers to activities or tasks.
*   **bí nínu àwọn kòtò ilé:** "both within homes" -  "kòtò ilé" literally means "home compounds" but "within homes" conveys the meaning well.
*   **bíbo àwọn koto omi:** "and around water bodies" - "koto omi" refers to bodies of water.
*   **àwọn ẹ̀fọn:** "pests" - This is the standard translation for this word.
*   **ọ̀nà pàtàkì:** "a crucial way" -  Emphasizes the importance.
*   **ó sì múnádóko láti ṣàkóso:** "and effective to control" -  Highlights the practicality and success of the approach.

translations.source: Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n nílò ìtọ́jú ni wọ́n "fi sílẹ̀ nínú ìnira" ìjábọ̀ tuntun ló gbé e jáde Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Lágbàyé (WHO) ti ṣe àtẹ̀jáde ìjábọ̀ tuntun lórí ànfààní sí mọ́fínì fún lílò nínú ìṣègùn, tí ó ń ṣàpèjúwe bí pínpín mọ́fínì káàkiri àgbáyé, gẹ́gẹ́ bí oògùn ara ríro tó ṣe pàtàkì, ò ṣe dọ́gba tí kò sì pé ìlò rẹ̀ nínú ìṣègùn. Ìjábọ̀ náà, tí àkòrí rẹ̀ jẹ́ "Left behind in pain", ṣàfihàn àwọn ìṣòro nínú níní ànfààní sí oògùn tó ṣe pàtàkì yìí tí ó sì tún pèsè iṣẹ́ láti mú àyípadà bá níní ànfààní aláìléwu nípasẹ̀ òfin tó dọ́gba. Pẹ̀lu bí mọ́fínì ṣe jẹ́ oògùn tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ tí owó rẹ̀ sì ṣe bí ẹni kéré fún ìtura ìrora tó lágbára, tí wọ́n gbé jáde láti ọdún 1977 nínú àtúnṣe àkọ́kọ́ àkọsílẹ Àwòṣe WHO fún Àwọn Oògùn Pàtàkì, ìyàtọ̀ tó wà nínú ànfààní káàkiri àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè lágbára. Ìyàtọ̀ bí ìlọ́po 5- sí 63- ni àpapọ̀ agbede-méjì ìlò mọ́fínì tó wà láàrin àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí wọ́n ìpawówọlé wọn pọ̀ àti àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ìpawówọlé wọn kéré.
translations.reference: People with medical needs are “left behind in pain” reveals new report The World Health Organization (WHO) has published a new report on access to morphine for medical use, which describes how the global distribution of morphine, as a vital pain medicine, is unequal and does not fulfil the medical need. The report, titled “Left behind in pain”, highlights the problems in access to this essential medicine and offers actions to improve safe access through balanced policy. Despite morphine being an effective and relatively low-cost medicine for relieving strong pain, listed since 1977 in the first edition of the WHO Model list of Essential Medicines, the disparity in access across countries is stark. There is a 5- to 63-fold difference in the estimated median consumption of morphine between high-income countries and lower income countries.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"People in need of analgesics are 'left behind in pain'," a new report from the World Health Organization (WHO) has revealed. The WHO has released a new report on access to pain relief for use in treatment, detailing how pain relief is distributed worldwide, particularly essential medicines, and their limited use in treatment. The report, titled "Left behind in pain," highlights the challenges in accessing essential medicines and also calls for action to improve safe access to pain relief through effective policies. With pain relief being a life-saving medicine and its cost being relatively small for treating severe pain, it was brought out from the first revision of the WHO’s List of Essential Medicines in 1977, highlighting the disparities in access across countries. Differences such as 5- to 63-fold differences in the average two-dose use of pain relief existed between high-income countries and low-income countries.

translations.source: Bátánì lílo yátọ̀ gidi gan-an káàkiri àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ọrọ̀ wọn dọ́gba; Kò dọ́gba pẹ̀lú ohun tí ìṣègùn nílò, gẹ́gẹ́ bí àpapọ iye ònkà ọjọ́ tí àwọn ènìyàn fi wà nínú ìrora tàbí tí wọ́n kérora pẹ̀lú àìlè mí dáadáa tó le fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ní àìlera àtìgbàdégbà ṣe fi hàn. Dátà náà tẹnumọ́ ti 2018 Lancet Commission tí ó ṣàpèjúwe àìsí ànfààní sí àwọn oògùn ara ríro gẹ́gẹ́ bí 'ọ̀kan nínú àwọn àìdọ́gba burúkú, tó farasin nínú ìlera àgbáyé' pẹ̀lú bí 10% àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tó lówó jùlọ ṣe ní 90% àwọn ópọ́ídì tó dọ́gba pẹ̀lú mọ́fínì. "Tí ó fi àwọn ènìyàn sílẹ̀ nínú ìnira nígbà tí àwọn oògùn tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ wà fún ìtọ́jú ìrora, pàápàá jùlọ ní ọ̀gangan ipò ìtọ́jú òpin-ìṣẹ̀mí, ní láti jẹ́ nǹkan àpérò fún àwọn aṣòfin," Dr Yukiko Nakatani, Igbákejì Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ fún Àwọn Èlò Oògùn àti Ìlera WHO ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "A gbọ́dọ̀ tètè jà fún ànfààní tí ò léwu tó sì ṣe déédé àsìkò fún mọ́fínì fún àwọn tí wọ́n nílò rẹ̀ fún ìlera nípasẹ òfin tó dọ́gba, níbi gbogbo."
translations.reference: Consumption pattern varies significantly across countries of similar wealth; it does not correspond to medical need, as indicated by the estimated number of days people are in pain or suffer with severe shortness of breath for people with a terminal illness. This data echoes that of the 2018 Lancet Commission which described the lack of access to pain relief medication as ‘one of the most heinous, hidden inequities in global health’ with the richest 10% of countries possessing 90% of distributed morphine-equivalent opioids. “Leaving people in pain when effective medicines are available for pain management, especially in the context of end-of-life care, should be a cause of serious concern for policy-makers,” says Dr Yukiko Nakatani, WHO Assistant Director-General for Medicines and Health Products. “We must urgently advocate for safe and timely access to morphine for those in medical need through balanced policy, everywhere.”
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"The use of genuine, distinct solutions across countries whose languages are marginalized is lacking. These solutions are not available with the necessary resources, especially regarding the high cost of essential medicines that people need when experiencing pain or suffering due to poor access to care, particularly for vulnerable and elderly populations. This is supported by the 2018 Lancet Commission, which described the lack of access to essential medicines as 'one of the most egregious injustices, spreading throughout global health,' with 10% of countries that spend the most accounting for 90% of the deaths due to lack of access to morphine. "This keeps people in unnecessary suffering when effective medicines are available for pain relief, especially in palliative and end-of-life care, and it should be a priority for policymakers," said Dr. Yukiko Nakatani, Deputy Director-General for Access to Medicines and Health at WHO. "We must advocate for access to safe and effective pain relief and morphine for those who need it for pain management through regulated access, everywhere."

translations.source: Àwọn èrèdí fún ìyàtọ̀ náà ànfààní sí mọ́fínì fún lílò nínú ìṣègùn ni oríṣiríṣi nǹkan tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ pọ̀ ṣe òkùnfà rẹ̀ tó fi mọ́ àwọn aṣokùnfa tó jẹ mọ́ ìṣèjọba tó dára, àwọn ìlànà fún rírà àti pínpín kiri tó ṣe é gbáralé/ṣiṣẹ́, àwọn ohun èlò tó wà àti àwọn iṣẹ́ ìdagbàsókè ilé-iṣẹ́, tó fi mọ́ àwọn ìdènà tó tan mọ́ àwọn òfin àti ìlànà-iṣẹ́ aṣèdíwọ́ lápapọ̀, ìpèsè iṣẹ́ tí ò tó. Pẹ̀lú wíwo ọ̀gangan ipò oríṣiríṣi orílẹ̀-ède, àwọn aṣokùnfa, àwọn ìdènà àti kàn-án-pá fún iṣẹ́ yàtọ̀; ìwádìí alẹ́nulọ́rọ̀ kan tí wọ́n gbékalẹ̀ nínú ìjábọ̀ yìí, pẹ̀lú àwọn olùjábọ̀ láti 105 Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ WHO, fi ìdí ìyàtọ̀ yìí múlẹ̀. Fún àpẹẹrẹ, àìsí déédé pínpín mọ́fínì àti àwọn ópọ́ídì alágbára mìíràn ní àwọn ilé-ìwòsàn nítorí àìtó owó ni wọ́n ṣe àkíyèsí rẹ̀ gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìdènà ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ìpawówọlé wọn kéré àti àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ìpawówọlé wọn pọ̀. Ó lé ní ìdá mẹ́ta àwọn olùjábọ̀ káàkiri gbogbo ẹkùn agbègbè tí wọ́n tún ṣe àkíyèsí àwọn ìdènà tó ń wáyé látara àwọn aṣokùnfa òfin àti ìlànà-iṣẹ́ nígbà tí wọ́n tún wo pàtàkì ìlànà òfin àti ìlànà-iṣẹ́ nínú ṣíṣe àṣeyọrí ànfààní aláìléwu.
translations.reference: Reasons for the disparity Access to morphine for medical use is influenced by many interacting factors including enablers related to good governance, reliable/efficient procurement and supply processes, resource availability and capacity-building activities, along with barriers related to overly restrictive legislation and policies, inadequate service provision, and misinformed attitudes and perception. Given different country contexts, enablers, barriers and priorities for action vary; a stakeholder survey presented in this report, with respondents from 105 WHO Member States, confirms this variation. For example, irregular supply of morphine and other strong opioids at health facilities due to limited financing were commonly noted as a barrier in low- and middle-income countries compared to high-income countries. More than a third of respondents across all regions also noted barriers arising from legislative and regulatory factors while recognizing the importance of a legislative and regulatory framework in achieving safe access.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English, aiming for accuracy and clarity:

"The challenges for addressing disparities are increasingly focused on utilization within the health system, encompassing various factors that significantly impact its effectiveness, including its cost and the influence of stakeholders who are knowledgeable about good governance, policies for implementation and continuous improvement, available resources, and the functions of healthcare facilities, as well as the regulations and established work procedures, and the limited scope of services. With a review of various country contexts, stakeholders, regulations, and best practices for addressing disparities; this comprehensive report, including contributors from 105 WHO Member States, initiated this disparity. For example, the lack of adequate funding and other significant challenges in healthcare facilities due to financial constraints are particularly highlighted as a key issue in low-income and high-income countries. Three rounds of consultations involving stakeholders across all regions also considered the challenges arising from legal and operational frameworks, while also examining the importance of policy and operational frameworks in ensuring safe and effective implementation. "

**Key Notes on the Translation:**

*   **Complex Sentence Structure:** Yoruba often uses long, complex sentences. I've broken these down into more manageable English sentences for better readability.
*   **Cultural Context:** Some phrases require careful interpretation to convey the intended meaning accurately in English.
*   **Terminology:** I've used standard English healthcare terminology where appropriate.
*   **"Ànfààní"**: This word appears frequently and refers to "addressing" or "tackling" a problem.
*   **"Ìjábọ̀"**: This translates to "report."



I hope this translation is helpful! Let me know if you have any other documents you'd like me to translate.

translations.source: Àwọn ìbéèrè ìdènà tí ò yẹ lè ṣe ìdènà fún ànfààní àwọn aláìsàn nítorí wọ́n máa ń ṣe ìdíwọ́ fún pínpín tàbí jẹ́ kí ìjúwe lílo oògùn àti fífún ni lóògùn nira fún àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ ètò-ìlera. Ó ṣe pàtàkì láti mọ̀ pé ìrònú nípa àwọn ipa ewu ópọ́ídì tó ṣeéṣe jẹ́ tòótọ́, gẹ́gẹ́ bíi agbára wọn láti yọrí sí àìsàn àṣìlo ópọ́ídì. Fún ìdí èyí, òdiwọn ìṣọ́ra díẹ̀ nípa àwọn ewu lílo ópọ́ídì tó ṣeéṣe (b.a lílò fún ìrora aláìṣe-jẹjẹrẹ tó lágbára) ṣe pàtàkì fún ìlera àwùjọ lódiwọ̀n ìgbà tí lílo rẹ̀ bá ti di mímọ̀ dáadáa tó sì jẹ́ ìwọ̀nba fún ewu. Irú ìrònú bẹ́ẹ̀ ò gbọdọ̀ fojú réna àwọn ànfààní lílo ópọ́ídì nígbà tí ó bá ti fojú hàn nínú ìṣègùn àti tí àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ bá lò ó láìséwu. Àwọn ànfààní fún iṣẹ́ Ìjábọ̀ náà ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ètò aṣèrànwọ́ fún àwọn agbègbè fún iṣẹ́ tí ó ń gbèrò láti mú àyípadà bá anfààní aláìléwu sí mọ́fínì.
translations.reference: Unduly restrictive requirements could hamper patient access because they impede the flow of supply or make prescribing and dispensing difficult for health-care professionals. It is important to note that concerns about the potential harmful effects of opioids are valid, such as their potential to lead to opioid use disorder. For this reason, a certain amount of caution about the potential harms of opioid use (e.g. use in chronic non-cancer pain) is important for public health insofar as its use is well-informed and proportionate to risks. Such concerns should not undermine the benefits of opioid use when clinically indicated and when used safely by trained professionals. Opportunities for actionThe report presents a complementary set of areas for action that aim to improve safe access to morphine.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Questions regarding prescriptions that should be made for patients due to the fact that they often prescribe broadly or may require the use of opioids and providing them with strong opioids are a challenge for healthcare professionals. It is important to know that concerns about the dangers of opioid misuse are accurate, especially regarding their ability to lead to opioid addiction. For this reason, careful consideration of the risks of using opioids that are difficult to manage (e.g., using them for non-cancer pain that is severe) is important for public health, provided their use is appropriate and safe. Such concerns should not prevent patients from using opioids when they are clearly indicated in treatment and when healthcare professionals use them safely. Initiatives for the Wellbeing Service establish a support program for areas with high demand to bring the benefits of safe opioid use to a minimum."

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   **Context is Key:** I've tried to maintain the nuance of the original Yoruba, assuming the context is related to opioid prescription and patient safety within a healthcare system.
*   **Complex Sentence Structure:** Yoruba often has complex sentence structures that are translated into more straightforward English for clarity.
*   **Terminology:** I've used standard English medical terminology where appropriate.
*   **"Ìjábọ̀"**: This word is tricky. It refers to a wellbeing or support service. I've interpreted it in the context of the document as a "Wellbeing Service" or "Support Program."



I hope this translation is helpful! Let me know if you have any other documents you'd like me to translate.

translations.source: Lára rẹ̀ lati rí ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn ètò agbègbè oníwọ̀n-kékeré tàbí jákèjádò-ìpínlẹ̀ lórí mímú àyípadà bá ànfààní sí mọ́fínì fún lílò ìṣègùn pẹ̀lú àpapọ àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ tó ṣe pàtàkì àti àwọn ohun èlò fún ìtọ́jú ìtura tí wọ́n ṣe ní ìbámu pẹ̀lú Àkọsílẹ Àwòṣe Àwọn Oògùn Pàtàkì WHO àti Àwọn Àkójọpọ̀ Ìtọ́jú Ìtura WHO tó ṣe Pàtàkì. Lára rẹ̀ la tún ti rí mímú àyípadà bá ìṣèjọba, ìsọdìrọ̀rùn ìlànà rírà àti pínpín láti kojú èyíkéyìí àìṣedéédé, mímú àyípadà bá wíwá ohun èlò bíi owó ìrànwọ́ tó dúróore, ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ìmọ̀ọ́ṣe àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera tó kájú òṣùwọ̀n, àti ṣíṣe ìkéde nípa àwọn ànfààní àti ewu tó ṣeéṣe látara lílo ópọ́ídì. Àṣeyọrí àwọn iṣẹ́ wọ̀nyìí máa gbára lé àjọṣepọ̀ àti ìfọwọ́sowọ́pọ̀ láàrín gbogbo àwọn alẹ́nulọ́rọ̀ ní ìpele orílẹ̀-èdè, ẹkùn agbègbè, àti lágbàyé.
translations.reference: These include implementing small-scale regional or state-wide programmes on improving access to morphine for medical use with a package of essential services and products for palliative care that is formulated according to the WHO Model List of Essential Medicines and the WHO Essential Package of Palliative Care. They also include improving governance, streamlining procurement and supply processes to address any inefficiency, improving resourcing such as stable funding, enhancing competent skill sets of the health workforce, and raising awareness about both the benefits and potential harms of opioid use. The success of these actions will hinge on collaboration and cooperation among all stakeholders at national, regional, and global levels.

translations.source: Àwọn Erékùṣù Kékeré gba láti gbé ìgbésẹ̀ akin lórí àwọn ààrùn tí kìí ràn àti ìlera ọpọlọ Lónì, Ìkéde Bridgetown lórí Àwọn Ààrùn tí kìí ràn (NCDs) àti Ìlera Ọpọlọ ni wọ́n ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ rẹ̀, láti kojú àwọn ààrùn aṣekúpani jùlọ lágbàyé ní Àwọn Ìpínlẹ̀ Onídágbàsókè Erékùṣù Kékeré (SIDS), tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu jùlọ. Ìkéde náà jẹ́ àbájáde pàtàkì fún Àpérò Mínísítà SIDS lórí Àwọn Ààrùn tí kìí ràn (NCDs) àti Ìlera Ọpọlọ, tó bẹ́rẹ̀ láti 14 sí 16 oṣù Òkudù, tí Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Lágbàyé (WHO) jọ ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ rẹ̀, Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Ajẹmọ́ Ilẹ̀ Amẹ́ríkà (PAHO) àti Ìjọba orílẹ̀-ède Barbados. NCDs ń kóbá SIDS lọ́pọ̀lọpọ̀ - tí ó ń ṣokùnfà 74% gbogbo ikú lágbàyé - nítorí ìgbẹ́kẹ̀lé wọn lórí àwọn oúnjẹ tí wọ́n ń kó wọlé, àwọn ipa okòwò àti rògbòdìyàn ojú ọjọ́. Ìjábọ̀ WHO tuntun fi hàn pé 8 nínú àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 15 tí wọ́n ní ju 30% ewu ikú àìtọ́jọ́ látara ààrùn ọkàn, ààrùn jẹjẹrẹ, àtọ̀gbẹ, tàbí ààrùn ọ̀fìnkì tó le ni SIDS.
translations.reference: Small islands agree bold plan of action on noncommunicable diseases and mental health Today, the 2023 Bridgetown Declaration on Noncommunicable Diseases (NCDs) and Mental Health was launched, to address some of the world’s deadliest diseases in Small Island Developing States (SIDS), which are especially at risk. The Declaration is a key outcome of the SIDS Ministerial Conference on Noncommunicable Diseases (NCDs) and Mental Health, running from 14 to 16 June, co-hosted by the World Health Organization (WHO), the Pan American Health Organization (PAHO) and the Government of Barbados. SIDS are disproportionately impacted by NCDs – which cause 74% of all deaths globally – due to their reliance on imported food, commercial influences and the climate crisis. A new WHO report shows that 8 of the 15 countries with more than a 30% risk of premature death from cardiovascular disease, cancer, diabetes, or chronic respiratory disease are SIDS.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Small Island States to Take Steps on Non-Communicable Diseases and Chronic Illnesses**

The Bridgetown Declaration on Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs) and Chronic Illnesses was held today, aiming to address the most prevalent diseases globally within Small Island Developing States (SIDS), which are among the most vulnerable. The declaration is a crucial priority for the SIDS Ministerial Meeting on Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs) and Chronic Illnesses, which will take place from 14 to 16 October and is being co-organized by the World Health Organization (WHO), the Pan American Health Organization (PAHO), and the Government of Barbados. NCDs are significantly impacting SIDS – accounting for 74% of all deaths globally – due to their reliance on imported foods, the impact of tobacco and alcohol, and unhealthy lifestyles. A recent WHO report reveals that 8 of the 15 countries with more than 30% of premature deaths due to heart disease, diabetes, cancer, or chronic respiratory disease are located in SIDS.

translations.source: Àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 10 tí wọ́n ní ònkà iye ènìyàn sísanra tó pọ̀ jùlọ lágbàyé ni gbogbo wọ́n jẹ́ SIDS ní Pàsífíkì, níbi tí 45% àwọn àgbàlagbà ti sanra. Àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ ìlera ọpọlọ wọ́pọ̀ ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè SIDS, tó sì ń kóbá àpapọ 15.2% àwọn ènìyàn ní Caribbean àti 11.2% àwọn ènìyàn ní Pàsífíkì. Àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ní ìpèníjà ìlera ọpọlọ ń kojú ọ̀pọ̀ ewu ikú àìtọ́jọ́, tó fi mọ́ látara àwọn ìpèníjà ara tí wọn kò tọ́jú àti látara ìgbẹ̀mí ara ẹni. Àbùku, àìtó àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ pàtàkì àti ipa àyípadà ojú ọjọ́ ń ṣe àfikún sí ìṣẹ̀lẹ ìpèníjà ní SIDS tí ó nílò ìgbésẹ̀ ojú ẹsẹ̀. Ìkéde Bridgetown 2023 ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ ìgboyà láti kojú oríṣiríṣi àwọn ìṣòro àwùjọ, àyíká, ọrọ̀-ajéàti okòwò tó ń yọrí sí àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ NCDs àti ìlera ọpọlọ.
translations.reference: The 10 countries with the highest obesity rates globally are all SIDS in the Pacific, where over 45% of adults live with obesity. Mental health conditions are common in SIDS countries, affecting an estimated 15.2% of the population in the Caribbean and 11.2% of the population in the Pacific. People with mental health conditions face a higher risk of premature mortality, including from unaddressed physical health conditions and from suicide. Stigma, specialized staff shortages and the impact of climate change contribute to a challenging situation in SIDS that requires immediate attention. The 2023 Bridgetown Declaration outlines bold steps to address the range of social, environmental, economic and commercial issues that lead to NCDs and mental health conditions.

The prevalence of NCDs is high in SIDS countries, accounting for approximately 15.2% of the population in the Caribbean and 11.2% of the population in the Pacific. People with NCDs face a higher risk of premature death due to complications from untreated conditions and self-harm. Obesity, lack of essential services, and the impact of age-related vision loss contribute to the prevalence of NCDs in SIDS that require eye care. The Bridgetown Initiative 2023 promotes concrete steps to address various social challenges, including, ultimately, the economic shocks that lead to NCDs and chronic diseases."

translations.reference: Developed through an inclusive process led ‘for’ and ‘by’ SIDS, the declaration highlights that NCDs and mental health conditions cannot be properly addressed without responding to the climate crisis. “Bold action for our climate, good health, and well-being relies on redressing and reorganizing global financing to unlock billions in investment, while making it less punishing for developing countries to pay their debts,” said Her Excellency Mia Amor Mottley, Prime Minister of Barbados. “Funding for climate change adaptation and mitigation in the most vulnerable countries is also key, with noncommunicable diseases and mental health accounted for.” "The challenges faced by Small Island Developing States are interconnected and multifaceted. Climate change, environmental degradation, social and economic inequalities, and the commercial determinants of health exacerbate the burden of NCDs and mental health conditions," said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General.

translations.reference: As part of agreeing the Declaration, SIDS signed up to undertake a suite of actions, covering areas such as engaging all of society, accelerating implementation, securing investments, aligning across governments and accounting for progress. The conference also showcased specific commitments by SIDS on NCDs and mental health, including to improve access to mental health care in primary care facilities, update tobacco control legislation to cover e-cigarettes and vaping, and reduce populations’ salt and sugar intake. These commitments align with WHO’s ‘best buys’ – a set of cost-effective, evidence-based interventions to tackle NCDs. The list of best buys was recently updated at the World Health Assembly in May to include secondary prevention for rheumatic fever, acute and long-term management of asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease as well as several interventions for cervical, breast, colorectal, liver and childhood cancer, and the comprehensive treatment of cancer for those living with HIV. “SIDS in the Americas remain overwhelmingly vulnerable to NCDs.

The agenda also highlights specific concerns from SIDS regarding Non-Communicable Diseases (NCDs) and multiple illnesses, aiming to improve access to quality healthcare in public health facilities, refine policies on tobacco control and taxation, and encourage reduced consumption of alcohol and sugar. These concerns align with those prioritized by the World Health Organization (WHO) – namely, programs for disease prevention and control, and prioritizing NCDs. The listing of these priorities was discussed at the World Health Assembly in February to add a second level of support for pharmacists, powerful and long-term solutions for prevention and chronic diseases, including various interventions for adolescent health, mental health, violence, disability, education, and timely treatment of diseases that are prevalent among those with Key Affected Statuses (KASA). "SIDS in the Americas are facing a serious and growing threat from NCDs."

translations.source: Àpérò Mínísítà yìí wáyé ní àkókò aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ yóò sì ṣe kókó láti fi kún ohùn àti àwọn ohun tí SIDS nílò, mímú NCDs àti ìlera ọpọlọ wá sí iwájú àkọsílẹ àwọn ohun tó ṣe pàtàkì jùlọ fún wa," Dr Jarbas Barbosa, Olùdarí PAHO, àti Olùdarí Ẹkùn Agbègbè fún Ilẹ̀ Amẹ́ríkà WHO. "Láti gùn lé ìlọsíwájú yìí, a gbọ́dọ̀ gbé ìgbésẹ̀ báyìí. PAHO ti ṣetán láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ìtètè ṣàmúlò àwọn ìdásí ní ẹkùn agbègbè wa, tó fi mọ́ ìtọ́jú ojoojúmọ́ fún àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ wọ̀nyìí sínú ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ètò ìlera alábọ́dé àti lílo ànfààní àwọn ìmọ̀ tuntun bíi àwọn irinṣẹ́ ìlera àkósódù." Àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè SIDS ní àkọsílẹ̀ àti máa ṣe bọ̀ fún ìdarí ìpinnu-ṣíṣe lágbàyé; àdéhùn ojú ọ̀na Port of Spain àti SAMOA ló ṣínà fún ìkéde òṣèlú UN àkọ́kọ́-irú-rẹ̀ lórí NCDs ní ọdún 2018. Àpérò àti Ìkéde náà máa ṣe ìfitóni yóò sì ṣàpẹẹrẹ Ìpàdé Onípele-Gíga Àpérò Àpapọ UN tó ń bọ̀ lórí kíkárí ìlera lágbàyé ní oṣù Ọ̀wẹwẹ̀ ọdún 2023 àti lórí NCDs ní ọdún 2025.
translations.reference: This Ministerial Conference comes at a unique time and will be key to amplifying the voice and needs of SIDS, bringing NCDs and mental health to the forefront of our priority list,” said Dr Jarbas Barbosa, PAHO Director, and WHO Regional Director for the Americas. “To build on this momentum, we must act now. PAHO is ready to support accelerated implementation of interventions in our region, including mainstreaming care for these conditions into primary health services and taking advantage of innovations such as digital health tools.” SIDS countries have a track record of driving global decision-making; their Port of Spain and SAMOA pathway agreements paved the way for the first-ever UN political declaration on NCDs in 2018. The conference and the Declaration will inform and shape forthcoming UN General Assembly High-Level Meetings on universal health coverage in September 2023 and on NCDs in 2025.
translations.hypothesis:

"This Minister's meeting takes place at a critical time and will focus on addressing the needs of SIDS, the rise of NCDs and communicable diseases, bringing forward the most important issues for us," said Dr Jarbas Barbosa, Director of PAHO, and Regional Director for the Americas of WHO. "To advance this, we must take steps forward now. PAHO is ready to support the strengthening of health systems in our region, incorporating daily monitoring of these events into the framework of health programs and utilizing new technologies such as telemedicine. SIDS countries have a voice and will contribute to global decision-making; the Port of Spain Roadmap and the SAMOA Pathway provided the first UN recognition of NCDs in 2018. The meeting and the Pathway will inform the First High-Level Meeting on Global Health at the UN General Assembly in September 2023 and on NCDs in 2025."

translations.source: Ìpàdé Ètò Ààrùn Ìgbóná Tí Wọ́n Ti Patì Lágbàyé WHO parí pẹ̀lu ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ìpè fún ìgbésẹ̀ lágbàyé Àwọn aṣojú ìjọba àti àwọn aṣojú kúrò níbi Ìpàdé Àwọn Alájọṣepọ̀ Ètò Ààrùn Ìgbóná Tí Wọ́n Ti Patì Lágbàyé pẹ̀lú ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ìpè láti ṣiṣẹ́ láti ọ̀dọ Olùdarí rẹ̀ Dr Ibrahim Socé Fall. Pẹ̀lú bí ìpàdé oríṣiríṣi orílẹ̀-èdè náà, tí àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ ṣànkò-ṣànkò 300 wà níbẹ̀ náà, ṣe wá sópin ní 13 ọjọ́ Ìṣẹ́gun oṣù Òkudù, Dr Fall rọ àwọn akópa láti ṣàmúlò àkọmọ̀nà pé "tí a ò bá pe ara wa níjà, a ò lè dàgbà sókè a ò sì lè yípadà" . Nínú ètò tó fẹjú tó wáyé láti ṣe àtúnyẹwò ìlọsíwájú tako ìlànà-iṣẹ́ WHO fún àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná tí wọ́n ti patì 2021-2030 àti láti pèsè òye tó dára lórí àwọn iṣẹ́ NTD lágbàyé láti àpapọ ojú ìwòye adálórí-ààrùn àti ààlà-fibọ̀, àwọn alájọṣepọ̀, àwọn olùpèsè owó, àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ onímọ̀ ẹ̀rọ, àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ àti àwọn aṣojú orílẹ̀-èdè gbọ́dọ̀ ṣe àtúnṣe sí ìfọkànsìn wọn láti ṣe àṣeyọrí àfojúsùn èròngbà kan: ilé-ayé tí kò sí àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná tí wọ́n ti patì tí yóò bá fi di ọdún 2030. Àkòrí àkọ́kọ́ ìpàdé náà dá lórí àfojúsun "Àwùjọ kan tó ń ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ Ètò NTD Lágbàyé kalẹ̀".
translations.reference: WHO’s Global Neglected Tropical Diseases Programme Partners’ Meeting ends with stirring call for worldwide action Delegates and representatives left the Global Neglected Tropical Diseases Programme Partners’ Meeting with a stirring call to action from its director Dr Ibrahima Socé Fall. As the multinational meeting, attended by some 300 key partners, came to an end in Geneva on Tuesday 13 June, Dr Fall urged participants to take on board the message that, “if we do not challenge ourselves, we cannot evolve and improve”. In a wide-ranging event held to review progress against the WHO road map for neglected tropical diseases 2021–2030 and generate a better understanding of global NTD operations from both disease-specific and cross-cutting perspectives, partners, donors, technical partners, institutions and country representatives reinforced their commitment to achieving an ambitious target: a world free from neglected tropical diseases by 2030. The meeting’s primary theme centred around the vision of “One community delivering one Global NTD Programme”.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Meeting on Neglected Tropical Diseases Convened by WHO in Collaboration with Numerous Languages**

The WHO convened a meeting on Neglected Tropical Diseases (NTDs) in collaboration with numerous languages, involving government representatives and stakeholders. The meeting, held with various countries, with approximately 300 partners present, lasted for 13 days, concluding on September 13th. Dr. Ibrahim Socé Fall, the Director, urged participants to understand the urgency, stating that "if we don't consider ourselves to be in an emergency, we cannot move forward and we cannot make progress."

Within the program, which aims to review progress in line with the WHO's 2021-2030 strategy for neglected tropical diseases and to improve understanding of NTD efforts globally to reduce the burden of disease and disability, partners, donors, technical partners, institutions, and country representatives must adjust their priorities to achieve a shared ambition: a world free of neglected tropical diseases by 2030. The main theme of the meeting was "A Unified Approach to Scaling Up Global NTD Programs."

translations.source: Wọ́n mú ọgbọ́n tiwọn látara àwọn àyípadà ọlọgbọ́n pàtàkì mẹ́ta tí wọ́n ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún nínú ìlànà-iṣẹ́ - ìsọdi torílẹ̀-èdè lọ́pọ̀lọpọ̀, wíwọn àwọn ipa kìí ṣe àwọn ìlànà, àti ìfọkànsìn fún àwọn iṣẹ́ ààlà-fibọ̀ àti àjọṣepọ̀ fún iṣẹ́ tó lágbára - àwọn ìjírórò náà jẹ́ ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ tòótọ́, ó ń ṣíni-lójú ó sì ń tàn káàkiri bákan náà pẹ̀lú ìdí fún ìlera adọ́gba àti ìdájọ́ òdodo láti kojú àwọn NTD. Irú ìlànà bẹ́ẹ̀ nìkan, tí ìpàdé náà gbọ́, lè mú àyípadà tí àwọn àwùjọ kọ̀ọ̀kan tí wọ́n kúṣẹ̀ jùlọ lágbàyé nílò láti ṣe àṣeyọrí Àwọn Èròngbà Ìdàgbàsókè Alálòpẹ́ 2030 Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé. Nínú irú ìpàdé àgbáyé àkọ́kọ́ bẹ́ẹ̀ láti ìgbà tí wọ́n ti ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ìlànà-iṣẹ́ náà ní oṣù Ṣẹẹrẹ ọdún 2021, àwọn aṣojú ìjọba tún gbọ́ láti ọ̀dọ Olùdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́-papọ̀ WHO Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. Nígbà tí ó ń kí àwọn akópa káàbọ̀ níbi ìbẹ̀rẹ̀ àpèjẹ ọlọ́jọ́-méjì náà, Dr Tedros sọ wí pé, "Ìjókòó yìí ṣàpèjúwe nǹkan tí WHO wà fún - kí àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ máa kóra jọ láti ṣàmúlò agbára àjọni wọn láti kojú àwọn ìpèníjà ìlera lágbàyé tí ìkankan nínú wa ò lè kojú lóun nìkan. "Ìpèníjà oríṣiríṣi bíi àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná tí wọ́n ti patì nílò ìlànà oríṣiríṣi, nǹkan tí a sì mú wá nìyẹn, lápapọ̀ ".
translations.reference: Taking their cues from the three key strategic shifts advocated in the road map – greater country ownership, measuring impacts not process, and a commitment to cross-cutting activities and collaboration for greater efficiency – discussions were pragmatic, clear-sighted and suffused also with the need for health equity and justice to tackle NTDs. Only such an approach, the meeting heard, can bring about the change that is needed by some of the world’s poorest communities to achieve the United Nations 2030 Sustainable Development Goals. In the first such global meeting since the launch of the road map in January 2021, delegates heard also from WHO Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. Welcoming participants at the beginning of the two-day symposium, Dr Tedros said, “This gathering illustrates what WHO is all about – partners coming together to leverage their collective strength to address global health challenges that none of us could address alone. “A multifaceted challenge like neglected tropical diseases takes a multifaceted approach, and that is what we bring, together”.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"The emergence of the ten priorities was driven by three critical gaps in the work plan – addressing multiple languages, recognizing that impacts are not processes, and strengthening support for cross-sectoral and collaborative work for high-impact interventions – these gaps are real, they are visible, and they are spreading with the urgency of addressing neglected tropical diseases and promoting truth from the NTDs. A system like this, which the meeting recognized, can generate the outcomes that each community most needs to achieve the Sustainable Development Goals 2030 of the United Nations. In this first global meeting of its kind since the work plan was launched in September 2021, government representatives also heard from the WHO Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. While kicking off the two-day meeting, Dr. Tedros stated, "This moment describes what WHO is for – to bring partners together to amplify their collective power to address global health challenges that none of us can solve alone." "Various challenges like infectious diseases require different approaches, which is what was brought up, collectively."

translations.source: Àwọn aṣojú ìjọba àti àwọn akópa lo ọjọ́ méji tó tẹ̀lé e láti ṣàwárí àwọn àlàfo pàtàkì àti àwọn ìpèníjà láti ṣe àṣeyọrí àwọn èròngbà onípele-gíga àti abẹ́lé, àwọn ìpèníjà nílé-lóko. Lára wọn ìpèníjà nílé-lóko wọ̀nyẹn lati rí ríríi dájú pé àwọn ènìyàn tí àwọn NTD bá ṣe àkóbá fún tààrà, àti àwọn tí wọ́n ní ojúṣe tààrà fún ṣíṣe àwọn ìdásí tako àwọn NTD, ni wọ́n bèèrè fún tí wọ́n sì gbà wọlé, pàápàá jùlọ níbi èyí tó níṣe pẹ̀lú ìbánisọ̀rọ̀ àti ìfọ̀rọ̀wérọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwùjọ. Ìpàdé náà tún ṣe àkọsílẹ̀ pàtàkì ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn àwùjọ ìfikọ́ra bákan náà, láti lè mú ìdàgbàsókè bá ìmọ̀ọ́ṣe àti ìgboyà àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ NTD lágbàyé. Nínú àfikún ìjírórò, àwọn aṣojú ìjọba fẹnukò lórí àwọn ìlànà-iṣẹ́ àti àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ tó ṣe é ṣe láti ṣàmúlò iṣẹ́ fún àwọn àfojúsùn tí wọ́n là kalẹ̀ tí wọ́n sì jọ ṣètò papọ̀ fún Ètò Amúṣẹ́ṣe Àgbàyé fún 2025-2030. Àwọn igbéṣẹ̀ pàtàkì àti ìpinnu tó ń wáyé látara ìpàdé náà la ti rí àwọn àbá fún ìrólágbára àti ìṣàmúlò àwọn ìkànnì ìdarí orílẹ̀-èdè láti ṣe àrídájú ìdarí fún ètò àjọṣe àti fún àmójútó àti ìgbéléwọ̀n, àti láti ṣe àrídájú àmójútó ojú-òpóìpèsè tó ṣiṣẹ́ lọ́pọ̀lọpọ̀ láàrín àwọn ètò orílẹ̀-èdè.
translations.reference: Delegates and participants spent the subsequent two days identifying the major gaps and challenges to achieving high-level goals and local, on-the-ground challenges. Those on-the-ground challenges include ensuring that people directly affected by NTDs, and those directly responsible for administering interventions against NTDs, are solicited and acknowledged, particularly in terms of communication and community engagement. The meeting noted also the importance of developing communities of practice, in order to build the capacity and confidence of the global NTD workforce. In further discussions, delegates agreed on principles and practical steps to implement action towards the stated targets and planned together for a Global Action Plan for 2025–2030. Key actions and decisions arising from the meeting included recommendations for strengthening and operationalizing national coordination platforms to ensure coordination for joint planning and for monitoring and evaluation, and to ensure more efficient supply chain management within national systems.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Government officials and partners spent two days deliberating on critical challenges and solutions to ensure the success of high-impact and cross-cutting interventions, including community health worker interventions. Among them, the community health worker intervention aimed to clearly establish that people who are vulnerable to neglected tropical diseases (NTDs) and those with disabilities hindering the implementation of NTD interventions should be prioritized and provided with support, especially in areas working with communities through dialogue and sensitization. The meeting also emphasized the importance of strengthening the capacity of local communities to support the knowledge and skills of NTD workers worldwide. Additionally, government officials discussed strategies and steps to scale up work for frontline health workers and jointly set priorities for the 2025-2030 Global Health Strategy. The key priorities and decisions emerging from the meeting were seen as opportunities to strengthen and accelerate the implementation of national health leadership to advance health programs and prevention and control, and to accelerate the implementation of multisectoral action across national programs."

translations.source: Ìpàdé Àwọn Alájọṣepọ̀ náà wáyé bákan náà ní ìparí ìlànà àtúnyẹwò iṣẹ́ pàtàkì láàrín Ẹ̀ka Ìdènà Àwọn Ààrùn Ìgbóná Tí Wọ́n Ti Patì WHO. Láti bíi oṣù mẹ́rin kọjá, WHO ti ń gbọ́ àfikún àti ìjẹ́rìsí láti ọ̀dọ gbogbo àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ àti àwọn alẹ́nulọ́rọ̀ rẹ̀, láti lè rí i dájú pé òun fúnra rẹ̀ ní àwọn àwòmọ́ ṣíṣe ìṣirò, kíkó àkóyawọ́ àti ìṣe déédé tí ó gbà wọlé tó sì ń bèèrè fún. Lára àwọn àwòmọ́ náà ni rírí i dájú pé wọ́n ṣe àdápadà NTD nínú àgbékalẹ̀ ìlera lágbàyé. Ṣíṣe ìpàdé pẹ̀lú àwọn akópa jẹ́ ìkíni káàbọ̀ fún àtẹnumọ́ lórí àwọn ojúṣe pàtàkì tí àwùjọ NTD lè ṣe tí ó sì gbọdọ̀ ṣe níbi ìdásí àwọn ìpèníjà káàkiri bíi Kíkárí Ìlera Lágbàyé, Ìlera Kan àti idúkokò àyípadà ojú ọjọ́ lágbàyé tó ń lékún. Àwọn aṣojú ìjọba tí wọ́n wá fúnra wọn ni wọ́n tún ráàyè gbádùn ìpàtẹ ọjà tí wọ́n fi sọrí àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná tí wọ́n ti patì ní olú ilé-iṣẹ́ WHO.
translations.reference: The Partners’ Meeting took place also at the end of a major functional review process within WHO’s Department of Control of Neglected Tropical Diseases. Over the course of the past four months, WHO has been hearing contributions and testimony from all its partners and stakeholders, in order to ensure that it itself embodies the values of accountability, transparency and equity that it espouses and demands. Those values also include ensuring that NTDs are repositioned in the global health architecture. Meeting participants welcomed the emphasis on the key roles that the NTD community can and must play in contributing to wider challenges such as Universal Health Coverage, One Health and the increasing threat of global climate change. Delegates attending in person were also able to enjoy an exhibition dedicated to neglected tropical diseases at WHO headquarters.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"The Partnership Meeting convened following the conclusion of the essential work plan review between the Neglected Tropical Diseases (NTDs) team of the World Health Organization (WHO). For over four months, WHO has been receiving feedback and suggestions from all partners and stakeholders to ensure it has the necessary resources for implementation, monitoring, and fulfilling requests. Among these resources is confirmation that they are prioritizing NTDs within global health initiatives. Holding meetings with donors is crucial for focusing on the key targets that the NTD community can and must achieve, particularly in areas of need like the Global Fund, One Health, and the global fight against blinding trachoma. Government representatives who attended also welcomed the market-based approach used to address the neglected tropical diseases that WHO has committed to."

translations.source: Wọ́n ṣíṣọ lójú àtò àwọn pánẹ́lì onírin kan láti ṣàpèjúwe àwọn májẹ̀mú 20 tí ó parapọ̀ di ìpínsísọ̀rí NTD, àwọn wọ̀nyìí ni wọ́n sì tẹ̀lé pẹ̀lú fọ́tò àti ìròyìn tó ń ṣàpèjúwe àwọn iṣẹ́ tí wọ́n ti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ rẹ̀ láti bíi àìmọye ọdún sẹ́yìn láti kojú wọn.
translations.reference: A set of metal panels was commissioned to illustrate the 20 conditions that make up the NTD grouping, and these were accompanied by photographs and information illustrating the actions that have been put in place over the course of many decades to tackle them.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Yoruba document:

"A group of medical panel members gathered at the top of the hall to explain the 20 records that have been compiled into a NTD (Neglected Tropical Diseases) registry. These were accompanied by photos and news detailing the work they have done to create awareness from previous years to benefit them."



**Explanation of some choices:**

*   **NTD:** I've kept "NTD" as it is, assuming it refers to "Neglected Tropical Diseases," a common abbreviation in this context.
*   **"àwọn pánẹ́lì onírin"**: Translated as "medical panel members" to convey the meaning accurately.
*   **"ìpínsísọ̀rí"**: Translated as "registry" to reflect the meaning of a compiled collection of records.
*   **"láti kojú wọn"**: Translated as "to benefit them" to convey the purpose of the awareness creation.

translations.source: Ìpàdé Àwọn Alájọṣepọ̀ Ètò Ààrùn Ìgbóná Tí Wọ́n Ti Patì Lágbàyé, 12-13 oṣù Òkudù ọdún 2023 Àwọn aṣojú àwùjọ NTD lágbàyé máa kóra jọ ní Geneva ní ọ̀sẹ̀ tó ń bọ̀ láti kópa nínú Ìpàdé Àwọn Alájọṣepọ̀ Ètò Ààrùn Ìgbóná Tí Wọ́n Ti Patì, tí yóò wáyé ní gbọ̀gan olú ilé-iṣẹ́ náà ní 12-13 oṣù Òkudù ọdún 2023. Àwọn èròngbà ìpàdé náà ni láti ṣe àtúnyẹwò ìtẹ̀síwájú lórí ìlànà-iṣẹ́ WHO fún àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná tí wọ́n ti patì 2021-2030 àti láti pèsè òye tó dára fún àwọn iṣẹ́ NTD lágbàyé láti àpapọ ojú ìwòye adálórí-ààrùn àti ààlà-fibọ̀, bákan náà ni ṣíṣe ìjírórò tó fẹjú káàkiri àfojúsùn fún ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ One WHO ní orí Ètò NTD Lágbàyé kan. Nígbà tó ṣe wí pé WHO ló pe ìpàdé àgbáyé àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ NTD ní ọdún 2007 àti 2017, ètò kẹ́ta tó ń bọ̀ lọ́nà yìí ni àkọ́kọ́ láti ìgbà tí wọ́n ti ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ìlànà-iṣẹ́ náà ní oṣù Ṣẹẹrẹ ọdún 2021. ìlànà-iṣẹ́ WHO fún àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná tí wọ́n ti patì 2021-2030 jẹ́ ìlànà-iṣẹ́ tó kún fọ́fọ́ tí ó sàlàyé àwọn àfojúsùn, àwọn ìlànà fún ìbámu pẹ̀lú Àwọn Èròngbà Ìdàgbàsókè Alálòpẹ́ àti àwọn èròngbà àgbáyé mìíràn àti ìpè fún àyípadà ọlọgbọ́n nínú àwọn ìlànà NTD pẹ̀lú èròngbà láti fi òpin sí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná tí wọ́n ti patì ní ọdún 2030.
translations.reference: Global Neglected Tropical Diseases Programme Partners’ Meeting, 12–13 June 2023 Representatives of the global NTD community will gather in Geneva next week to participate in the Global Neglected Tropical Diseases Programme Partners’ Meeting, which takes place in headquarters’ auditorium on 12-13 June 2023. The objectives of the meeting are to review progress on the WHO road map for neglected tropical diseases 2021–2030 and generate a better understanding of global NTD operations from both disease-specific and cross-cutting perspectives, as well as to engage in wide-ranging discussions around the vision for One WHO delivering on one Global NTD Programme. While previous global meetings of NTD partners were convened by WHO in 2007 and 2017, this third upcoming event is the first since the launch of the road map in January 2021. The WHO road map for neglected tropical diseases 2021–2030 is a comprehensive framework that outlines targets, mechanisms for alignment with the Sustainable Development Goals and other global agendas and calls for strategic shifts in NTD approaches with the aim of ending the epidemic of neglected tropical diseases by 2030.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Yoruba document:

**Meeting of the Partnership for Neglected Tropical Diseases (NTDs) Held Globally, 12-13 October 2023**

Global NTD community representatives will convene in Geneva next week to participate in the Meeting of the Partnership for Neglected Tropical Diseases, which will take place at the organization's headquarters on 12-13 October 2023. The meeting participants are expected to review progress on the WHO’s strategy for neglected tropical diseases 2021-2030 and to develop a better understanding of global NTD operations to reduce the burden of disease and disability. They will also discuss the implementation of a robust framework for the implementation of One WHO within a Global NTD Programme. It should be noted that WHO convened the global NTD partnership meeting in 2007 and 2017, and this is the third in the series since the launch of the strategy in April 2021. The WHO strategy for neglected tropical diseases 2021-2030 is a comprehensive strategy that outlines priorities, approaches for collaboration with Regional and Global Technical Experts, and targets for knowledge enhancement within NTD programs, including efforts to eliminate neglected tropical diseases by 2030.

translations.source: Ìpàdé náà máa dá lórí: àtúnyẹwò ìtẹ̀síwájú lọ́wọ́lọ́wọ́ láti ṣe àṣeyọrí àwọn àfojúsùn ọdún 2030; mímọ àwọn àlàfo pàtàkì àti àwọn ìpèníjà láti débi àwọn àfojúsùn wọ̀nyẹn; gbígbà àwọn ìpìlẹ̀-òye iṣẹ́ àti àwọn igbéṣẹ̀ tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ fún bí a ṣe lè ṣàmúlò iṣẹ́ tọ àwọn àfojúsùn náà; ṣíṣe ìwúrí fún àtìlẹ́yìn àti ìfọkànsìn láti ọ̀dọ àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ NTD láti ṣiṣẹ́ lórí àwọn àyípadà tí wọ́n bèèrè fún nínú ìlànà-iṣẹ́ náà àti àwọn àfojúsùn rẹ̀; àti

The meeting will focus on: reviewing progress with the 2030 Sustainable Development Goals; identifying key challenges and priorities to achieve those goals; strengthening the capacity of the workforce and institutions to effectively implement work towards those goals; mobilizing resources and commitment from NTD partners to work on the deliverables requested in the work plan and its goals; and

translations.source: ṣíṣe ètò papọ̀ fún Ètò Amúṣẹ́ṣe Àgbàyé 2025-2030. 300 lé àwọn akópa nínú ìpàdé náà ni ìrètí wà pé wọn yóò kópa, yálà ní-tara tàbí lórí ẹ̀rọ ayélujára. Àtò àwọn pánẹ́lì onírin máa ṣe àpèjúwe àwọn májẹ̀mú 20 tí wọ́n pín sísọ̀rí lábẹ́ àbùradà NTD tí yóò sì pèsè àlàyé lórí ìgbésẹ̀ tí wọ́n ṣàmúlò láti kojú ẹrù wúwo wọn.

translations.reference: This is the fourth year of the film festival and competition was no less fierce, with some 93 shortlisted films covering issues ranging from anxiety and depression through to the effects of climate change on health, as well as the health challenges of people with disabilities. The films were judged by a panel of distinguished professionals, artists and activists, including the renown actors, Sharon Stone and Alfonso Herrera, dance choreographer, Sherrie Silver, climate activist, Sophia Kianni and media personality, Adelle Onyango. They were joined by senior United Nations officials and WHO staff. “The Health for All Film Festival brings a human face to WHO’s scientific work,” said WHO Director-General, Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. “Listening to the voices of people affected by health issues is a powerful way to raise awareness and improve our understanding of people’s experiences and this can help us advance towards health for all.”

"The fourth Film Festival year had a competition that wasn't particularly powerful, with 93 films each that brought forth struggles starting from anxiety and mental distress through the impact of eye diseases on health, and then the human health advocates who have self-advocacy. The selection committee of filmmakers, actors, and youth ambassadors, who included popular actors Sharon Stone and Alfonso Herrera, event directors Sherrie Silver, eye disease ambassador Sophia Kianni, and media personality Adelle Onyango, were the ones who brought the judging to the films. Experts from within the United Nations Task Force and WHO staff joined them. "The Film Festival for Health for All brought people's eyes to the WHO's scientific works," said WHO Under-Secretary-General Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. "Paying attention to the suffering that health crises cause is a powerful way to increase understanding and to make us aware of the experiences of these people, and this can lead to providing health for all."
