Ìwé tó fara jọ ọ́: EB152(23) -Sáyẹnsì ìhùwàsí fún ìlera tó dára EB152/25 - Sáyẹnsì Ìhùwàsí fún ìlera tó dára, Ìjábọ̀ láti ọwọ́ Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ Sáyẹnsì Ìhùwàsí fún Ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ Ìlera Tó Dára Àwọn ohun tó wà lókè yìí ni wọ́n jíròrò lé lórí gẹ́gẹ́ bí ọ̀kan lára àwọn ìwé A76/7 Rev.1 - Ìjábọ̀ ìṣọ̀kan láti ọwọ́ Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀.
translations.reference: They stressed the importance of building capacity in this area, particularly in regional offices, and of creating a repository of evidence and synergies between sectors, including with academia and the private sector. Related documents: EB152(23) - Behavioural sciences for better health EB152/25 - Behavioural sciences for better health, Report by the Director-General Behavioural Sciences for Better Health Initiative The above items were discussed as part of the document A76/7 Rev.1 - Consolidated report by the Director-General.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"There is a significant need for the implementation of occupational safety and health practices in this area, especially in local government offices, and the creation of collaboration and partnership between departments, working together with schools and individual units. The relevant documents are: EB152(23) - Basic Science for Quality Health EB152/25 - Basic Science for Quality Health, Report from the Director-General of Basic Science for Quality Health for the Establishment of Quality Health Services. The items listed here are heavily based on one of the documents A76/7 Rev.1 - Report of Consolidation from the Director-General."



**Notes on the Translation:**

*   **Cultural Context:** I've tried to maintain a formal tone appropriate for official documents.
*   **Specificity:** Some terms are difficult to translate directly without more context. I've aimed for the most accurate and understandable English equivalent.
*   **"Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀":** This translates to "Director-General."
*   **"Ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ Ìlera Tó Dára":** This refers to the "Establishment of Quality Health Services."

translations.source: Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé kẹrìn-dín-lọ́gọ́rin - Ìròyìn ojoojúmọ́: 27 oṣù Èbìbí ọdún 2023 ìlànà-iṣẹ́ àgbáyé àkọ́kọ́ lórí ìdíwọ́ àti ìdènà àkóràn Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé ti fẹnukò lónì lórí ìlànà-iṣẹ́ àgbéṣe lórí ìdíwọ́ àti ìdènà àkóràn (IPC) tó jẹ́ àkọ́kọ́-irú-rẹ̀, tí ó gùn lé àwọn ìgbìyànjú bíi ògún ọdún tí WHO àti àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ rẹ̀ léwájú rẹ̀. ìlànà-iṣẹ́ náà pèsè àwọn ọ̀nà ọlọ́gbọ́n fún Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ láti mú àdínkù alálòpẹ́ bá ewu àwọn àkóràn ajẹmọ́-ìlera (HAIs) tó ń lọ lọ́wọ́, tó fi mọ́ àwọn tí wọ́n ń ṣàfihàn ẹ̀yàwuuru jáàgùn. àwọn HAI wà lára àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àìfẹ́ gbogbo ìgbà tó pọ̀ jùlọ tó ń wáyé ní ọ̀gangan ipò ìpèsè ètò ìlera. Àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 àti àwọn àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn ńlá láìpẹ́ yìí bíi ààrùn kòkòrò Ẹ̀bọ́là, àìsàn èémí Àárín Ìlà-oòrùn àti kòkòrò ààrùn Sudan ti tú àṣírí àlàfo tó wà nínú ètò IPC ní gbogbo àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè kedere.
translations.reference: Seventy-sixth World Health Assembly – Daily update: 27 May 2023 First global strategy on infection prevention and controlThe World Health Assembly agreed today on the first-ever global strategy on infection prevention and control (IPC), which builds on almost two decades of efforts led by WHO and partners. The strategy provides Member States with strategic directions to substantially reduce the ongoing risk of health care-associated infections (HAIs), including those that exhibit antimicrobial resistance. HAIs are among the most frequent adverse events occurring in the context of health service delivery. The COVID-19 pandemic and recent major disease outbreaks such as Ebola virus disease, the Middle East respiratory syndrome and the Sudan virus disease have clearly exposed the existing gaps in IPC programmes in all countries.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**World Health Assembly – Daily Bulletin: 27 April 2023**

The World Health Assembly today finalized a landmark global strategy on infection prevention and control (IPC), its first of its kind, aimed at strengthening the capacity of countries, including high-burden settings like those of WHO and its partners. The strategy outlines evidence-based approaches for Member States to increase resilience to healthcare-associated infections (HAIs), which disproportionately affect vulnerable populations. HAIs are among the most common and preventable adverse events occurring in healthcare settings. The COVID-19 pandemic and other recent major outbreaks, such as Ebola, the Middle East Respiratory Syndrome (MERS), and the Sudan virus outbreak, have exposed critical gaps in IPC systems in all countries.

translations.source: ìlànà-iṣẹ́ náà ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àfojúsùn tó hàn: ní ọdún 2030, gbogbo ènìyàn tó ń lo tàbí pèsè ètò ìlera rí ìdáàbòbò kúrò níbi àwọn àkóràn tó so mọ́ ọ. Àwọn kókó èròngbà rẹ̀ mẹ́tẹ̀ẹ̀ta ni: láti dènà àkóràn nínú ètò ìlera; ṣiṣẹ́ láti rí i dájú pé àwọn ètò IPC wà ní ìkàlẹ̀ wọ́n sì ń ṣàmúlò rẹ̀; kí ó sì darí àwọn iṣẹ́ IPC pẹ̀lú àwọn agbègbè àti ẹ̀ka mìíràn. ìlànà-iṣẹ́ náà ni wọ́n fi sun èyíkéyìí ààyè tí wọ́n ti ń pèsè ètò ìlera, káàkiri ètò ìlera; ó dá lórí ìpìlẹ̀-òye ìtọ́jú tó mọ́ tí kò sì léwu gẹ́gẹ́ bí òpó ìpìlẹ̀ fún ẹ̀tọ́ sí ìlera, tí ìdọ́gba ń darí, tí ó sì gbọdọ̀ ṣe àrídájú ìṣirò àti alálòpẹ́. ìlànà-iṣẹ́ IPC lágbayé ni wọ́n máa ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún pẹ̀lú, tí wọn ó sì lò ó papọ̀ pẹ̀lú, ètò amúṣẹ́ṣe àgbáyé àti ìlànà-iṣẹ́ àmójútó tó ní nǹkan ṣe pẹ̀lú ẹ̀, tí wọn yóò ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ rẹ̀ ní ọdún 2023-2024. Ìwé tó fara jọ ọ́ EB152/9 - Àkọsílẹ ìlànà-iṣẹ́ àgbáyé lórí ìdíwọ́ àti ìdènà àkóràn, Ìjábọ̀ ìsọníṣókí Aláṣẹ láti ọwọ́ Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀
translations.reference: The strategy sets a clear vision: by 2030, everyone accessing or providing health care is safe from associated infections. Its three key objectives are: to prevent infection in health care; act to ensure IPC programmes are in place and implemented; and coordinate IPC activities with other areas and sectors. The strategy is focused on any setting where health care is delivered, across the health system; it is based on the principle of clean and safe care as a fundamental component of the right to health, which is equity driven, and which should ensure accountability and sustainability. The global IPC strategy will be complemented by, and used in conjunction with, an associated global action plan and monitoring framework, that will be developed in 2023–2024. Related document EB152/9 - Draft global strategy on infection prevention and control, Executive summary Report by the Director-General
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"The strategy's focus is on reducing the burden of disease: by 2030, all people using or benefiting from health programs should be free from the diseases associated with them. Its three main pillars are: to prevent disease within health programs; to ensure that IPC programs are in place and effectively implemented; and to integrate IPC activities with other regions and sectors. The strategy will build on existing efforts to improve health systems globally; it is based on a solid evidence base with minimal risk, like other pillars for access to health, which is led by commitment and must promote equity and sustainability. The global IPC strategy will be supported by, and used in conjunction with, the global prevention program and the action plan that relates to it, which will be developed in 2023-2024. Document reference EB152/9 - Report on the global strategy for prevention and control of disease, Report of the Secretary-General from the Under-Secretary-General."

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   I've aimed for clarity and accuracy while maintaining a formal tone appropriate for a strategy document.
*   Some phrases required interpretation to convey the intended meaning in English.
*   "IPC" is assumed to stand for "Infection Prevention and Control" based on the context.

translations.source: Iṣẹ́ WHO lórí ìdíwọ́ àti ìdènà àkóràn Ìpinnu mánigbàgbé ṣínà fún ìrólágbára ìtúnirọ nínú ètò ìlera Lónì Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé fẹnukò lórí ìpinnu tó lápẹrẹ lórí ìrólágbára ìtúnirọ nínú ètò ìlera. Ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìtúnirọ kó ipa pàtàkì láti rí i dájú pé ìgbádùn ẹ̀tọ́ ọmọnìyàn tó fi mọ́ ojúlówó ìlera ara àti ọpọlọ tó ga jùlọ tí ènìyàn lè ní. Ó tún ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ àti ìbímọ, ó sì mọ rírì ẹ̀tọ́ sí iṣẹ́ àti ẹ̀tọ́ sí ètò ẹ̀kọ́. Ìpinnu tó lápẹrẹ yìí gbèrò láti yanjú àwọn ìpèníjà nínú ìtúnirọ gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ìdí láti: mú àlékún bá ìpolongo ìtúnirọ nígbà tí wọ́n bá ń ṣe ètò àwọn kàn-án-pá ìlera àti èròngbà ìwádìí, pínpín ohun èlò, ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ìfọwọ́sowọ́pọ̀ àti gbígba ìfiránṣẹ́ ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ;
translations.reference: WHO's work on infection prevention and control Historic resolution paves the way for strengthening rehabilitation in health systemsToday the World Health Assembly agreed a landmark resolution on strengthening rehabilitation in health systems. Rehabilitation services play a key role in ensuring the enjoyment of human rights including the highest attainable standard of physical and mental health. It also promotes sexual and reproductive health, and recognizes the right to work and the right to education. This landmark resolution aims to address the challenges in rehabilitation such as the need to: increase awareness of rehabilitation when setting health priorities and research agendas, allocating resources, promoting cooperation and enabling technology transfer;
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"WHO's work on prevention and control of non-communicable diseases is geared towards strengthening health systems. During the World Health Assembly, a resolution was passed focusing on strengthening health systems. The work of health systems plays a crucial role in ensuring the well-being of the population and reducing the most prevalent diseases that people can experience. It also supports reproductive health and childbirth, and respects the right to work and the right to education. This resolution further encourages stakeholders in health systems generally as a basis for: reducing barriers to health system planning when implementing health and wellness programs and addressing health inequalities, allocating resources, implementing and supporting innovation and promoting collaboration and the adoption of digital health technologies."

translations.source: rí i dájú pé àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ní ohun èlò tó dára láti dáhùn sí àlékún òjijì nínú ìnílò ìtúnirọ tó fi mọ́ ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ aṣèrànwọ́ nítorí àwọn pàjáwírì ìlera; rí i dájú pé àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n wà nípò ìdẹ́yẹsí tí wọ́n sì kángun séwu ní ànfààní sí ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìtúnirọ tó rọjú, tó jẹ́ ojúlówó tó sì péye tó fi mọ́ àwọn ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ aṣèrànwọ́; sọ́ra fún ìnáwó àpò-ara-ẹni fún àwọn ènìyàn láti ní ànfààní sí ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìtúnirọ àti ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ aṣèrànwọ́ tí ó lè fa ìnira owó; àti yanjú ìpele àìtó àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìtúnirọ lọ́wọ́lọ́wọ́ láti ṣíṣe fún ìnílò àwọn ará ìlú. Ìpinnu yìí ṣe àkọsílẹ̀ ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ìgbésẹ̀ tí Ilé-iṣẹ́ Ìjọba WHO ní láti gbé bíi:
translations.reference: ensure countries are better equipped to respond to the sudden increase in rehabilitation needs including assistive technology due to health emergencies; ensure persons in marginalized and vulnerable situations have access to affordable, quality and appropriate rehabilitation services including assistive technology; avoid high out-of-pocket costs for people to access rehabilitation services and assistive technology that can cause financial hardships; and address the current insufficient level of rehabilitation workforce to serve the needs of the population. The resolution lists a range of actions to be taken by the WHO Secretariat such as:
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"It is confirmed that countries have a valuable tool to respond to specific needs within the emergency response that supports digital health technologies due to health emergencies; it is confirmed that people who are in vulnerable positions and are at risk benefit from access to digital health technology services, which are essential and affordable, that support digital health technologies; provide support for mental health services for people to benefit from access to digital health technology services and digital health technologies that can alleviate financial hardship; and improve the capacity of emergency workers to serve the needs of urban populations. This decision outlines several steps that the World Health Organization (WHO) will take:"



Let me know if you'd like any part of the translation clarified or adjusted!

translations.source: ṣíṣe àtẹ̀jáde ìjábọ̀ ní ìparí ọdún 2026 pẹ̀lú àlàyé lórí ìkápá Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ láti dáhùn sí àwọn ohun tí ìtúnirọ nílò; ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn àfojúsùn àti atọ́ka fún kíkárí ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìtúnirọ tó ṣe é lò ní ọdún 2023; ṣíṣe àrídájú pé wọ́n pèsè àwọn ohun èlò tó yẹ fún WHO láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ nínú ìṣàmúlò ìtọ́nisọ́nà ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ àti ohun èlò; àti ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ láti ṣe àfikún ìtúnirọ àti ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ aṣèrànwọ́ nínú ètò ìgbaradì àti ìdáhùn pàjáwírì wọn. Ilé-iṣẹ́ ìjọba WHO máa jábọ̀ lórí ìtẹ̀síwájú nínú ìṣàmúlò ìpinnu yìí fún Àpéjọ Ìlera ní ọdún 2026, 2028 àti 2030.
translations.reference: publishing a baseline report by the end of 2026 with information on the capacity of Member States to respond to rehabilitation needs; developing targets and indicators for effective coverage of rehabilitation services by 2030; ensuring appropriate resources are allocated at WHO to support Member States in implementing technical guidance and resources; and supporting Member States to integrate rehabilitation and assistive technology in their emergency preparedness and response plan. The WHO Secretariat will report on progress in the implementation of this resolution to the Health Assembly in 2026, 2028 and 2030.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Implementation of the Action Plan at the End of 2026 with an Explanation on the Responsibilities of Member States to respond to emergency needs; Implementation of the allocation of resources and indicators for the strengthening of the national health emergency response system as it was used in 2023; Ensuring that they procure the necessary commodities for WHO to support Member States in the mobilization of health technology and commodities; and Ensuring support for Member States to enhance emergency preparedness and technological knowledge within their development and resilience programs. The WHO government body will report on the progress in the implementation of this decision for the Health Assembly in 2026, 2028 and 2030.**

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   I've aimed for clarity and accuracy while maintaining a formal tone appropriate for a WHO document.
*   Some phrases required interpretation to convey the intended meaning in English.
*   The Yoruba language can be nuanced, and this translation represents my best understanding of the text.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Ìwé tó fara jọ ọ́: EB152/8 - Ìrólágbára ìtúnirọ nínú ètò ìlera, Ìjábọ̀ láti ọwọ́ Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ ojú-òpótó fara jọ ọ́ Iṣẹ́ WHO lórí ìtúnirọ Àwọn nǹkan tó wà lókè yìí ni wọ́n jíròrò lé lórí gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ọ̀kan lára ìwé A76/7 Rev.1 - Ìjábọ̀ ìṣọ̀kan láti ọwọ́ Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀. Ìpinnu lórí ìrólágbára ìkápá ìdámọ̀ àisàn
translations.reference: Related document: EB152/8 - Strengthening rehabilitation in health systems, Report by the Director-General Related link WHO's work on rehabilitation The items above were discussed as part of the document A76/7 Rev.1 - Consolidated report by the Director-General. Resolution on strengthening diagnostics capacity
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Document Number: EB152/8 - Strengthening of Health Security, Report from the Director-General of the WHO Secretariat on Health Emergency Preparedness**

The content of this document is largely based on one of the chapters of document A76/7 Rev.1 - Report of the Director-General. **Decision on strengthening the capacity for prevention and control of infectious diseases.**



**Notes on the Translation:**

*   **EB152/8:** This is likely a document number and is kept as is.
*   **Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀:** This translates to "Director-General."
*   **Iṣẹ́ WHO:** This refers to the "WHO Secretariat."
*   **Ìtúnirọ:** This is a key term meaning "health security" or "health emergency preparedness."
*   **Ìjábọ̀:** This means "Report."
*   **Ìrólágbára:** This means "Strengthening" or "Enhancing."
*   **Ìkápá ìdámọ̀ àisàn:** This refers to the "capacity for prevention and control of infectious diseases."

translations.source: Ní 26 oṣù Èbìbí Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ buwọ́lù ìpinnu kan láti ró ìkápá ìdámọ̀ àisàn ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè lágbára àti láti mú àyípadà bá ànfààní sí ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìdámọ̀ àìsàn. Ìpinnu oríṣiríṣi tó gbòòrò náà mọ̀ wí pé ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìdámọ̀ àìsàn ṣe pàtàkì fún ìdènà, ìtọpinpin , ìdámọ̀ àìsàn, àmójútó ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀, ṣíṣe àmójútó àti ìtọ́jú ààrùn tí kìí ràn, àwọn ààrùn ìgbóná àti èyí tó ṣọ̀nwọ́n tí wọ́n ti patì, ìfarapa, àti ìpèníjà ara. Ìdámọ̀ àìsàn fi ààyè gba ìdánimọ̀ àìsàn ní pàtó, fún ìdí èyí ìbẹ̀rẹ̀ àwọn ìtọ́jú tó péye fún àbájáde ìlera tó dára lásìkò. Ìpinnu náà ro gbogbo ìpìlẹ "ìdámọ̀ àisàn", torí náà a rí àpapọ̀ àwọn àyẹ̀wo ààyè fún ìmọ̀ ìjìnlẹ̀ "nínú ìgò" b.a àyẹ̀wò ìdámọ̀ àìsàn ojú ẹsẹ̀ àti pínpín DNA sí ìṣọ̀rí (PVR) àti ìdánimọ̀ àìsàn "tí kìí ṣe nínú ìgò" b.a. àwọn ẹ̀rọ ayàwòrán tàbí wíwọn ìfúnpá. Ó kárí àwọn igbéṣẹ̀ fún ìwádìí àti ìdàgbàsókè, ìpèsè nǹkan (tó fi mọ́ ìpèsè lábẹ́lé àti ìfiránṣẹ́ ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ), òfin, yíyàn àti rírà, ìpolongo, ìgbẹnusọ àti kíkojú àwọn ìdíwọ́ ànfààní lápapọ̀.
translations.reference: On 26 May Member States endorsed a resolution to strengthen diagnostics capacity in countres and to improve access to diagnostic services. The broad ranging resolution recognizes that diagnostic services are vital for the prevention, surveillance, diagnosis, case management, monitoring and treatment of communicable, noncommunicable, neglected tropical and rare diseases, injuries, and disabilities. Diagnostics allow for the precise identification of diseases, and therefore the timely initiation of the correct treatments for better health outcomes. The resolution considers the full spectrum of “diagnostics”, thus including both “in vitro” laboratory tests e.g. rapid diagnostic tests and polymerase chain reaction (PCR) and “non in vitro” diagnostics e.g. imaging or blood pressure measurement devices. It covers actions for research and development, manufacturing (including local production and technology transfer), regulation, selection and procurement, awareness, advocacy and addressing access barriers in general.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

On June 26, the Member States of the World Health Organization adopted a resolution to strengthen disease surveillance in high-capacity countries and to accelerate progress towards the goal of disease surveillance. The various resolutions passed that day noted that disease surveillance is essential for prevention, detection, outbreak response, investigation of incidents, management of adverse events, and the diagnosis of diseases that cause them, including infectious diseases and those that are chronic, disability, and self-harm. Disease surveillance provides a basis for specific disease diagnosis, and therefore, it initiates investigations that are necessary for good health outcomes. The resolution covered all aspects of "disease surveillance," therefore, it included comprehensive assessments for "in-country" knowledge, such as genomic sequencing and DNA fingerprinting for pathogen identification and disease detection "outside the country," such as imaging technologies or predictive risk assessment tools. It also included provisions for training and capacity building, essential services (which include services under the primary health care and referral system), policy, planning and coordination, governance and the integration of all beneficial interventions comprehensively.

translations.source: Ìṣàmúlò ìpinnu náà máa gùn lé yóò sì tún fẹ àwọn iṣẹ́ àtẹ̀yìnwá àti lọ́wọ́lọ́wọ́ ní àwọn ìpele mẹ́tẹ̀ẹ̀ta WHO lójú láti ran àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè lọ́wọ́ láti mú àyípadà bá ànfàní sí ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìdámọ̀ àìsàn. Wọ́n ní kí Ilé-iṣẹ́ ìjọba náà jábọ̀ lórí ìtẹ̀síwájú àmúlò ní ọdún 2025. Ìwé tó fara jọ ọ́ EB152/6 ríro ìkápá ìdámọ̀ àisàn lágbára
translations.reference: The implementation of the resolution will build and expand on previous and current work at three levels of WHO to help countries improve access to diagnostic services. The Secretariat is requested to report on implementation progress in 2025. Related document EB152/6 Strengthening diagnostics capacity
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Yoruba document:

"The implementation of that decision will be accelerated and will also focus on emergency response and at all levels of WHO to assist countries in bringing adaptation to align with the work of disease control. They have called on the Government Agency to prioritize the advancement of implementation by 2025. Document EB152/6 addresses the issue of strong disease control."



**Notes on the translation:**

*   I've aimed for clarity and accuracy while maintaining a natural English flow.
*   Some phrases required interpretation to convey the intended meaning.
*   "Ilé-iṣẹ́ ìjọba" is translated as "Government Agency" as it's the most appropriate equivalent in this context.

translations.source: Àmì-ẹ̀yẹ fún ìgbìyànjú aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ sí ìlera àwùjọ tí wọ́n gbékalẹ̀ lásìkò Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé Kẹrìn-dín-lọ́gọ́rin Lásìkò tí ayẹyẹ ń lọ lọ́wọ́ níbi Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé Kẹrìn-dín-lọ́gọ́rin ní Geneva, wọ́n pín àmì-ẹ̀yẹ fún àwọn ènìyàn àti àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ káàkiri àgbáyé fún ìgbìyànjú aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ wọn sí ìlera àwùjọ. "Ẹ̀bùn àti àmì-ẹ̀yẹ ìlera àwùjọ ọdún 2023 náà gbóríyìn fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n kópa ribiribi tí wọ́n kó nínú ìlera àwùjọ, àti àwọn tí iṣẹ́ wọn ti ré kọjá ìpè fún iṣẹ́ lásán," Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. *Ní àkókò tí gbogbo ayé ń kojú ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ìpèníjà, ìkọ̀ọ̀kan wọn jẹ́ ìwúrí àti ìtanilólobó nípa ìtẹ̀síwájú tí ó ṣe é ṣe láti mú àyípadà bá ìlera àti ìgbáyégbádùn fún gbogbo ènìyàn." Ìpè fún yíyan àwọn akópa fún ẹ̀bùn kọ̀ọ̀kan ni wọ́n máa ń fi ránṣẹ́ síta ní ọdún kọ̀ọ̀kan lẹ́yìn ìparí Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé náà.
translations.reference: Awards for outstanding contributions to public health presented during the Seventy-sixth World Health Assembly During a moving ceremony at the Seventy-sixth World Health Assembly in Geneva, awards were presented to persons and institutions from around the world for their outstanding contributions to public health. “The 2023 public health prizes and awards celebrate people who have made a significant contribution to global health, and whose work has extended far beyond the call of normal duty,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. “At a time when the world faces many challenges, each is an inspiration and a reminder of the progress that can be made to improve health and wellbeing for all.” The call for nominations of candidates for each prize is sent out each year after closure of the World Health Assembly.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Tribute to Outstanding Contributors to Public Health during the 76th World Health Assembly Period**

Following the period of the 76th World Health Assembly, and in recognition of the work carried out during that period in Geneva, the organization presented awards to individuals and institutions around the world for their outstanding contributions to public health. "The 2023 Public Health Award recognizes individuals who have made significant contributions to public health and whose work has gone above and beyond in the area of health security," said Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of the WHO. *“In a time when the world faces many challenges, each of them is a source of inspiration and a reminder of the progress that can be made in combating disease and promoting well-being for all.”* Nominations for the award for each prize are sent out annually after the conclusion of the World Health Assembly.

translations.source: Yíyàn lè jẹ́ ṣíṣe láti ọ̀dọ àwọn alákòso ìlera orílẹ̀-èdè fún Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ WHO àti láti ọ̀dọ ẹni tí ó ti gba ẹ̀bùn náà tẹ́lẹ̀. Níbi ìjókòó 152nd rẹ̀ ní oṣù Ṣẹẹrẹ ọdún 2023, Ìgbìmọ̀ Alàkóso yan àwọn tí wọ́n gba ẹ̀bùn náà ní ọdún 2023, tó dá lórí àwọn àbá tí ìgbìmọ̀ àwọn adájọ́ aṣẹ àṣàyàn tí wọ́n ní àfọkànsìn náà ṣe fún ẹ̀bùn kọ̀ọ̀kan. Wọ́n ti yan àwọn ẹni iyì méjèèje náà láti ṣe àjọyọ̀ ipa aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ wọn àti àfikún wọn sí ìlera àwùjọ ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè wọn àti káàkiri àgbáyé. Wọ́n wá láti Àwọn Ẹkùn agbègbè WHO mẹ́rin - fún àwọn Amẹ́ríkà, Eastern-Mediterranean, Gúúsù Ìlà-oòrùn Asia àti Western Pacific. Àwọn olóríire márùn-ún nínú wọn jẹ́ ẹni kọ̀ọ̀kan, mẹ́ta nínú wọn sì jẹ́ obìnrin.
translations.reference: Nominations can be made by national health administrations of a WHO Member State and by any former recipient of the prizes. At its 152nd session in January 2023, the Executive Board designated the 2023 winners of the prizes, on the basis of proposals made by the dedicated selection panels for each prize. The seven laureates have been selected to celebrate their unique role and contribution for public health in their countries and globally. They come from four WHO Regions – for the Americas, the Eastern-Mediterranean, South-East Asia and the Western Pacific. Five winners are individuals, and three among them are women.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Yoruba text:

"Nominees can come from national public health authorities of WHO member states and from individuals who have previously received the award. At its 152nd session in September 2023, the Board selected the recipients for the 2023 award, based on the recommendations made by the selection committee of experts. They selected seven individuals to recognize their outstanding contribution and dedication to public health in their countries and around the world. They hail from the WHO’s six regions – for the Americas, Eastern Mediterranean, South-East Asia, Western Pacific. Seven of the laureates are men, and three are women."

translations.source: Ní àfikún, àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ méjì tí wọ́n ti gba àmì-ẹ̀yẹ ọdún 2023 ló jẹ́ wí pé obìnrin ló léwájú wọn. Àwọn tí wọ́n gba ẹ̀bùn ọdún 2023 Ẹ̀bùn Ìlera Sasakawa Àwọn ẹni iyì ọdún 2023: Nick Simons Institute láti Nepal, àti Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Vichai Tienthavorn láti Thailand Nick Simons Institute jẹ́ ilé-iṣẹ́ tí kìí ṣe ti ìjọba tó ń gbèrò láti mú àyípadà bá ètò ìlera ní àwọn agbègbè ìgbèríko Nepal. Ó ń ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ pẹ̀lú Ilé-iṣẹ́ Ìjọba tó ń rí sí Ètò Ìlera àti Ònkà ènìyàn ní Nepal, Ilé-iṣẹ́ náà ti kọ́ 7000 àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ ètò ìlera (bíi àwọn agbẹ̀bí àti àwọn amúgbá lẹ́gbẹ̀ apèsè abẹ́rẹ́ apa-ìrora) ní àwọn agbègbè ìgbèríko, tí wọ́n lè pèsè àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ tí wọ́n nílò jùlọ fún àwọn àwùjọ ìbílẹ̀ níbi tí ò bá ti sí orísun ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera àti akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́. Ilé-iṣẹ́ náà ti pèsè àwọn irinṣẹ́ pàtàkì ó sì ti pèsè àfikún àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ ìlera láti mú àdínkù bá àìsí àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ ìlera ní ilé-ìwòsàn 44, ó sì ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àgbékalẹ̀ ètò amúṣẹ́ṣe fún ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ojúlówó tó dára ní àwọn ilé-ìwòsàn ìgbèríko tó lé ní 100.
translations.reference: In addition, the two institutions that have received a 2023 award are led by women. Winners of the 2023 prizes Sasakawa Health PrizeThe 2023 laureates: The Nick Simons Institute from Nepal, and Professor Vichai Tienthavorn from Thailand The Nick Simons Institute is a nongovernmental organization aiming to improve health care in the rural areas of Nepal. Working closely with the Ministry of Health and Population of Nepal, the Institute has trained 7000 medical professionals (such as midwives and anaesthetic assistants) in rural areas, who now provide most-needed services to local communities in the absence of a sufficient pool of general practitioners and specialists. The Institute has supplied essential equipment and provided additional health personnel to alleviate the lack of medical professionals in 44 hospitals, and supported the design of an action plan for better quality services in more than 100 rural hospitals.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Yoruba text:

Furthermore, two organizations that received the 2023 awards were recognized for being led by women. The recipients of the 2023 Sasakawa Health Prize: Nick Simons Institute from Nepal, and Dr. Vichai Tienthavorn from Thailand.

The Nick Simons Institute is a non-governmental organization working to improve health services in remote areas of Nepal. It works closely with the government agency responsible for Health and Human Services in Nepal. The Institute has trained 7,000 health workers (such as community health volunteers and skilled birth attendants) in remote areas, who can address the most pressing needs of local communities where there is a lack of sufficient health workers and volunteers. The Institute has addressed critical shortages and has trained additional health workers to support the workload in 44 health posts, and it provides support for the development of referral systems for quality eye care services in rural health facilities with 100.

translations.source: Ilé-iṣẹ́ náà tún ṣe ìwádìí àti ìgbẹnusọ fún àwọn iṣẹ́ láti yàwòrán ojú ìwòye àti láti dásí àwọn òfin tó kan àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera ìgbèríko ní Nepal. Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Vichai Tienthavorn ni Ààrẹ Praboromarajchanok Institute ní Ilé-iṣẹ́ ìjọba tó ń rí sí Ètò Ìlera àwùjọ ní Thailand. Ọ̀kan lára àwọn ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ jákèjádò-àgbáyé tí ó ti léwájú ni òfin ìpolongo ìlera onímọ̀ ọ̀tun láti báni kojú ìṣòro àìtó hóró ẹ̀jẹ̀ agbẹ̀mí, pẹ̀lú bí iṣẹ́ rẹ̀ ṣe yọrí sí gbígba òfin orílẹ̀-èdè tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ fún ìdíwọ́ àti ìdènà ìṣòro àìtó hóró ẹ̀jẹ̀ agbẹ̀mí wọlé. Ó tún ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ irinṣẹ́ ìbáraẹnisọ̀rọ̀ tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ àti ìlànà àyẹ̀wò láti ran àwọn ará ìlú lọ́wọ́ láti gba ìhùwàsí wọn fún ìdíwọ́ àti ìdènà àtọgbẹ àti ẹ̀jẹ̀ ríru. Nítorí iṣẹ́ yìí, wọ́n gba òfin orílẹ̀-èdè kan wọlé pẹ̀lú ipa rere tó lápẹrẹ lórí ẹrù ààrùn orílẹ̀-èdè.
translations.reference: The Institute also conducts research and advocacy activities to shape perceptions and influence policies that affect rural health workers in Nepal. Professor Vichai Tienthavorn is President of the Praboromarajchanok Institute at the Ministry of Public Health of Thailand. One of the nation-wide initiatives he has led is an innovative health promotion policy to help address thalassemia, with his work resulting in the adoption of an effective national policy for thalassemia prevention and control. He also designed an effective communications tool and screening method to help citizens to adapt their behaviours in order to prevent and control diabetes and hypertension. As a result of this work, a national policy was adopted with significant positive impact on the country’s disease burden.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"The organization also conducted research and advocacy for projects to examine the impact of eye disease and to address the policies affecting community health workers in Nepal. Mr. Vichai Tienthavorn, the President of the Praboromarajchanok Institute, a government agency overseeing the Universal Health Coverage program in Thailand, is one of the key international collaborators. One of the successful initiatives was the policy advocacy for a specialist doctor to be present to manage severe burns, with his work leading to the adoption of national policies to support and prevent severe burn injuries. He also established a communication network and assessment mechanisms to assist communities in accessing their entitlements for the prevention and treatment of burns and injuries. As a result of this work, a national policy was adopted with a positive impact on the country's disease burden."

translations.source: Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Tienthavorn tún kó ipa pàtàkì nínú kíkojú ìpèníjà pínpín àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera lọ́nà tí ò dọ́gba, pàápàá jùlọ ní àwọn agbègbè ibodè àti ìgbèríko Thailand. Ó léwájú ìdásílẹ̀ àjọṣepọ̀ kan pẹ̀lú àwọn ilé-ẹ̀kọ́ ìgbèríko láti gba àwọn akẹ́kọ̀ọ́ ilé-ẹ̀kọ girama, kọ́ wọn ó sì tún ràn wọ́n lọ́wọ́ láti padà sí ìgbèríko lẹ́yìn tí wọ́n ti parí kọ̀ríkúlọ́mù ètò ìlera tó yára. Ẹ̀bùn United Arab Emirates Health Foundation ọdún 2023 fún ẹni iyì: Dr Maria Asuncion Silvester láti Philippines ní àmì-ẹ̀yẹ Ní àfikún sí jíjẹ́ gbajúgbajà òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera àti oníwádìí, Dr Maria Asuncion Silvester jẹ́ agbẹnusọ fún ògédé ìfọ́mọlọ́yàn tọkàn-tọkàn àti olùdásílẹ̀ Kalusugan ng Mag-Ina (Ìlera Ìyá àti Ọmọ) - Ilé-iṣẹ́ tí kìí ṣe ti ìjọba ní orílẹ̀-èdè Philippine. Dr Silvester ti ṣàmúlò ìrírí rẹ̀ gẹ́gẹ́ bíi òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera láwùjọ láti ṣe ìwádìí ó sì ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìlànà láti mú àyípadà bá ìlera àwọn ìyá àti àwọn ọmọ tuntun. Ìlànà àkọ́kọ́ tí ó pèsè ṣàpèjúwe ètò amúṣẹ́ṣe tó rọrùn tí àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ètò ìlera tí wọ́n ń dá àwọn ìyá lóhùn lásìkò ìbímọ àti ọmọ rẹ̀ lẹ́yìn ìbímọ yóò ṣe.
translations.reference: Professor Tienthavorn also played an essential role in addressing the challenge of uneven distribution of the health workforce, especially in border and rural areas of Thailand. He led the establishment of partnerships with village schools to recruit high-school students, train them and help them settle back locally after having accomplished an accelerated medical curriculum. United Arab Emirates Health Foundation Prize2023 award laureate: Dr Maria Asuncion Silvestre from the PhilippinesIn addition to being a renowned clinician and researcher, Dr Maria Asuncion Silvestre is a passionate advocate for exclusive breastfeeding and the founder of Kalusugan ng Mag-Ina (Health of Mother and Child) – a Philippine non-governmental organization. Dr Silvestre has leveraged her experience as a community practitioner to conduct research and design a protocol to help improve the health of mothers and newborns. The pioneering protocol she created describes a simple set of actions to be undertaken by health workers attending a mother during delivery and her newborn after birth.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

Tienthavorn's respected figure played a crucial role in the development of specialized healthcare services for healthcare workers, especially in remote and underserved areas of Thailand. She spearheaded the establishment of a partnership with nursing schools to recruit high school students, train them, and then encourage them to return to nursing in their communities after completing a fast-track healthcare program. The United Arab Emirates Health Foundation 2023 Humanitarian Award was given to: Dr. Maria Asuncion Silvester from the Philippines. In addition to her extensive work as a healthcare professional and volunteer, Dr. Maria Asuncion Silvester is a champion for tokenized philanthropy and the founder of Kalusugan ng Mag-Ina (Mother and Child Health) - a non-governmental organization in the Philippines. Dr. Silvester has consistently demonstrated her dedication as a healthcare worker, providing medical services and developing strategies to improve the health of mothers and newborns. The initial program she designed outlines a simple counseling program that healthcare workers providing care to mothers during pregnancy and their children after birth will implement.

translations.source: Ìlànà yìí, tí ó ṣe é lò ní gbogbo ààyè ìtọ́jú, ń pèsè ìrànlọ́wọ́ láti dí àlàfo ìdọ́gba ìlera nínú ìtọ́jú ìyá-àti-ọmọ. Ní àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú ìjọba orílẹ̀-èdè Philippines bákan náà ni àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 17 ní Ẹkùn Agbègbè Western Pacific WHO, ó ti kó ipa pàtàkì nínú fífẹ ìpolongo Ìdìmọ́ra Àkọ́kọ́ fún Ìtọ́jú ọmọtuntun Tó Ṣe Pàtàkì ní Ìbẹ̀rẹ̀ lójú - ètò àwọn ìdásí tó rọrùn tí WHO dábàá tí kò ga ju ara lọ. Ẹ̀bùn Ọlọ́lá Jùlọ Sheikh Sabah Al-Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah fún Ìwádìí nínú Ètò Ìlera fún Àwọn Arúgbó àti nínú Ìpolongo Ìlera Àwọn ẹni iyì àmì-ẹ̀yẹ náà fún ọdún 2023: Ààyè Orílẹ̀-èdè fún Ààrùn tó Lágbára tí Kìí ràn láti China; àti Dr Alba Mehio Sibai láti Lebanon Ààyè Orílẹ̀-èdè fún Ìdíwọ́ àti Ìdènà Ààrùn tó Lágbára tí Kìí ràn, apá kan lára Ààyè fún Ìdíwọ́ àti Ìdènà Ààrùn Chinese (China CDC), ti gbàlejò ẹ̀ka kan tó ń gbájúmọ́ ìlera àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ti dàgbà láti ọdún 2013.
translations.reference: This protocol, which is applicable in all resource settings, helps to bridge the health equity gap in mother-and-child care. In partnership with the national government of the Philippines as well as 17 countries in the WHO Western Pacific Region, she has played a key role in scaling up the First Embrace campaign for Early Essential Newborn Care – a set of simple WHO-recommended and cost-effective interventions. His Highness Sheikh Sabah Al-Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah Prize for Research in Health Care for the Elderly and in Health Promotion 2023 award laureates: The National Center for Chronic and Noncommunicable Disease Control and Prevention from China; and Dr Abla Mehio Sibai from Lebanon The National Center for Chronic and Noncommunicable Disease Control and Prevention, part of the Chinese Center for Disease Control and Prevention (China CDC), has hosted a department focusing on the health of older people since 2013.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"This strategy, which is being implemented across all areas of surveillance, aims to provide support for strengthening disease prevention within maternal and child health surveillance. In partnership with the government of the Philippines, the 17 countries of the WHO Western Pacific Region have played a crucial role in implementing the Integrated Morbidity Reporting for Newborns and Children at Risk program at the community level – a simple program of WHO interventions that is not overly complex. The Sheikh Sabah Al-Ahmad Al-Jaber Al-Sabah Prize for Health in Elderly Care and in the Health Program for People with Disabilities for 2023: Country Award for Combating the Pandemic that Originated in China; and Dr. Alba Mehio Sibai from Lebanon Country Award for Prevention and Control of the Pandemic that Originated, a part of the Award for Prevention and Control of Chinese Diseases (China CDC), has addressed a common health issue among the elderly since 2013."

translations.source: Ó ń pèsè àwọn ètò lóriṣiríṣi àti iṣẹ́ tí ó ń mú ìlọsíwájú bá ìpolongo ìlera fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ti dàgbà, bẹ̀rẹ̀ láti nínú òye tó dára nípa àwọn aṣokùnfa àìsàn àti ikú nípasẹ ìtọpinpin fún àwọn ààrùn tí kìí ràn, ìwádìí, àwọn àkọsílẹ̀ ikú àti ìwádìí, fún àwọn ètò tí wọ́n ṣe láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ìmọ̀ ìlera àti àwọn òfin adálórí-ẹ̀rí. Lára ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ náà ni irinṣẹ́ tí wọ́n fi sọrí àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ti dàgbà, ìdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ètò ìlera alábọ́dé 2000, ọ̀sẹ̀ ìgbẹnusọ fún ìlera tí wọ́n ṣe fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ti dàgbà, àti iṣẹ́ àkànṣe tuntun láti mú àyípadà bá ìlera ọpọlọ àti òye fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ti dàgbà tí wọ́n lé ní ìdajì mílíọ̀nù ní àwọn ìlú àti ìgbèríko. Dr Abla Mehio Sibai jẹ́ Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n nípa Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn àti Díínì Ẹ̀ka-ẹ̀kọ́ Àwọn Sáyẹ́nsì Ìlera ní American University of Beirut ní Lebanon. Ó ti kó ipa aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ sí ìlera àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ti dàgbà ní Lebanon àti ní ẹkùn agbègbè náà, ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìgbésẹ̀ àpapọ̀ fún àtìlẹ́yìn ìlera àwọn arúgbó. Ó léwájú ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ ìmọ̀-ọ̀tunláti ṣe ìforúkọsílẹ̀ àwọn ènìyàn tó ti dàgbà tí wọ́n tó 600 ní ọdọọdún ní Yunifásítì fún Àwọn Àgbàlagbà ní American University of Beirut, ní àfikún sí kíkọ́ àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ ìlera àwùjọ.
translations.reference: It delivers a broad range of programmes and activities that advance health promotion for older people, ranging from better understanding the causes of morbidity and mortality through national surveillance for noncommunicable diseases, surveys, death registries and research, to tailored programmes to promote health literacy and evidence-based policies. Initiatives include a dedicated toolkit for older people, capacity‑building of 2000 primary care staff, a health advocacy week dedicated to older people, and an innovative project to improve the mental and cognitive health of more than half a million older persons in both urban and rural areas. Dr Abla Mehio Sibai is Professor of Epidemiology and Dean of the Faculty of Health Sciences at the American University of Beirut in Lebanon. She has made an outstanding contribution to older people’s health in Lebanon and the region, developing a holistic approach to the promotion of healthy ageing. She led the innovative initiative of enrolling close to 600 older people every year at the University for Seniors at the American University of Beirut, in addition to training public health professionals.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"It addresses various programs and initiatives that promote health development for older adults, starting with a better understanding of the causes of illness and death through the identification of preventable diseases, disability, causes of death and disability, for programs designed to support health knowledge and social welfare policies. Among these initiatives are the training of older adults, the expansion of health program workers since 2000, weekly health sessions for older adults, and innovative interventions to promote health and well-being for older adults who number in the millions in cities and rural areas. Dr. Abla Mehio Sibai is a Professor of Geriatrics and Clinical Gerontology at the Faculty of Health Sciences at the American University of Beirut in Lebanon. She has played a significant role in the health of older adults in Lebanon and the surrounding region, establishing a collaborative platform for supporting the health of older adults. She led an initiative to register older adults aged 60 and over at the University for Seniors at the American University of Beirut, in addition to developing community health referral systems."

translations.source: Ó pẹ̀lú jọ léwájú Ìṣòro Àgbáyé WHO fún Ìwádìí Ààrùn tó lápẹẹrẹ ní Lebanon (2000) ó sì ṣe ìwádìí orílẹ̀-èdè àkọ́kọ́ lórí àwọn ààrùn tí kìí ràn àti àwọn aṣokùnfà ewu, ṣíṣe agbẹnusọ, òfin-ṣíṣe àti ṣíṣe ètò fún ìpolongo ìlera. Gígùn lórí ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn àṣeyọrí rẹ̀, Dr Sibai ti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ "ìlànà-iṣẹ́ Orílẹ̀-èdè fún Àwọn tí wọ́n ti dàgbà" ní Lebanon (2020-2030). Dr Sibai tún ti dá ojú-òpóArúgbó Árábù àti Ààyè fún Ìwádìí lórí Arúgbó sílẹ̀ láti ṣe ìdásílẹ̀ àjọṣepọ̀ láàrín ìwádìí àti òfin ní àwọn ìpele orílẹ̀-èdè, ẹkùn agbègbè àti ilẹ̀ òkèèrè. Ẹ̀bùn Ìrántí Dr LEE Jong-wook fún Ìlera àwùjọ Àwọn ẹni iyì àmì-ẹ̀yẹ náà fún ọdún 2023: Dr Jorge Francisco Meneses láti Guatemala
translations.reference: She co-led the landmark WHO Global Burden of Disease Study in Lebanon (2000) and conducted the first national survey on noncommunicable diseases and risk factors, informing advocacy, policy-making and programme planning for health promotion. Building on her numerous achievements, Dr Sibai has developed the “National Strategy for Older Persons” in Lebanon (2020–2030). Dr Sibai also created the Arab Ageing Network and the Centre for Studies on Ageing to create links between research and policy at the national, regional and international levels. Dr LEE Jong-wook Memorial Prize for Public Health 2023 award laureate: Dr Jorge Francisco Meneses from Guatemala
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Yoruba document:

With the support of the WHO's Global Health Emergency Response, he responded to the Beirut explosion in Lebanon (2020) and conducted national assessments of diseases that cause morbidity and mortality, risk factors, operational and program management for health planning. Building on many of his successes, Dr Sibai established the "National Strategy for Older Adults" in Lebanon (2020-2030). Dr Sibai also founded the Arab and International Geriatric Institute for Research to promote collaboration between health and policy at the national, regional, and international levels. The Dr. LEE Jong-wook Prize for Public Health: The awardees for 2023 are Dr. Jorge Francisco Meneses from Guatemala.

translations.source: Lẹ́yìn tí ó ti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ìkópa àwùjọ nínú ìtọ́jú ilé-ìwòsàn fún bíi 20 ọdún, Dr Francisco Meneses ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìgbìmọ̀ adálórí-àwùjọ tó gbájúmọ́ ààbò àti ìtẹ́lọ́rùn àwọn aláìsàn, àwọn ẹbí wọn àti àwùjọ. Pẹ̀lú ipa rere iṣẹ́ rẹ̀ láti mú àyípadà bá ojúlówó ìtọ́jú aláìsàn, àwòṣe ìtọ́jú tó dára adálórí-aláìsàn tí ó ṣe ni wọ́n fẹ̀ lójú ní ìpele orílẹ̀-èdè. Ní àsìkò yìí, gẹ́gẹ́ bíi alámòjútó orílẹ̀-èdè fún àwọn ilé-ìwòsàn ti Guatemala, ó ti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ètò kan láti mú àyípadà bá ojúlówó ìpèsè iṣẹ́ fún àwọn aláìsàn ní ilé-ìwòsàn àti àwọn ẹbí wọn. Dr Meneses ti ṣàkóso iṣẹ́ àkànṣe onímọ̀ tuntun kékeré ní Agbègbè Ìlera Gúúsù Guatemala láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ojú-òpóaláfibọ̀ orílẹ̀-èdè àkọ́kọ́ fún ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera, pẹ̀lú ọ̀rayàn láti gbájúmọ́ àwọn àwùjọ tí wọn kò tọ́jú dáadáa. Iṣẹ́ rẹ̀ ti yọrí sí gbígba ìlànà-iṣẹ́ orílẹ̀-èdè lórí àwọn ojú-òpóìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera aláfibọ̀ wọlé, àkọ́kọ́ rẹ̀ ní Àárín Gbùngbùn ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀.
translations.reference: Having promoted community participation in hospital care for over 20 years, Dr Jorge Francisco Meneses set up community-based committees focusing on the safety and satisfaction of patients, their families and the community. Given the positive impact of his work in improving the quality of patient care, the model of ethical and patient-centred care that he designed was scaled up at the national level. Nowadays, as the national supervisor for hospitals of Guatemala, he has designed a programme to improve the quality of services for hospital patients and their families. Dr Meneses has recently coordinated an innovative pilot project in the Guatemala South Health Area to build the first national integrated network of health services, with a priority focus on underserved communities. His work led to the adoption of a national strategy of integrated health services networks, the first in Central America.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"After dedicating himself to the care of patients within the hospital system for about 20 years, Dr. Francisco Meneses established a leadership council that addresses both the needs and concerns of patients, their families, and the community. Due to his excellent work in improving patient care, a quality leadership model for patient care that he implemented has been recognized at the national level. During this time, he has served as a national consultant for hospitals in Guatemala, and he has created a program to improve the quality of work life for patients in hospitals and their families. Dr. Meneses has managed the training of new junior doctors in the Southern Health Region of Guatemala to establish the first national protocol for healthcare work, with the aim of addressing communities that do not receive adequate care. His work has led to the incorporation of national guidelines on healthcare work protocols, initially in the Central Region of the State of Jalisco."

translations.source: Àmì-ẹ̀yẹ Nelson Mandela fún Ìpolongo Ìlera Ẹni iyì àmì-ẹ̀yẹ náà fún ọdún 2023: Dr Mariam Athbi Al Jalahma láti Bahrain Ní àfikún sí jíjẹ́ Olórí Aláṣẹ fún Àwọn Aláṣẹ Aṣòfin Ìlera Orílẹ̀-èdè ti Bahrain, Dr Mariam Athbi Al Jalahma ti di oríṣiríṣi ipò asíwájú mú nínú ìjọba àti ní àwọn ilé-ẹ̀kọ́ ní orílẹ̀-èdè rẹ̀. Dr Al Jalahma ti kó ipa pàtàkì nínú ìdàgbàsókè oríṣiríṣi àwọn ìlànà-iṣẹ́ àti ètò orílẹ̀-èdè àti káàkiri ẹkùn agbègbè ní ẹ̀ka ètò ìlera alábọ́dé, ìlera ọmọ àti ọ̀dọ́, àwọn ààrùn tó lágbára àti àìmu-sìgá. Ní ipò rẹ̀ lọ́wọ́lọ́wọ́, Dr Al Jalahma ti léwájú àwọn ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ aláṣeyọrí tó ṣe pàtàkì, bíi ètò àyẹ̀wò orílẹ̀-èdè fún àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ ìlera, àyípadà oní àkósódù fún Àwọn Aláṣẹ Aṣòfin Ìlera Orílẹ̀-èdè, àti oríṣiríṣi ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ tí ó ń yọrí sí àlékún iṣẹ́ àti àṣeyọrí ní ẹ̀ka ètò ìlera.
translations.reference: Nelson Mandela Award for Health Promotion 2023 award laureate: Dr Mariam Athbi Al Jalahma from Bahrain In addition to being the Chief Executive Officer of the National Health Regulatory Authority of Bahrain, Dr Mariam Athbi Al Jalahma has held various leadership positions in government and academic institutions in her country. Dr Al Jalahma has played a major role in the development of numerous national and region-wide strategies and plans in the field of primary healthcare, child and adolescent health, chronic diseases and antismoking. In her current role, Dr Al Jalahma has led major successful initiatives, such as the national accreditation system for health institutions, the digital transformation of the National Health Regulatory Authority, and several initiatives leading to increased efficiency and excellence in the health sector.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Yoruba text:

**Nelson Mandela Health Leadership Award for 2023: Dr. Mariam Athbi Al Jalahma from Bahrain**

In addition to being the President of the Bahraini Health Ministry's Pharmaceutical Services Council, Dr. Mariam Athbi Al Jalahma has held various leadership positions within government and educational institutions in her country. Dr. Al Jalahma has played a significant role in the development of various national and regional policies and programs in the areas of primary healthcare, child and adolescent health, infectious diseases, and non-communicable diseases.

In her various roles, Dr. Al Jalahma has spearheaded successful initiatives that have been crucial, such as a national accreditation program for healthcare facilities, the development of a code of ethics for the Pharmaceutical Services Council, and various initiatives that lead to efficiency and success in the healthcare sector.

translations.source: Ó tún jẹ́ ohùn tó gbajúgbajà fún ìpolongo ìlera káàkiri oríṣiríṣi ojú-òpójákèjádò gbogbo àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè Ìgbìmọ̀ Ìfowópamọ́ Ọ̀gbun.
translations.reference: She is also a prominent voice for health promotion across various channels throughout all countries of the Gulf Cooperation Council.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Yoruba sentence:

"It is also a significant contribution to the health policies surrounding various eye diseases across all the member states of the United Nations."

**Explanation of the translation choices:**

*   **Ó tún jẹ́:** "It is also"
*   **ohùn tó gbajúgbajà:** "a significant contribution" (literally, "a contribution that is great")
*   **fún ìpolongo ìlera:** "to the health policies"
*   **káàkiri oríṣiríṣi ojú-òpójákèjádò:** "surrounding various eye diseases"
*   **gbogbo àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè:** "across all the member states"
*   **Ìgbìmọ̀ Ìfowópamọ́ Ọ̀gbun:** "United Nations" (literally, "Assembly of Nations")



I hope this is helpful!

translations.source: WHO àti Republic of Korea buwọ́lu àdéhùn tó lápẹrẹ láti mú àlékún bá ìkápá ìpèsè ajẹmọ́-ìbí WHO àti Republic of Korea ti tọwọ́ bọ Ìwé Àdéhùn Àsọyépọ̀ láti ṣe ìdásílẹ̀ ibùdó ìdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ àgbáyé fún ìpèsè ajẹmọ́-ìbí. Ààyè ìdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ àgbáyé náà máa ṣiṣẹ́ sin gbogbo àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ìpawówọlé wọn kéré àti agbede-méjì (LMICs) láti pèsè àwọn ajẹmọ́-ìbí, bíi àjẹsára, insulínì, àkàndá sójà ara àti àwọn oògùn ààrùn jẹjẹrẹ. Àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 ṣàfihàn ìdí láti fẹ ìpèsè àjẹsára lábẹ́lé àti ní ẹkùn agbègbè àti àwọn ohun èlò ìlera mìíràn tó ṣe pàtàkì nínú LMICs láti ṣe àrídájú ànfààní tó dọ́gba lójú. Láti pèsè ìmọ̀ọ́ṣe fún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè láti lè tètè pèse àjẹsára mRNA, WHO, ní àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn alájọṣepọ̀, ṣe ìdásílẹ̀ ètò Ìfiránṣẹ́ Ìmọ̀ Ẹ̀rọ nRNA, pẹ̀lú ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ilé-ìwòsàn ibùdó ní South Africa ní oṣù Igbe ọdún 2023.
translations.reference: WHO and Republic of Korea sign landmark agreement to boost biomanufacturing capacity WHO and the Republic of Korea have signed a Memorandum of Understanding to establish a global training hub in biomanufacturing. This global training centre will serve all low- and middle-income countries (LMICs) to produce biologicals, such as vaccines, insulin, monoclonal antibodies, and cancer medicines. The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted the need to expand the local and regional production of vaccines and other essential health products in LMICs to ensure equitable access. To provide countries with the know-how to rapidly produce mRNA vaccines, WHO, in collaboration with partners, established the mRNA Technology Transfer programme, with the formal inauguration of the Hub facility in South Africa in April 2023.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Yoruba text:

The World Health Organization (WHO) and the Republic of Korea have established a partnership to advance the WHO and Republic of Korea’s Joint Initiative on Maternal and Newborn Health. The global research agenda will focus on addressing research gaps for maternal and newborn health. The global research initiative will serve all low-income and middle-income countries (LMICs) to address maternal health issues, such as caesarean sections, insulin, safe surgical care, and essential medicines. The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted the need to strengthen routine immunization services and other essential health commodities in LMICs to address the increased burden. To build capacity for countries to assess mRNA vaccines, WHO, in partnership with partners, established the mRNA Technology Transfer Programme, with the launch of a pilot training center in South Africa in September 2023.

translations.source: Ìgbésẹ̀ oríṣiríṣi yìí àti àwọn ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ ìpèsè lábẹ́lẹ́ mìíràn láìpẹ́ yìí pè fún ìlànà-iṣẹ́ ìdagbàsókè àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ aláfọkànsìn fún ìgbaniwọlé, ìdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ àti láti tẹ̀síwájú pẹ̀lú àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ ní àwọn ilé-ìwòsàn wọ̀nyìí, pẹ̀lú ìmọ̀ọ́ṣe tó yàtọ̀ tí ó ṣe pàtàkì nínú ìpèsè ajẹmọ́-ìbí. Láti wá iyànjú sí àlàfo yìí, WHO àti Republic of Korea ti kópa nínú àjọṣepọ̀ abẹ́lé láti bí ọdún kan wọ́n sì ti ń sọ ìfọkànsìn wọn fún ìdásílẹ̀ ibùdó ìdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ àgbáyé fún ajẹmọ́-ìbí di tòótọ́. "Inú wa dùn láti buwọ́lù Ìwé Àdéhùn Àsọyépọ̀ yìí láti gbé àjọṣepọ̀ wa lọ sí ìpele tí ó kàn kí á sì ní àjọṣepọ̀ nínú àgbékalẹ̀ kọ̀ríkúlọ́mù Ìdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ kíkún. A lérò wí pé ibùdó ìdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ tó dára jùlọ lágbàyé yìí máa ràn wá lọ́wọ́ láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ tí wọ́n nímọ̀ àti láti ríi dájú pé àwọn ohun èlò ìlera tó jẹ́ ojúlówó lè di ṣíṣe káàkiri àgbáyé, Dr Yukiko Nakatani, Igbákejì Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO, Ànfààní sí Àwọn Oògùn àti Àwọn Ohun Èlò Ìlera. Ìjọba orílẹ̀-èdè Republic of Korea ń ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ilé-ìwòsàn ìdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ orílẹ̀-èdè àti ètò lóriṣiríṣi tó kárí gbogbo ìpele ìpèsè ajẹmọ́-ìbí tó sì ń fẹ iṣẹ́ rẹ̀ lójú láti ṣe àfikún ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn akẹ́kọ̀ọ́ láti àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè mìíràn pẹ̀lú kíkọ́ Ọgbà Ilé-ẹ̀kọ́ Ìbí Àgbáyé tí wọn yóò ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ rẹ̀ ní ọdún 2025.
translations.reference: This multilateral effort and other recent local production initiatives call for ambitious workforce development strategies to recruit, upskill and retain the staff working at these facilities, with unique competencies required in bioproduction. To address this gap, WHO and the Republic of Korea have engaged in closed collaboration for over a year and are now formalizing their commitment towards the establishment of a global training hub in biomanufacturing. “We are delighted to sign this Memorandum of Understanding to elevate our partnership to the next level and collaborate in the development of a comprehensive training curriculum. We hope this world-class training hub will help to build a skilled workforce and ensure quality health products can be manufactured around the world,” said Dr Yukiko Nakatani, WHO Assistant Director-General, Access to Medicines and Health Products. The Government of the Republic of Korea is offering a large range of national training facilities and programmes covering the full spectrum of biological manufacturing and is expanding its operations to accommodate more trainees from other countries with the construction of a Global Bio Education Campus to be launched in 2025.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"These various steps and the foundations of healthcare under this initiative call for a work plan to strengthen the capacity of healthcare workers for maternal, newborn, and child health, and to advance with those working in these hospitals, with the necessary expertise that is crucial in newborn care. To achieve this goal, WHO and the Republic of Korea have partnered in a multi-year collaboration and have been advocating for the full implementation of the global newborn health hub. "We are delighted to launch this Compendium of Best Practices to take our partnership to the next level, requiring us to have a partnership in the development of a comprehensive Newborn Health Curriculum. We believe that the best newborn health hub globally will be instrumental in building a workforce with knowledge and ensuring that essential health products are accessible worldwide," said Dr. Yukiko Nakatani, WHO Assistant Director-General for Access to Medicines and Health Products. The Republic of Korea government is developing national newborn health facilities and various programs across all levels of maternal health care and is committed to expanding the number of partners from other countries, including the launch of the Global Maternal and Newborn Learning Hub in 2025."

translations.source: Mo lérò pé Àdéhùn Àsọyépọ̀ yìí máa ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ìfọwọ́sowọ́pọ̀ láàrín Republic of Korea àti WHO láti ró ìkápá ìpèsè ajẹmọ́-ìbí fún LMICs lágbára," Mr Hwang Seunghyun Olùdarí Àpapọ̀, Ọ́fíísì Ibùdó Àjẹsára Àgbáyé ní Ilé-iṣẹ́ Ìjọba tó ń rí sí Ètò Ìlera àti Ìgbáyégbádùn láti Republic of Korea. Ní àfiwé, WHO ń ṣe àtúnṣe sí ìrólágbára ètò òfin láti rí i dájú pé ojúlówó àti ààbò àwọn ohun èlò ìlera wà. Àwọn àjọ alámòjútó tó lágbára máa tún mú kí ìgbàgbọ́ wà nínú àwọn ohun èlò tí wọ́n ṣe ní abẹ́lé tí yóò sì gbógun ti ìròyìn ẹlẹ́jẹ̀ àti wiwà àwọn oògùn tí kìí ṣe ojúlówó tàbí ayédèrú tó léwu.
translations.reference: “I hope this Memorandum of Understanding will enhance cooperation between the Republic of Korea and WHO to strengthen biomanufacturing capacities of LMICs,” said Mr Hwang Seunghyun Director General, Global Vaccine Hub Office at the Ministry of Health and Welfare from Republic of Korea. In parallel, WHO is intensifying regulatory system strengthening to ensure the quality and safety of health products. Stronger regulatory agencies will also enhance confidence in locally produced products and counter misinformation and the availability of unsafe substandard or falsified medicines.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"I believe that this Memorandum of Understanding will serve as a support for collaboration between the Republic of Korea and the WHO to address the challenge of maternal health for LMICs (Low and Middle-Income Countries)," said Mr. Hwang Seunghyun, Director General, Health and Welfare Diplomacy Division, Ministry of Foreign Affairs of the Republic of Korea. According to the statement, WHO is strengthening its regulatory systems to ensure the safety and quality of medical supplies. Strong civil society organizations will also be encouraged to have a voice in the selection of medical supplies, which will further strengthen the monitoring of medicines and the quality of vaccines that are safe and effective.

translations.source: WHO rọ àwọn ìjọba láti dáwọ́ owó ìrànwọ́ lórí àwọn ọ̀gbin tábà tó lè sẹku pani dúró Ní Àyájọ́ Ọjọ́ Kò Sí Tábà, WHO rọ àwọn ìjọba láti dáwọ́ ìpèsè owó ìrànwọ́ fún dídáko tábà kí wọ́n sì ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ọ̀pọ̀ àwọn ohun ọ̀gbìn alálòpẹ́ tí ó lè bọ́ àwọn mílíọ̀nù. "Tábà ni okùnfà fún mílíọ̀nù 8 ikú ní ọdún kan, síbẹ̀ àwọn ìjọba káàkiri ayé ń ná mílíọ̀nù láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún oko tábà," Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO. "Pẹ̀lu yíyan láti gbin oúnjẹ dípò tábà, a ti mú ìlera lọ́kùkúdùn, tọ́jú àwùjọ àwọn abẹ̀mí, àti ìrólágbára fún ààbò oúnjẹ fún gbogbo ènìyàn." Ó lé ní mílíọ̀nù 300 àwọn ènìyàn lágbàyé tí wọ́n ń kojú àìsí ààbò oúnjẹ tó lágbára.
translations.reference: WHO urges governments to stop subsidizing life-threatening tobacco crops On World No Tobacco Day, WHO urges governments to stop subsidizing tobacco farming and support more sustainable crops that could feed millions. “Tobacco is responsible for 8 million deaths a year, yet governments across the world spend millions supporting tobacco farms,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. “By choosing to grow food instead of tobacco, we prioritize health, preserve ecosystems, and strengthen food security for all.” More than 300 million people globally are faced with acute food insecurity.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Yoruba document:

WHO urges governments to allocate funding for tobacco control on products that can be taxed. At the No Tobacco Day, WHO urges governments to allocate a portion of tobacco revenue for prevention programs and to ensure support for numerous tobacco-related diseases that can affect millions. "Tobacco is a killer for 8 million people each year, yet governments around the world are allowing tobacco companies to profit," said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. "With the opportunity to tax unhealthy products instead of tobacco, we can improve health, reduce poverty, and increase food security for everyone." He noted that 300 million people worldwide are suffering from severe food insecurity.

translations.source: Ní báyìí ó lé ní mílíọ̀nù 3 saare ilẹ̀ káàkiri àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tó lé ní 120 tí wọ́n ń lò láti gbin tábà aṣekúpani, kódà ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè níbi tí ebi ti ń pa àwọn ènìyàn. Ìjábọ̀ tuntun WHO kan, "Gbin oúnjẹ, kìí ṣe tábà," ṣàfihàn àwọn aburú ọ̀gbin tábà àti àwọn ànfààní ṣíṣẹ́rí padà sí àwọn ọ̀gbin oúnjẹ alálòpẹ́ tó pọ̀ fún àwọn àgbẹ̀, àwọn àwùjọ, ọrọ̀ ajé, agbègbè, àti gbogbo ayé lápapọ̀. Ìjábọ̀ náà tún tù àṣírí ẹ̀ka ilé-iṣẹ́ tábà fún fífi àwọn àgbẹ̀ pamọ́ sínú gbèsè àìlópin, ṣíṣe ìpolongo fún ọ̀gbin tábà pẹ̀lú àsọdùn àwọn ànfààní ọrọ̀-ajérẹ̀ àti bíbẹ̀bẹ̀ nípasẹ̀ àwọn ẹgbẹ́ àgbẹ̀ ẹlẹ́yinjú àánú. Ọ̀gbin tábà ń fa ààrùn fún àwọn àgbẹ̀ fúnra wọn bẹ́ẹ̀ ó lé ní mílíọ̀nù 1 àwọn ọmọdé tí wọ́n ń ṣe òwò ẹrú tí wọ́n ṣírò pé wọ́n ń ṣiṣẹ́ lórí àwọn oko tábà, tí wọ́n sì ń pàdánù ànfààní wọn láti ní ẹ̀kọ́ ìwé. "Tábà kìí ṣe ewu ńlá fún ààbò oúnjẹ nìkan, ṣùgbọ́n fún àpapọ̀ ìlera, tó fi mọ́ ìlera àwọn àgbẹ̀ oní tábà.
translations.reference: Meanwhile more than 3 million hectares of land across more than 120 countries are being used to grow deadly tobacco, even in countries where people are starving. A new WHO report, “Grow food, not tobacco,” highlights the ills of tobacco growing and the benefits of switching to more sustainable food crops for farmers, communities, economies, the environment, and the world at large. The report also exposes the tobacco industry for trapping farmers in a vicious cycle of debt, propagating tobacco growing by exaggerating its economic benefits and lobbying through farming front groups. Tobacco farming causes diseases to the farmers themselves and more than 1 million child laborers are estimated to be working on tobacco farms, missing their opportunity for an education. “Tobacco is not only a massive threat to food insecurity, but health overall, including the health of tobacco farmers.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Currently, there are 3 million tobacco-related deaths worldwide among countries with 120 that use it to combat malnutrition, even in countries where disease is killing people. A new WHO report, "Food First, Not Tobacco," highlights the harms of tobacco farming and shifts benefits towards food systems that greatly benefit farmers, communities, economies, regions, and the entire world. The report also advocates for the tobacco industry to be integrated into the poverty reduction program, implementing tobacco farming with the benefits of economic opportunities and stability through farmer cooperatives. Tobacco farming causes illness for farmers and has 1 million children working as child laborers who are reportedly working on tobacco farms, depriving them of the benefits of education. "Tobacco is not only a major threat to food security but also to public health, impacting the health of tobacco farmers."

translations.source: Ó ń gbé àwọn àgbẹ̀ síta sí àwọn oògùn apa kòkòro oní kẹ́míkà, èéfín tábà àti iye èròjà tábà tí a lè rí nínú sìgá 50 - tó ń yọrí sí àìsàn bíi ààrùn ẹdọ̀fóró tó lágbára àti májèlé èròjà tábà," Dr Ruediger Krech, Olùdarí Ìpolongo Ìlera ní WHO ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. Ọ̀gbin tábà jẹ́ ìṣòro àgbáyé. Àfojúsùn náà ti wà tipẹ́ ní Asia àti South America, ṣùgbọ́n àwọn dátà tuntun fi hàn pé àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ tábà ń gbòòrò si sí ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀. Láti ọdún 2005, ó ti tó bíi àlékún 20% nínú oko ọ̀gbin tábà tó ti wà káàkiri ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀. WHO, Ilé-Iṣẹ́ Nǹkan Ọ̀gbìn àti Oúnjẹ àti Ètò Oúnjẹ Àgbáyé ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ Àwọn Oko Aláìní Tábà tí yóò pèsè ìrànlọ́wọ́ fún 5000 àwọn àgbẹ̀ ní orílẹ̀-èdè Kenya àti Zambia láti gbin èso oúnjẹ alálòpẹ́ dípò tábà.
translations.reference: Farmers are exposed to chemical pesticides, tobacco smoke and as much nicotine as found in 50 cigarettes – leading to illnesses like chronic lung conditions and nicotine poisoning,” said Dr Ruediger Krech, Director of Health Promotion at WHO. Tobacco growing is a global problem. The focus has so far been in Asia and South America, but the latest data show tobacco companies are expanding to Africa. Since 2005, there has been a nearly 20% increase in tobacco farming land across Africa. WHO, the Food and Agriculture Organization and the World Food Programme support the Tobacco Free Farms initiative that will provide help to more than 5000 farmers in Kenya and Zambia to grow sustainable food crops instead of tobacco.

Tobacco cultivation is a global problem. The warning is particularly prevalent in Asia and South America, but new data shows that tobacco companies are expanding into sub-Saharan Africa. Since 2005, it has accounted for about 20% of the global tobacco crop. WHO, the World Health Organization and the World Food Programme are supporting the establishment of Tobacco-Free Farms, which will provide support for 5,000 farmers in Kenya and Zambia to grow alternative food crops instead of tobacco. translations.source: Ní ọdọọdún Àyájọ́ Ọjọ́ Kò Sí Tábà máa ń bu ọlá fún àwọn tí wọ́n ń mú àyípadà bá ìdènà tábà. Ní ọdún yìí ọ̀kan nínú àwọn alámì-ẹ̀yẹ, Ms Sprina Robi Chacha, àgbẹ̀ obìnrin láti orílẹ̀-èdè Kenya, ni wọ́n ń fún ní àmì-ẹ̀yẹ fún ìṣípòrọpò láti ọ̀gbin tábà sí àwọn ẹ̀wà oní purotéénì púpọ̀ nìkan, ṣùgbọ́n fún kíkọ́ ọgọ́rùn lọ́na ọgọ́rùn àwọn àgbẹ̀ tí ó kù lórí bí wọ́n ṣe lè ṣe èyí láti pèsè àwùjọ tí ní àlàáfíà. Àwọn ẹgbẹ́ 182 níbi Àpérò ìlànà-iṣẹ́ WHO lórí Ìdènà Tábà ti ní àfọkànsìn láti "... ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn àfidípò tó dára fún ọrọ̀-ajéfún àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ àti àwọn tó ń gbin tábà". Ọ̀nà kan tó ṣe pàtàkì tí àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè lè mú ojúṣe yìí wá sí ìmúṣẹ ni fífi òpin sí owó ìrànwọ́ fún ọ̀gbin tábà àti ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn èso tó ní àlàáfíà. Pẹ̀lu yíyan láti gbin oúnjẹ dípò tábà, a ti mú ìlera lọ́kùkúdùn, dáàbò bo àwùjọ abẹ̀mí, àti àlékún ààbò oúnjẹ.
translations.reference: Every year World No Tobacco Day honours those making a difference in tobacco control. This year one of the Awardees, Ms Sprina Robi Chacha, a female farmer from Kenya, is being recognized for not only switching from growing tobacco to high protein beans, but also training hundreds of other farmers on how to do this to create a healthier community. 182 Parties to the WHO Framework Convention on Tobacco Control have committed to “...promote economically viable alternatives for tobacco workers and growers”. A crucial way that countries can fulfill this obligation is by ending subsidies for tobacco growing and supporting healthier crops. By choosing to grow food instead of tobacco, we prioritize health, preserve ecosystems, and increase food security.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"In the Year of Action for Tobacco Control, recognition is given to those who are taking steps to reduce tobacco use. This year, one of the awardees, Ms. Sprina Robi Chacha, a female farmer from Kenya, is being honored for her initiative to transition from tobacco farming to cultivating various protein-rich crops, but also for training nearly one hundred farmers on how to do this sustainably. 182 countries under the WHO Framework Convention on Tobacco Control have committed to "...provide support for effective cessation interventions for tobacco workers and farmers." One crucial way countries can achieve this goal is by redirecting tobacco funds for tobacco farming and providing support for those with addiction. With the shift towards eating nutritious food, there has been an increase in well-being, a reduction in household poverty, and improved food security."

translations.source: Ìjábọ̀ tó lápẹẹrẹ ya ojú ọ̀nà fún ṣíṣe àtúntò àwọn ọrọ̀ ajé láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn Nínú ìjábọ̀ àkọ́kọ́-irú-rẹ̀, Ìgbìmọ WHO lórí Àwọn ọrọ̀-ajéÌlera fún Gbogbo Ènìyàn ti ṣe àlàkalẹ̀ ọ̀nà tuntun láti ṣe àtúntò àwọn ọrọ̀-ajéláti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn nǹkan tó ṣe kókó - ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn. Ìgbìmọ̀ náà, tí Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ rẹ̀ ní oṣù Bẹlu ọdún 2020 ní ìdáhùn sí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19, tí Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Mariana Mazzucato jẹ́ alaga rẹ̀, ti lo ọdún méjì sẹ́yìn láti gbèrò ọrọ̀-ajénáà láti ojú ìwòye ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn, tí ó sì ń fi gbogbo agbára ti ìpìlẹ̀-òye pé ìlera ènìyàn àti àgbáyé gbọ́dọ̀ wà ní àárín gbùngbùn bí a bá ṣe ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ètò àwùjọ, ìlera àti ọrọ̀-ajéwa àti àwọn òfin. Ìgbìmọ̀ náà ti ṣe ìtìsíwájú àlàyé tuntun tó fojú hàn tó fẹsẹ̀múlẹ̀ nínú ọgbọ́n ọrọ̀-ajé tuntun láti ṣe àtúntò àwọn ọrọ̀-ajé láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn kárí àwọn kókó ọ̀rọ̀ mẹ́rin tó jọra:
translations.reference: Landmark report charts route for reorienting economies to deliver health for all In the first-ever report of its kind, the WHO Council on the Economics of Health for All has outlined a bold new path to reorient economies to deliver what matters - health for all. The Council, created by WHO Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus in November 2020 in response to the COVID-19 pandemic, and chaired by Professor Mariana Mazzucato, has spent that last two years rethinking the economy from a health for all perspective, and pushing forcefully the principle that human and planetary health must be at the heart of how we design our social, health and economic systems and policies. The Council has put forward a bold new narrative grounded in new economic wisdom to reorient economies to deliver health for all across four interrelated themes:

Within this report, the WHO Council on Economic-Health Policies for All has developed a new approach to formulate economic policies to promote health for all. The Council has advanced a new framework that emerged within the new economic science to formulate economic policies to promote health for all, focusing on four key areas:

translations.source: Báyìí, dípò kí wọ́n rí ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn gẹ́gẹ́ bíi ẹrú fún ìdàgbàsókè ọrọ̀-ajé, a ní ìlànà-iṣẹ́ fún àtúntò àwọn igbéṣẹ̀ ọrọ̀-ajé ní ọ̀nà tí yóò mú wa débi èròngbà rírí gbogbo ènìyàn pẹ̀lú ànfààní sí ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì tó yára pẹ̀lú àwọn àbájáde tó dára," Dr Tedros ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Láti bí ọdún méjì sẹ́yìn, Ìgbìmọ̀ WHO lórí Àwọn Ọrọ̀-ajé Ìlera fún Gbogbo Ènìyàn ti ṣiṣẹ́ láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àlàyé ọrọ̀-ajé tuntun - èyí tí ó lè mú àyípadà bá ìpèsè owó fún ìlera láti ìnáwó sí ìdókówò," Alága Ìgbìmọ̀ náà, Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Mariana Mazzucato ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "A ti ṣe àyẹ̀wò àwọn àyípadà tó nílò - tó fi mọ́ sí àgbékalẹ̀ ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀, àwọn àjọṣepọ̀ ìjọba-aládàni, àti àwọn ètò ìṣúná - láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ọrọ̀-ajé tí yóò pèsè ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn. Nínú ìjábọ̀ ìgbẹ̀yin wa, a pè fún òfin ọrọ̀-ajé tuntun tí kìí ṣe nípa ìṣàkóso ọjà ṣùgbọ́n nípa ṣíṣe àto àwọn ọjà lọ́nà tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ àti ní àjọṣepọ̀ tí ó mú ìlera àwọn ènìyàn àti àgbáyé ní ọ̀kùkúdùn." Wọ́n ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ rẹ̀ ní oní ní àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé Kẹrìn-dín-lọ́gọ́rin, ìjábọ̀ náà, tí àkòrí rẹ̀ jẹ́ Ìlera fún Gbogbo Ènìyàn: Mímú Àyípadà Bá Àwọn Ọrọ̀-ajé láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn nǹkan tó ṣe pàtàkì (https://www.who.int/groups/who-council-on-the-economics-of-health-for-all), pèsè ìlànà-iṣẹ́ tuntun tí wọ́n gbé kalẹ̀ lórí àwọn òpó mẹ́rin tó wà lókè, pẹ̀lú àwọn àbá kan pàtó lábẹ́ ìkọ̀ọ̀kan - tó mú látara iṣẹ́ àtẹ̀yìnwá Ìgbìmọ̀ náà.
translations.reference: Now, instead of health for all being seen as the servant of economic growth, we have a roadmap for structuring economic activity in a way that will allow us to reach the goal of seeing all people with access to essential health services faster with better results,” said Dr Tedros. “Over the past two years, the WHO Council on the Economics of Health for All has worked to craft a new economic narrative – one that transforms financing for health from an expenditure to an investment,” said the Council’s Chair, Professor Mariana Mazzucato. “We have examined the changes needed – including to the structure of patents, public-private partnerships, and budgets – to design an economy that delivers Health for All. In our final report, we call for new economic policy that is not about market fixing but about proactively and collaboratively shaping markets that prioritize human and planetary health.” Launched today in conjunction with the Seventy-sixth World Health Assembly, the report, entitled Health for All: Transforming economies to deliver what matters (https://www.who.int/groups/who-council-on-the-economics-of-health-for-all), provides a new framework built on the above four pillars, with specific recommendations under each – drawing from the Council’s previous work.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Currently, instead of seeing healthcare as a commodity for profit, we have a plan for transforming health systems in a way that will bring us closer to achieving universal health coverage with access to essential health services quickly and with quality care," said Dr. Tedros. "For the past two years, the WHO Council on the Economics of Health for All has been working to develop new economic models – those that can shift the paradigm of healthcare financing from sickness to wellness," said the Council's Chair, Professor Mariana Mazzucato. "We have examined the transformations needed – focusing on the creation of foundations, public-private partnerships, and investment programs – to build economic models that deliver health for all. In our report, we call for new economic policies that are not about market control but about shaping markets in a way that works and in partnership that promotes the health of people and the planet." Launched today in partnership with the U.S. Center for Global Health Security, the report, titled Health for All: Charting a Path to Transform Economic Models to Prioritize Essential Goods (https://www.who.int/groups/who-council-on-the-economics-of-health-for-all), presents a new approach built on four pillars, with specific actions under each – stemming from the Council's work.

translations.source: Lára àwọn àbá pàtàkì ni: A nílò láti mọ iyì àti òdiwọn àwọn nǹkan tó ṣe pàtàkì tòótọ́ - gbígbèrú àwọn ènìyàn àti àgbáyé - yàtọ̀ sí lílé ìdagbàsókè ọrọ̀-ajé àti jíjẹ èrè GDP lọ́pọ̀ láì ní ṣe pẹ̀lú àwọn àbájáde rẹ̀. Láti ṣe àṣeyọrí ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn, àwọn ìjọba gbọ́dọ̀ gbèrò iye kí wọ́n sì ṣe àtúntò àti ìdarí ọrọ̀-ajé tó dá lórí ìgbáyégbádùn àwùjọ àti àgbáyé, tí mẹ́tíríkì tuntun ń darí. Àtúnṣe ìpìlẹ̀ fún àwọn ètò orílẹ̀-èdè àti ilẹ̀ òkèèrè fún ìpèsè owó fún ìlera ni a nílò, kí a lè ṣe ìnáwó lórí ìlera gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìdókówò ọlọ́jọ́-pípẹ́. Ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ Ìlera fún Gbogbo Ènìyàn máa nílò ọ̀pọ̀ owó, àti ojúlówó ìpèsè owó tó pọ̀. ìmọ̀-ọ̀tunnílò òye àjọni - Kìí ṣe èso ilé-iṣẹ́ kan ṣoṣo tàbí ilé-iṣẹ́ ìjọba. Ṣùgbọ́n àyàfi tí wọ́n bá darí ìmọ̀-ọ̀tunfún dáadáa gbogbo ènìyàn, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn ni ó máa yọ kúrò nínú àwọn ànfààní rẹ̀.
translations.reference: Key recommendations include: We need to value and measure the things that truly matter - human and planetary flourishing - rather than pursuing economic growth and GDP maximization regardless of the consequences. To achieve health for all, governments must rethink value and reshape and redirect the economy based on social and planetary well-being, guided by new metrics. A fundamental overhaul of national and international systems for financing health is needed, so that spending on health is treated as a long-term investment. Delivering Health for All will require both more money, and higher quality financing.Innovation requires collective intelligence—it is never the fruit of just one company or government agency. But unless innovation is governed for the common good, many people remain excluded from its benefits.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Among the key challenges are: We need to understand the importance and magnitude of things that truly matter – the large number of people and the planet – in relation to the current economic growth model and high GDP figures, and how they are not aligned with their outcomes. To achieve health for all, governments must increase the resources they allocate and manage the economy in a way that promotes social and global well-being, driven by new priorities. Adjustments to the funding structure for national and regional health programs are needed to ensure adequate financial resources for health, so that we can build a truly resilient and sustainable health system. Implementing Universal Health Coverage will require significant investment and a substantial increase in health funding. Evidence-based policy making – not just a single business or government entity. However, unless they lead to good, equitable knowledge for all, many people will be excluded from its benefits."

*   **Nuance:** Yoruba can be very nuanced, and some phrases have multiple possible interpretations. I've aimed for the most likely meaning based on the overall theme.
*   **Terminology:** I've used standard English terminology for concepts like "Universal Health Coverage" and "GDP."

translations.source: Àwùjọ ìmọ̀-ọ̀tunìlera ìbẹ̀rẹ̀-dé-òpin tuntun tí ó mú dáadáa gbogbo ènìyàn ní ọ̀kùkúdùn ni a nílò. Gẹ́gẹ́ bí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 ṣe ti fi hàn, ojúlówó àti ìkápá ìjọba ṣe pàtàkì. Àwọn ìjọba tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ kọ́ ni wọ́n kéré jùlọ, ṣùgbọ́n àwọn tí wọ́n ní àgbékalẹ̀ tó dára tí wọ́n sì ṣe ìpèsè fún dáadáa, pẹ̀lú ìpèsè owó àti èyí tó níṣe pẹ̀lú àwọn ènìyàn wọn àti ohun amáyédẹrùn. Ṣíṣe ìdókówò nínú ìkápá ìjọba ṣe pàtàkì láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ Ìlera fún Gbogbo Ènìyàn. Ìjábọ̀ náà tún pèsè àmọ̀ràn lórí ohun tí ó lè jẹ́ ṣíṣe nínú ìfikọ́ra láti ṣàmúlò àwọn àyípadà tí wọ́n nílò láti ṣe àtúntò àwọn ìlànà fún iye ọrọ̀-ajé, ìpèsè owó fún ìlera, ìmọ̀-ọ̀tunàti ṣíṣe ìdagbàsókè ìkápá ẹ̀ka àwùjọ nínú ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn.
translations.reference: A new end-to-end health innovation ecosystem that prioritises the common good is needed. As the COVID-19 pandemic has made clear, the quality and capacity of government matters. Effective governments are not the smallest, but those that are well-designed and properly resourced, both financially and in terms of their people and infrastructure. Re-investing in government capacity is crucial to delivering Health for All. The report also provides suggestions on what can be done in practice to implement the changes needed to reorient measures of economic value, the financing of health, innovation and building public sector capacity in the service of health for all.
translations.hypothesis:
