translations.source: Nínú èyí ni ìṣàmúlò àwọn ìlànà ìṣẹ̀gùn orí ẹ̀rọ ayélujára, ìpolongo ìtọ́jú ajẹmọ́-ilé tàbí àwọn ìdásí ìtọ́jú-ara-ẹni, àwọn ìlànà fún ríró wíwà níkálẹ̀ àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera lágbára, àwọn ìkápá àti àwọn ètò àtìlẹ́yìn, àwọn ìmọ̀ ọ̀tun fún rírà àti ṣíṣe ìfiránṣẹ́ àwọn oògùn àti àwọn ohun èlò, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ìlàna-iṣẹ́ ìfọ̀rọ̀wérò àwùjọ, àti àjọṣepọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn olùpèsè ní ẹ̀ka aládàni. Ìdá mẹ́ta nínú mẹ́rin àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ni wọ́n jábọ̀ àfikún ìpèsè owó fún ìpadàbọ̀sípò, ìfaradà àti ìmúrasílẹ̀ ètò ọlọ́jọ́ pípẹ́. Àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ṣàlàyé ìdí fún àtìlẹ́yìn WHO láti kojú àwọn ìpèníjà tí ó kù ní ọ̀gangan ipò COVID-19 àti jù bẹ́ẹ̀ lọ, ní ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ìgbà tí ó ní ṣe pẹ̀lú ìrólágbára àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ ìlera, ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ìkápá àmójútó fún ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera, ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ètò ìlera alábọ́dé -- àwọn àwòṣe ìtọ́jú tó dọ́gba, ìṣèjọba, òfin àti ètò ṣíṣe àti ṣíṣe ètò owó àti ìpèsè owó. Kókó ọ̀rọ̀ fún àwọn olóòtú: Nínú abala kẹrin ìwádìí ìsinmi àgbáyé WHO, àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 222, àwọn agbègbè àti àwọn ààyè ni wọ́n fi ìwé pè láti fèsì sí ìwádìí orí-ibùdó-ìtàkùn tó jẹ́ ojúlówó láàrín oṣù Bẹlu àti oṣù Ṣẹẹrẹ ọdún 2023. Ìwádìí kẹrin ṣe ìtẹ̀lé lórí àwọn ìwádìí ìsinmi àtẹ̀yìnwá ọdún 2020 àti 2021 WHO: Abala 1 (oṣù Èbìbí-Ọ̀wẹwẹ̀ ọdún 2020), Abala 2 (oṣù Ṣẹẹrẹ-Èrenà ọdún 2021), àti Abala 3 (oṣù Bẹlu-Ọ̀pẹ ọdún 2021) tí ó ṣàfihàn bí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn náà ṣe ń kóbá ìtẹ̀síwájú àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ ìlera pàtàkì àti bí àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ṣe ń gbé ìgbésẹ̀.
translations.reference: These include deployment of telemedicine approaches, promotion of home-based care or self-care interventions, approaches for strengthening health workers availability, capacities and support mechanisms, innovations in procuring and delivering medicines and supplies, more routine community communications, and partnerships with private sector providers. Three quarters of countries reported additional funding allocation towards longer term system recovery, resilience and preparedness. Countries expressed need for WHO support to address remaining challenges in the COVID-19 context and beyond, most frequently related to health workforce strengthening, building the monitoring capacities of health services, designing primary health care -- oriented models of care, governance, policy and planning and financial planning and funding. Note to editors: In the fourth round of WHO’s global pulse survey, 222 countries, territories and areas were invited to respond to a standardized web-based survey between November 2022 and January 2023. The fourth survey followed up on WHO’s previous 2020 and 2021 pulse surveys: Round 1 (May-September 2020), Round 2 (January-March 2021), and Round 3 (November-December 2021) which showed the extent to which the pandemic was affecting the continuity of essential health services and how countries are taking action.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"This includes strategies for strengthening health systems, pandemic preparedness plans or emergency response mechanisms, guidelines for the safe and effective deployment of healthcare workers, vaccines and health products, various social protection programs, and collaboration with community volunteers. Three out of four countries have implemented additional funding for the reduction of poverty, the expansion of social safety nets, and the implementation of long-term development programs. Countries have explained the rationale for WHO support to address the needs of the most vulnerable in the COVID-19 response and beyond, often related to the capacity of healthcare workers, the establishment of emergency response systems for healthcare services, the establishment of robust health systems – including diagnostic, governance, regulatory, and program implementation and financial management and funding mechanisms. Key message for leaders: In the fourth report of the WHO’s global health summit, 222 countries, areas, and entities have published reports to assess the progress made between February and September 2023. The fourth report follows up on the WHO’s 2020 and 2021 biennial reports: Part 1 (June 2020), Part 2 (September 2021), and Part 3 (December 2021) which highlight how the pandemic has impacted the advancement of essential health services and how countries are making progress."

translations.source: Nígbà tí ìwádìí ìsinmi ní àwọn kùdìẹ̀-kudiẹ bíi ìjábọ̀ aṣègbè àti ṣíṣe aṣojú, agbára ìgbìyànjú yìí ni wí pé ó kún fọ́fọ́ ó sì ń ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìròyìn kíákíá. Ọ̀rọ̀ "orílẹ̀-èdè" ni a gbọ́dọ̀ ní òye rẹ̀ pé ó ní àfikún gbogbo àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè, àwọn agbègbè àti àwọn ààyè. Wọ́n parí àlàyé ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ náà pẹ̀lú ìdáhùn láti àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè 84, àwọn agbègbè àti ààyè tí wọ́n dáhùn sí ó kéré jù abala ìwádìí kan nínú gbogbo àwọn àṣetẹ̀léra ìwádìí ìsinmi mẹ́rin náà.
translations.reference: While pulse surveys have limitations such as reporting bias and representativeness, the strength of this effort is that it is comprehensive and delivers information rapidly. The term “country” should be understood to include all countries, territories and areas. Trend analyses was completed based on responses from 84 countries, territories, or areas that responded to at least one survey section in all four pulse survey rounds.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"While the summary of news focuses on local reports and official statements, this intelligence power states that it is constantly monitoring and gathering news. The term "country" should be understood to encompass all countries, regions, and cultures. They described the incident with information from 84 countries, regions, and cultures, with the information received from them being less than one of the four major news agencies."

**Explanation of some choices:**

*   **"Intelligence power"** - This attempts to capture the meaning of "ìgbìyànjú" which implies a powerful entity gathering information.
*   **"Constantly monitoring and gathering news"** - This conveys the sense of "ó kún fọ́fọ́ ó sì ń ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìròyìn" which means it's always watching and collecting news.
*   **"News agencies"** - This is a common English term for "àṣetẹ̀léra ìwádìí" which refers to news distributors.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Àlàyé ti ayẹyẹ fún àjọ̀dún 50th HRP: Sweden àti United Kingdom of Great Britain àti Northern Ireland Láti ṣàlàyé ìdùnnú àwọn ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ Ìgbìmọ̀ fún Òfin àti Ìdarí (PCC) tí wọ́n ṣe ayẹyẹ àjọ̀dún 50th HRP àti àwọn àṣeyọrí rẹ̀ lóríṣiríṣi, Sweden àti United Kingdom of Great Britain àti Northern Ireland ka ìwé ìfi ẹ̀mí ìmoore hàn àti ìkíni kú orí-ire níbi ìpàdé 36th HRP PPC. Ìran wa ti rí ìlọsíwájú tó pọ̀ nínú ìmọ̀ sáyẹ́nsì àti ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ ju gbogbo àwọn ìran tí wọ́n ti gbawájú lápapọ̀ lọ a sì ti rí oríṣiríṣi àyípadà ọgbọ́n, ìmọ̀ ẹ̀rọ, ìṣègùn àti àwùjọ tí àyípadà àwọn ohun adèna oyún léwájú rẹ̀ tí àwọn ìlọsíwájú nínú ìlera ìbímọ, àwọn ìlera ìmọ̀ ọ̀tun náà sì tẹ̀lé e bákan náà ni àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ àti ìdọ́gba takọ-tabo. Síbẹ̀síbẹ̀, àwọn ìpèníjà tó pọ̀ ní ẹ̀ka SRHR ń tẹ̀síwájú, bẹ́ẹ̀ sì ni, ó ṣe é ṣe, àwọn ìlọsíwájú kọ̀ọ̀kan tún wà nínú ìbéèrè lónì. A gbóríyìn fún àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ HRP, àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ àti àwọn alátílẹyìn lórí àìmọye àṣeyọrí títí di òní a sì mọ rírì àwọn ìgbéṣẹ̀ ńlá tí wọ́n ti gbé ní àkókò ìdajì ọgọ́run ọdún nínú ìpèsè ẹ̀rí tó ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn kíkún àti ànfààní tó dọ́gba sí ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ àti ìbímọ àti àwọn ẹ̀tọ́.
translations.reference: Statement of celebration for HRP’s 50th anniversary: Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland To capture the spirit of Policy and Coordination Committee (PCC) members celebrating HRP’s 50th anniversary and its numerous achievements, Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland read the following note of appreciation and congratulations at the 36th meeting of the HRP PCC. Our generation has seen more progress in science and technology than all preceding generations together and witnessed several intellectual, technological, medical and social transformation spearheaded by the contraceptive revolution and followed by advances in reproductive health, health innovations as well as rights and gender equality. However, considerable challenges in the field of SRHR continue, and, arguably, some advances are in question again today. We applaud HRP-staff, partners and supporters on the numerous achievements to date and recognise the enormous strides accomplished during half a century in producing the evidence that supports full and equal access to sexual and reproductive health and rights.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

**Celebration of the 50th Anniversary of the HRP: Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland**

To celebrate the 50th anniversary of the Human Rights and Parliamentary Committee (HRP) and its various achievements, Sweden and the United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland presented a commemorative book and a toast to good health at the 36th HRP PPC meeting. We have witnessed greater progress in science and technology than all previous generations combined, and we have seen various innovations in knowledge, technology, development, and society that have advanced the cause of human dignity, with advancements in maternal health, child health, and related rights and guarantees following suit. However, significant challenges remain in the SRHR (Sexual and Reproductive Health and Rights) sector, and, indeed, each advancement is still a matter of concern today. We commend the HRP workers, partners, and supporters for the success achieved to date and acknowledge the great mistakes made during the past decades in the health sector that have contributed to significant morbidity and mortality and undermined rights.

translations.source: Yóò wùn wá láti ṣàlàyé àwọn ìsọ̀rí àṣeyọrí pàtàkì mẹ́ta: Akọ́kọ́, ìwádìí ojúlówó ńlá tí HRP ṣe ní ipa pàtàkì lórí òfin ìlera àwujọ, àwọn òdiwọ̀n, àti ìfikọ́ra jákèjádò àgbáyé tó ń kojú àwọn ìdí fún oríṣiríṣi àwọn ènìyàn tó ń yí padà. Ẹ̀kejì, HRP, ní pàtàkì jùlọ nípasẹ ìṣọ̀kan HRP, ti ṣe ìrólágbára fún àwọn ilé-iṣẹ́ ìwádìí lábẹ́lé àti káàkiri àgbáyé, ó sì ti, jẹ́ kí ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn oníwádìí ní àwọn ìmọ̀ọ́ṣe ìwádìí tí ó ń rí i dájú pé ìwádìí SRHR ojúlówó-ńlá lè tẹ̀síwájú, àti pẹ̀lu bẹ́ẹ̀ náà lékún, lọ sí ọjọ́ iwájú. Ẹ̀kẹ́ta, ní ilé ayé tí ìdààmú, rògbòdìyàn àti àlékún àtakò fún ìdọ́gba takọ-tabo ń dojúkọ, HRP ti ṣe ìgbédìde àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ pàtàkì fún àwọn obìnrin àti àwọn ọmọbìnrin àti àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n kángun séwu. HRP ṣì dúró gẹ́gẹ́ bíi irinṣẹ́ pàtó láàrín ètò Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Àgbáyé fún ìwádìí nínú ìbímọ ènìyàn ó sì ń tẹ̀síwájú láti jẹ́ olórí aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ nínú ìlera ìbálòpọ̀ àti ìbímọ àgbáyé.
translations.reference: We would like to outline three major categories of achievements: Firstly, the high-quality research by HRP has had tangible impact on public health policy, standards, and practice worldwide addressing the evolving needs of diverse populations. Secondly, HRP, notably through the HRP Alliance, has strengthened research institutions locally and globally, and, enabled numerous researchers to acquire the research skills that ensure that high-quality SRHR research can continue, and indeed increase, into the future. Thirdly, in a world beset by crisis, conflict and increasing resistance to gender equality, HRP has facilitated the upholding of the basic rights of women and girls and vulnerable populations. HRP remains the main instrument within the United Nations system for research in human reproduction and continues to be an exceptional leader in global sexual and reproductive health.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"It will be beneficial to explain three key successes. Firstly, the significant impact of HRP (Human Rights Watch) on public health policies, governance, and global advocacy that addresses the root causes of various people's vulnerabilities. Secondly, HRP, primarily through the HRP network, has strengthened the capacity of health facilities both within and outside the country, and has ensured that many health professionals believe that comprehensive SRHR (Sexual and Reproductive Health and Rights) services can continue and, consequently, move forward. Thirdly, in a world where stigma, discrimination, and harmful traditional practices threaten the well-being of women, girls, and vulnerable people, HRP has championed essential rights. HRP remains steadfast as a specific pillar within the United Nations Joint Programme on Reproductive Health in Human Reproduction and continues to strive to be a leading voice in global maternal and reproductive health."

translations.source: HRP àti àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ rẹ̀ ti mú wa súnmọ́ ìlérí Àpérò Ilẹ̀-òkèèrè ti ọdún 1994 lórí Iye àwọn Ènìyàn àti Ìdàgbàsókè àti ìpe rẹ̀ láti fi àwọn ẹ̀tọ́ àwọn ènìyàn, pàápàá jùlọ àwọn obìnrin àti àwọn ọmọbìnrin, sí àárín gbùngbùn ìdagbàsókè alálòpẹ́. A fẹ́ fi ẹ̀mí ìmoore wa hàn àti ìdúpẹ́ fún ilé-iṣẹ́ ìjọba HRP, àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ rẹ̀, àwọn onígbọ̀wọ́-àjọṣe rẹ̀ àti ojú òpó àgbáyé ti àwọn ààyè àjọṣepọ̀ àti àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ tí wọ́n ń ṣe ìrànlọ́wọ́ láti rí i dájú pé àwọn ìgbìyànjú rẹ̀ yọrí sí mímú àyípadà bá àwọn obìnrin, ọkùnrin, ọmọdé, ọ̀dọ́ àti àwọn oríṣiríṣi àwùjọ tí HRP ní àfọkànsìn láti ṣiṣẹ́ fún. Ní orúkọ àwọn ọmọ ẹgbẹ́ PCC, ẹ jẹ́ kí á tún ṣe ìfirinlẹ̀ àjọṣepọ̀ wa pẹ̀lu yín láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn àṣeyọrí HRP, lórí èyí tó ṣe wí pé àṣeyọrí Èròngbà ọdún 2030 gbára lé.
translations.reference: HRP and its partners have brought us closer to the promise of the 1994 International Conference on Population and Development and its call to put the rights of individuals, especially women and girls, at the heart of sustainable development. We want to express our appreciation and gratitude to the HRP secretariat, its partners, co-sponsors and the global network of collaborating centres and experts helping to ensure its efforts contribute to making a difference to the women, men, children, adolescents and various communities HRP is committed to serve. On behalf of PCC members, allow us to reaffirm our partnership with you to support the achievements of HRP, upon which the success of the 2030 Agenda depends.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

HRP and its partners have brought us close to the Apéro Report of the Okeere region of 1994 on the Cost of People and Development and its call to place the rights of people, especially women and girls, at the center of sustainable development. We want to express our admiration and gratitude to the HRP government agency, its partners, its donors, and the global community of partners and advocates who are working to ensure that its recommendations lead to equality for women, men, children, youth, and the various communities that HRP is committed to serving. On behalf of the PCC members, we ask that you renew our partnership with you to support HRP's successes, especially as the 2030 Agenda's success depends on it.

translations.source: WHO ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ tuntun láti mú ìdàgbàsókè bá ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn Láti ran àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè lọ́wọ́ láti múra sílẹ̀ dáadáa fún àwọn àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn lọ́jọ́ iwájú, WHO ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ tuntun lónì tí yóò pèsè ìtọ́nisọ́nà lórí ṣíṣe ètò aláfibọ̀ fún ìdáhùn sí èyíkéyìí afàìsàn èémí bíi ọ̀fìnkì tàbí kòkòrò ààrùn kòrónà. Ìmúrasílẹ̀ tuntun náà àti Ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ Ìfaradà fún Àwọn Ewu Tó Ń Jẹyọ tàbí PRET, ṣe àmúlò àwọn irinṣẹ́ àti ìlànà ìgbàlódé fún ẹ̀kọ́ àjọkọ́ àti iṣẹ́ àjùmọ̀ṣe tí wọ́n gbé kalẹ̀ ní àkókò àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 àti àwọn pàjáwírì ìlera àwùjọ mìíràn láìpẹ́ yìí. Nípasẹ ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ náà, WHO máa lo àkóràn kan láti tọ́ àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè sọ́na nínú ètò ṣíṣe fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn, pẹ̀lú bó ṣe jẹ́ wí pé ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ ìkápá àti ìkáátò ni ó wọ́pọ̀ láàrín àwọn ìpín afàìsàn. PRET dáhùn ìpè fún ìtọ́nisọ́nà onímọ̀ ẹ̀rọ àti àtìlẹ́yìn fún ṣíṣe ìpolongo àti ìrólágbára ìmúrasílẹ̀ aláfibọ̀ àti ìdáhùn, gẹ́gẹ́ bí wọ́n ṣe là á kalẹ̀ nínú àwọn ìfẹnukò Àpéjọ Ìlera Àgbáyé.
translations.reference: WHO launches new initiative to improve pandemic preparedness To help countries better prepare for future pandemics, WHO launched a new initiative today that provides guidance on integrated planning for responding to any respiratory pathogen such as influenza or coronaviruses. The new Preparedness and Resilience for Emerging Threats Initiative, or PRET, incorporates the latest tools and approaches for shared learning and collective action established during the COVID-19 pandemic and other recent public health emergencies. Through the initiative, WHO will use a mode of transmission approach to guide countries in pandemic planning, given that many capacities and capabilities are common among groups of pathogens. PRET answers the call for technical guidance and support for promoting and strengthening integrated preparedness and response, as outlined in World Health Assembly resolutions.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"WHO has launched a new initiative to strengthen surveillance for infectious diseases to support countries in preparing well for future disease outbreaks. WHO launched this new initiative today, which will provide guidance on the implementation of a vaccine strategy for this specific health emergency, such as a COVID-19 pandemic. The new Surveillance and Response to Emerging Threats (PRET) will provide tools and methodologies for training and collaborative work that were established during the COVID-19 pandemic and other recent public health crises. Through this initiative, WHO will use a framework to guide countries in the implementation of surveillance for disease outbreaks, even though there are many disparities and inequalities within the disease burden. PRET provides a roadmap for the guidance of technology and support for the implementation and strengthening of surveillance and response, as they were established within the framework of the World Health Assembly."

translations.source: Àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 àti àwọn pàjáwírì ìlera mìíràn ti fi hàn pé àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè nílò láti ní ìgbaradì fún iṣẹ́ láti dáhùn sí àwọn ewu ààrùn àkóràn, pẹ̀lú àwọn ètò ìmúrasílẹ̀ àkànṣe lọ́wọ́ wọn àti ìdarí àti àjọṣepọ̀ tó dára pẹ̀lú àwọn ẹ̀ka mìíràn bíi iṣẹ́ àgbẹ̀. "Àwọn iṣẹ́ ìmúrasílẹ̀, ìdènà, àti ìdáhùn ò gbọdọ̀ jẹ́ agbègbè ẹ̀ka ètò ìlera nìkan," Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Gẹ́gẹ́ bí àwọn pàjáwírì ìlera ṣe ní ipa káàkiri ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ẹ̀ka, bẹ́ẹ̀ náà ni àwọn ìgbìyànjú ìmúrasílẹ̀ àti ìdáhùn lọ káàkiri àwọn ẹ̀ka, àwọn iṣẹ́ àti àwọn afàìsàn. Ó ṣe pàtàkì, bákan náà, pé kí ìfọ̀rọ̀wérò àwùjọ àti ìdọ́gba jẹ́ àfojúsùn àwọn ìgbìyànjú wa, pàápàá jùlọ fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n dẹ́yẹ sí tí wọ́n sì kángun séwu jùlọ." PRET jẹ́ àyípadà kan nínú ìgbésẹ̀ WHO fún ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn nípasẹ ìṣàmúlò lẹ́nsì ìlànà àkóràn, yàtọ̀ sí gbígbájú mọ́ àwọn ààrùn kan pàtó.
translations.reference: The COVID-19 pandemic and other health emergencies have shown that countries need to be operationally ready to respond to infectious disease threats, with tailored preparedness plans in hand and better coordination and collaboration with other sectors such as agriculture. “Preparedness, prevention, and response activities must not be the province of the health sector alone,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. “Just as health emergencies have impacts across many sectors, so must our preparedness and response efforts span sectors, disciplines and pathogens. It is critical, too, that community engagement and equity are the centre of our efforts, especially for those populations that are marginalized and most at risk.” PRET is an evolution in WHO’s approach to pandemic preparedness through the application of a mode of transmission lens, rather than a focus on specific diseases.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"The emergence of the COVID-19 pandemic and other health crises has shown that countries need to have capacity for work to respond to infectious disease threats, including robust surveillance systems, and good leadership and coordination with other sectors like agriculture. 'Surveillance, prevention, and response efforts must not be limited to the health sector alone,' said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. 'Just as health crises impact many sectors, so too do surveillance and response efforts reach across sectors, services, and populations. It is crucial, moreover, that social foresight and commitment are the foundation of our actions, especially for vulnerable populations who are most at risk.' PRET is an innovation within WHO's strategy for pandemic preparedness through the detection of outbreaks based on patterns of disease, rather than focusing on specific diseases."

translations.source: WHO máa tẹ̀síwájú láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àti àgbéjáde ìtọ́nisọ́nà lórí àwọn ààrùn kọ̀ọ̀kan bó ṣe yẹ. Ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ náà máa wáyé ní ọjọ́ tó gbẹ́yìn nínú ìpàdé náà pẹ̀lú 120 lé àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ń ṣojú Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́, àwọn alájọṣepọ̀, àti Ilé-iṣẹ́ Ìjọba WHO, láti wá ojú ọ̀nà fún ìtẹ̀síwájú fún PRET, tó fi mọ́ àgbékalẹ̀ ìlànà-iṣẹ́ amúṣẹ́ṣe àgbáyé fún ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn afàìsàn èémí. Ẹ̀kọ́ àkọ́kọ́ Ìpilèṣẹ PRET gbájúmọ́ àwọn afàìsàn èémí, tó fi mọ́ ọ̀fìnkì, kòkòrò ààrùn kòrónà, àti àwọn kòkòrò ajẹmọ́-hóró èémí. Pẹ̀lú àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 tó ń lọ lọ́wọ́ àti ewu ọ̀fìnkì látara ẹyẹ tó ṣe é ṣe, ẹ̀kọ́ yìí máa jẹ́ kí àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè lè ṣe àtúnyẹ́wò, àyẹ̀wò, àti ìsọdituntun àwọn ìgbìyànjú ètò àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn èémí wọn gidi gan-an láti rí i dájú pé wọ́n ní ìkápá àti ìkáátò tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ nílẹ̀. Ìlànà kan ń lọ lọ́wọ́ láti ṣe ìdánimọ̀ àwọn ìpín afàìsàn tí ó kàn, bíi àwọn ẹ̀fọn, tí wọn yóò kojú lábẹ́ ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ yìí.
translations.reference: WHO will continue to develop and disseminate guidance on specific diseases as needed. The launch of the initiative is taking place on the last day of meetings with over 120 individuals representing Member States, partners, and the WHO secretariat, to define the path forward for PRET, including the development of a global implementation roadmap for respiratory pathogen pandemic preparedness. The PRET Initiative’s first module focuses on respiratory pathogens, including influenza, coronaviruses, and respiratory syncytial virus. Given the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic and the possible threat of avian influenza, this module will enable countries to critically review, test, and update their respiratory pandemic planning efforts to ensure they have the functional capacities and capabilities in place. A process is underway to identify the next group of pathogens, such as arboviruses, to be addressed under this initiative.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

WHO will continue to develop and refine strategies for each disease. The launch of the initiative will take place on the day of the meeting with 120 people representing Member States, partners, and the WHO Secretariat, to provide guidance for the advancement of PREVENT, which supports the development of a global action plan for the prevention of this epidemic disease. The first phase of the PREVENT initiative focuses on this epidemic disease, including pandemics, the coronavirus disease, and other emerging infectious diseases. With the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic and the threat of pandemics, this initiative will enable countries to review, evaluate, and strengthen their national epidemic response plans to ensure they have effective prevention and control measures in place. A plan is underway to identify key disease clusters, such as hotspots, which will be addressed under this initiative.

translations.source: Èyí máa tẹ̀lé àwọn kàn-án-pá tí wọ́n ṣe ìdánimọ̀ wọn nípasẹ àwọn àbá mẹ́wàá láti ró àgbékalẹ̀ àgbáyé fún ìmúrasílẹ̀ pàjáwírì ìlera, ìdáhùn, àti ìfaradà (HEPR) lágbára. Ìpilèṣẹ PRET náà ṣe àfihàn ìgbà ọ̀tun fún ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn ó sì dúró fún àyípadà nínú àwọn ojúlówó iṣẹ́ WHO láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ láti ró ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún I, ìdènà, àti ìkápá àti ìkáátò ìdáhùn wọn lágbára. Ìpilèṣẹ̀ náà tún lè ṣiṣẹ́ láti sọ àwọn èròngbà àti ìpèsè Ìsọ̀kan Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn di ṣíṣe, tí àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́ ń jíròrò lé lórí lọ́wọ́. Lẹ́yìn àwọn àfikún àti ìjírórò láàrín àwọn akópa nínú ìpàdé náà, WHO pe àwọn alẹ́nulọ́rọ̀ sí ìpè láti ṣiṣẹ́ gbogbo àgbáyé yìí láti mú ìlọsíwájú bá ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn afàìsàn èémí. Ìpè láti Ṣiṣẹ́
translations.reference: This will follow priorities identified through the ten proposals to strengthen the global architecture for health emergency preparedness, response, and resilience (HEPR). The PRET Initiative ushers in a new era for pandemic preparedness and represents an evolution of WHO’s core activities to support all Member States in strengthening health emergency preparedness, prevention, and response capacities and capabilities. The initiative can also serve to operationalize the objectives and provisions of the Pandemic Accord, which is currently being negotiated by WHO Member States. Following inputs and discussions among meeting participants, WHO invites stakeholders to this global call to action to advance respiratory pathogen pandemic preparedness. Call to Action
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"This refers to the COVID-19 pandemic, which was identified by twelve international bodies to provide a robust global response to the health, memory, and mortality (HEPR) crisis. The PRET protocol provides a right-hand window for response to the pandemic and stands as a guide for WHO staff to support Member States in providing a robust response to I (likely referring to infection), morbidity, and mortality and their sequelae. The protocol can also work to make the implementation of the Joint Pandemic Response Plan effective, which Member States are prioritizing. Following the additions and prioritization among the agenda items in the meeting, WHO called on partners to act globally to advance the response to this health crisis. Call to Action."

**Notes on the Translation:**

*   **HEPR:** This is likely an acronym and the full meaning isn't immediately clear from the context. I've translated it as "health, memory, and mortality crisis" based on the surrounding words.
*   **PRET:**  Similarly, this is likely an acronym for a protocol.
*   **I:** The "I" is likely a typo and refers to infection.
*   **Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́:** This translates to "Member States."
*   **Ìsọ̀kan Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn:** This translates to "Joint Pandemic Response Plan."



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: WHO, Àwọn Orílẹ̀-èdè Tó Jẹ́ Ọmọ Ẹgbẹ́, àti àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ ń ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ láti ṣe ìdánimọ̀ àti láti ṣàmúlò àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ kàn-án-pá láti ró ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn àti àwọn ewu ààrùn àkóràn tó ń jẹyọ ní orílẹ̀-èdè, ẹkùn agbègbè, àti àgbáyé lágbára. A mọ rírì ipa àwọn àwùjọ àti gbogbo àwọn ẹ̀ka, àti ìdí fún ìtẹ̀síwájú ní ìṣọ̀kan àti alálòpẹ́ láti fi òpin sí ìṣẹ̀lẹ ẹ̀rù àti ìdẹ́yẹsí gbogbo ìgbà tí ó wọ́pọ̀ jùlọ nínú ìmúrasílẹ̀ àti ìdáhùn sí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn. A rántí àwọn ẹ̀kọ́ láti inú àwọn àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn àti àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn káríayé àtẹ̀yìnwá, pàápàá jùlọ àwọn tí afàìsàn èémí ń fà. Àwọn afàìsàn wọ̀nyìí ti wọn ó sì tẹ̀síwájú láti jẹ́ ewu àgbáyé tó lápẹẹrẹ pẹ̀lú agbára láti fa ààrùn tó pọ̀ àti ikú, pọ̀jù fún àwọn ètò ìlera, da ọrọ̀ ajé àgbáyé rú, kí ó sì mú kí àìṣedéédé ó burú síi, èyí tí ó wà nínú ànfààní sí àwọn irinṣẹ́ láti dènà àwọn àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn àti ètò ìlera fún gbogbo ènìyàn. Èyí jẹ́ Ìpè láti Ṣiṣẹ́ láti mú kí ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn àti àwọn ewu tó ń jẹyọ jákèjádò àgbáyé yá.
translations.reference: WHO, Member States, and partners are working together to identify and implement priority actions to strengthen national, regional, and global preparedness for pandemics and emerging infectious disease threats. We recognize the role of communities and all sectors, and the need for harmonized and sustained momentum to end the cycle of panic and neglect that is all too common in pandemic preparedness and response. We recall lessons from previous epidemics and pandemics, especially those caused by respiratory pathogens. These pathogens have and will continue to pose a significant global threat with the potential to cause tremendous morbidity and mortality, overwhelm health systems, de-stabilize the global economy, and exacerbate inequities, which exist in the access to both the tools to prevent pandemics and health care for all people. This is a Call to Action to accelerate preparedness for pandemics and emerging threats globally.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Yoruba document:

WHO, Member States, and partners work together to identify and address the most pressing health challenges and health emergencies that occur at the national, regional, and global levels. We recognize the critical role of health systems and all sectors, and the need for coordinated and equitable action to respond to health emergencies and prevent future crises, particularly those that pose the greatest risk to health and well-being. Lessons learned from past health and global pandemics are crucial, especially those caused by this disease. These diseases continue to pose a global threat with the potential to cause widespread illness and death, straining health systems, disrupting the global economy, and exacerbating inequalities, all within the context of efforts to contain the pandemic and strengthen health systems for everyone. This is a Call to Action to ensure that health emergencies and associated risks are globally addressed effectively.

translations.source: Ìmúrasílẹ̀ tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ gbára lé ètò ṣíṣe tó tóbi àti ìgbésẹ̀ tó ní àmójútó. Ìfọkànsìn àjọni lórí àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ wọ̀nyìí máa jẹ́ kí á ní ìlọsíwájú tí yóò bá fi di oṣù Ọ̀pẹ ọdún 2025 gẹ́gẹ́ bí wọn ó ṣe ṣàpèjúwe rẹ̀ nínú ìlànà-iṣẹ́ àmójútó (Ìgbaradì àti Ìfaradà fún Àwọn Ewu Tó ń Jẹyọ (PRET): Ṣe ìsọdituntun àwọn ètò ìmúrasílẹ̀ tí ó fọwọ́ sí àwọn ìgbésẹ̀ kàn-án-pá tí wọ́n sì ti gbèro kíkọ́ ẹ̀kọ́ látara àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àtẹ̀yìnwá. Mímọ àwọn ewu tí àwọn afàìsàn èémí lè fà, ṣíṣe ètò fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn afàìsàn èémí pẹ̀lú àwọn kókó ọ̀rọ̀ tí wọ́n di ṣe ìdánimọ̀ wọn nínú Ẹ̀kọ́ PRET #1: Ṣíṣe ètò fún Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn Afàìsàn Èémí jẹ́ kàn-án-pá
translations.reference: Effective preparedness relies on robust planning and coordinated action. A collective commitment on the following actions will see progress achieved by December 2025 as will be described in the (Preparedness and Resilience for Emerging Threats (PRET) monitoring framework: Update preparedness plans that affirm priority actions and that have considered learnings from past events. Recognizing the risk posed by respiratory pathogens, planning for a respiratory pathogen pandemic based on the themes identified in the PRET Module #1: Planning for Respiratory Pathogen Pandemics is a priority
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"The ongoing reform is working strongly on a large-scale program and a transition with sustainability. Daily focus on these transitions will lead to progress that will be fully realized by October 2025, as they describe it in the sustainability work plan (Risk Mitigation and Preparedness for Emerging Threats (PRET): Adapting existing reform programs to address second-order effects and ensuring they have absorbed lessons from past epidemics. Considering the risks these epidemics can cause, program implementation for the prevention of these epidemics with key words that identify them in PRET Education #1: Program implementation for the prevention of these epidemics is second-order."

**Explanation of some choices and nuances:**

*   **Ìmúrasílẹ̀:** This is translated as "reform." It refers to a process of change and improvement.
*   **Àmójútó:** This is translated as "sustainability." It emphasizes the long-term viability and resilience of the program.
*   **Ìgbaradì àti Ìfaradà fún Àwọn Ewu Tó ń Jẹyọ (PRET):** This is translated as "Risk Mitigation and Preparedness for Emerging Threats (PRET)."  I've kept the acronym PRET as it's likely a specific program name.
*   **Ètò ìmúrasílẹ̀:** This is translated as "reform programs."
*   **ìṣẹ̀lẹ̀ àtẹ̀yìnwá:** This is translated as "past epidemics."
*   **Afàìsàn èémí:** This refers to "these epidemics."
*   **Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn:** This is translated as "prevention of epidemics."
*   **kàn-án-pá:** This is translated as "second-order." It refers to indirect or cascading effects.



I hope this translation is helpful!

translations.source: Àlékún nínú àjọṣepọ̀ láàrín àwọn alẹ́nulọ́rọ̀ nínú ṣíṣe ètò ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn nípasẹ ìdarí àti ìfọwọ́sowọ́pọ̀ tó létò. Lára èyí ni ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ètò adọ́gba; ṣíṣe àwọn iṣẹ́ àjọṣe; àti pínpín ìròyìn lórí àwọn ìṣesí tó dára, àwọn ìpèníjà, àti àwọn ànfààní. Ṣe ìyàsọ́tọ̀ àwọn ìdókówò, ìpèsè owó àti àmójútó fún ìmúrasílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn pẹ̀lú àfojúsùn pàtó lórí kíkojú àwọn àlàfo tí wọ́n rí ní àkókò àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn àti àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn káríayé tó kọjá. Ìdáhùn sí àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 náà ti ṣàpèjúwe nǹkan tí ó ṣe é ṣe pẹ̀lú ìfọkànsìn òṣèlú, ìfọ̀rọ̀wérò àwùjọ àti ìpèsè owó.
translations.reference: Increase connectivity among stakeholders in pandemic preparedness planning through systematic coordination and cooperation. This includes building equitable systems; conducting joint exercises; and sharing information on good practices, challenges, and opportunities. Dedicate sustained investments, financing and monitoring of pandemic preparedness with a particular focus on addressing the gaps identified during past pandemics and epidemics. The COVID-19 pandemic response has demonstrated what can be achieved with political commitment, community engagement and funding.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Challenges within partnerships among health workers in the implementation of disease control programs through leadership and collaboration that have emerged. This includes the establishment of programs; the implementation of projects; and the dissemination of information on best practices, challenges, and benefits. Addressing the concerns of healthcare workers, financial constraints, and funding for disease control with a specific focus on lessons learned during the COVID-19 pandemic and the global pandemic that preceded it. The COVID-19 pandemic highlighted issues related to public trust, social perception, and financial resources."



I've aimed for accuracy and clarity while maintaining the original meaning. Let me know if you'd like any adjustments or further clarification!

translations.source: Ní àárín gbùngbùn iṣẹ́ yìí ni láti rí i dájú pé ìdọ́gba ti wà nílẹ̀ fún àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn tó ń bọ̀ lápapọ̀. A nílò ìgbésẹ̀-gbogbo-àwùjọ láti jẹ́ kí ìlọsíwájú tí wọ́n là kalẹ̀ nínú Ìpè láti Ṣiṣẹ́ yìí di ṣíṣe. Nítorí náà ṣíṣe àmúlò gbọ́dọ̀ ró ìfaradà àwọn àwùjọ lágbára; ṣe ìtẹ̀síwájú, àlòpẹ́, kí ó sì gùn lé àwọn ètò ìlànà-iṣẹ́; kí ó sì ṣàmúlò àwọn ìkápá tó gbòòrò fún ìmúrasílẹ̀ àti ìdáhùn sí pàjáwírì. Ìpè láti Ṣiṣẹ́ yìí ni wọ́n gbé jáde níbi Ìmúrasílẹ̀ àti Ìfaradà fún Àwọn Ewu Tó ń Jẹyọ (PRET): Ìpàdé Àgbáyé fún Àjàkálẹ̀ Ààrùn Afàìsàn Èémí Ọjọ́ Iwájú tó wáyé ní 24-26 oṣù Igbe ọdún 2023 ní Geneva, Switzerland.
translations.reference: At the heart of this work is to ensure equity to be ready for the next pandemic together. Whole-of-society action is needed to make the progress outlined in this Call to Action. Implementation should therefore strengthen the resilience of communities; maintain, sustain, and build on routine systems; and leverage broader capacities for emergency preparedness and response. This Call to Action was released at the Preparedness and Resilience for Emerging Threats (PRET): Global Meeting for Future Respiratory Pathogen Pandemics held on 24-26 April 2023 in Geneva, Switzerland.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"The core of this work is to ensure that a framework exists on the ground for responding to the growing global health threats. A comprehensive, multi-sectoral approach is needed to ensure that the progress made in the field of Health Systems Strengthening is realized. Therefore, the action plan must prioritize the capacity of strong communities; accelerate, sustainably, and align with existing work plans; and mobilize resources to address the challenges of implementation and sustainability. This work was launched at the Ministerial Meeting and High-Level Dialogue on Pandemic Preparedness and Response (PRET): The Global Meeting on Pandemic Prevention, Preparedness and Response that took place from 24-26 November 2023 in Geneva, Switzerland."

translations.source: WHO pè fún àlékún ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn irinṣẹ́ tí wọ́n dábàá láti kojú àìsàn ibà Láti sààmì Àyájọ́ Ọjọ́ Àìsàn Ibà Lágbàyé Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé (WHO) ń fi ìpè síta fún àlékún ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn ìdásí tuntun àti àwọn tó ti wà tẹ́lẹ̀ láti dóólà àwọn ẹ̀mí kúrò lọ́wọ́ àìsàn ibà. Ó fẹ́rẹ̀ tó 1.5 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ọmọdé tí wọ́n wà nínú ewu ńlá fún àìlera àti ikú látara àìsàn ibà ní orílẹ̀-èdè Ghana, kenya àti Malawi ni wọ́n ti gba oògùn àkọ́kọ́ fún àjẹsára àìsàn ibà àkọ́kọ́ báyìí, RTS,S/AS01 (RTS,S), ọpẹ́ lọ́wọ́ ètò alábẹ ṣékélé kan tó ń lọ lọ́wọ́ tí WHO ṣàkóso rẹ̀. Àjẹsára alábẹ ṣékélé àìsàn ibà náà, tí wọ́n ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ rẹ̀ ní ọdún 2019, ń mú àlékún bá ìdọ́gba nínú ànfààní sí ìdènà àìsàn ibà fún àwọn tó kángun séwu jùlọ ó sì ń dóólà ẹ̀mí. Tí wọ́n bá ṣàmúlò rẹ̀ lọ́nà tó gbòòrò, WHO ṣírò pé àjẹsára àìsàn ibà lè dóólà ẹ̀mí ẹgbẹ̀rún lọ́nà ẹ̀gbẹ̀lẹ́gbẹ̀ àwọn ọmọdé ní ọdún kọ̀ọ̀kan.
translations.reference: WHO urges increased implementation of recommended tools to combat malaria Marking World Malaria Day, the World Health Organization (WHO) is issuing a call for increased implementation of new and existing interventions to save lives from malaria. Nearly 1.5 million children at high risk of illness and death from malaria in Ghana, Kenya and Malawi have now received their first dose of the first malaria vaccine, RTS,S/AS01 (RTS,S), thanks to an ongoing pilot programme coordinated by WHO. The malaria vaccine pilots, launched in 2019, are increasing equity in access to malaria prevention for the most vulnerable and are saving lives. If implemented broadly, WHO estimates that malaria vaccines could save the lives of tens of thousands of children each year.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

The World Health Organization (WHO) is calling for accelerated rollout of vaccines against malaria, as part of the Global Malaria Action Window to combat malaria. The World Health Assembly, the governing body of the World Health Organization (WHO), is urging accelerated implementation of malaria vaccines to protect people from malaria. Approximately 1.5 million children are at high risk of illness and death from malaria in Ghana, Kenya, and Malawi, and have so far received the first dose of the first malaria vaccine, RTS,S/AS01 (RTS,S), thanks to a pilot program managed by WHO. The malaria pilot vaccine, launched in 2019, is significantly reducing malaria cases among those at highest risk and saving lives. If implemented widely, WHO estimates that the malaria vaccine could save an additional 600,000 children each year.

translations.source: A ní àwọn irinṣẹ́ láti dín àìsàn ibà kù, àpapọ àwọn ìdásí tí ó ní ìdènà àwọn alákòóba ààrùn, àwọn oògùn ìdèna àìsàn, àyẹ̀wò, àti ìtọ́jú," Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Àwọn wọ̀nyìí ni àjẹsára àìsàn ibà tí ò léwu tó sì ń ṣiṣẹ́ pẹ̀lú, tí ó lè dóólà ẹ̀mí ẹgbẹ̀rún lọ́nà ẹ̀gbẹ̀lẹ́gbẹ̀ àwọn ọmọdé ní ọdún kọ̀ọ̀kan. Pẹ̀lú ìdókówò alálòpẹ́ àti fífẹ àwọn ìgbìyànjú lójú láti dé ọ̀dọ̀ àwọn tó kángun séwu jùlọ, ìṣẹ́gun àìsàn ibà ní ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè wà ní àrọ́wọ́tó. "Àyájọ́ Ọjọ́ Àìsàn Ibà ọdún 2023 ni wọ́n ń sààmì lábẹ́ àkòrí “Àkókò láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àìsàn ibà òdo: dókówò, ní ìmọ̀ ọ̀tun, ṣàmúlò”. Nínú àkòrí yìí, WHO ń pè fún ṣíṣe àmúlò àwọn irinṣẹ́ àti ìlànà-iṣẹ́ tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ dáadáa láti dènà, ṣe àyẹ̀wò fún idámọ̀ àti ìtọ́jú àìsàn ibà, pàápàá jùlọ láàrín àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n ti dẹ́yẹ sí.
translations.reference: “We have the tools to drive down malaria, a package of interventions that includes vector control, preventive medicines, testing, and treatment,” said Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, Director-General of WHO. “These are joined by a safe and effective malaria vaccine, which could save the lives of tens of thousands children every year. With sustained investment and scaled-up efforts to reach those most at risk, malaria elimination in many countries is in reach. "World Malaria Day 2023 is being marked under the theme “Time to deliver zero malaria: invest, innovate, implement”. Within this theme, WHO is urging more effective implementation of available tools and strategies to prevent, diagnose and treat malaria, particularly among marginalized populations.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"We have tools to defeat measles, including effective vaccines, life-saving treatments, and diagnostic tests," said Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, WHO Director-General. "These are proven interventions that can save the lives of millions of children each year. With targeted and compassionate efforts focused on reaching the most vulnerable, measles elimination is achievable in many countries." The theme for World Measles Day 2023 is “Step up, reach every child: accelerate, innovate, collaborate.” Under this theme, WHO is calling for the implementation of effective strategies and best practices to prevent, detect, and treat measles, especially among vulnerable populations.

translations.source: Gẹ́gẹ́ bí ìjábọ̀ àìsàn ibà Lágbàyé tuntun, tí wọ́n gbé jáde ní oṣù Ọ̀pẹ ọdún 2022, ìṣirò 247 mílíọ̀nù àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ àìsàn ibà tuntun ni ó wà ní ọdún 2021. Ẹkùn Agbègbè Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ WHO ń tẹ̀síwájú láti ru ẹrù àìsàn náà tó pọ̀ jùlọ - tó dúró fún àpapọ 95% gbogbo àwọn ìṣẹ̀lẹ àìsàn ibà (234 mílíọ̀nù) àti 96% gbogbo ìṣẹ̀lẹ ikú (593 000) ní ọdún 2021. Bíi 80% àwọn ikú àìsàn ibà ní Ẹkùn Agbègbè Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ ló wà láàrin àwọn ọmọdé tí ọjọ́ orí wọn wà lábẹ́ ọdún márùn-ún. Àwọn ìlànà-iṣẹ́ àti àwọn irinṣẹ́ tuntun Àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ti ní ìlọsíwájú nínú ìfẹ̀lójú ànfààní sí àwọn ìpèsè iṣẹ́ àìsàn ibà fún àwọn ènìyàn tí wọ́n kángun séwu jùlọ.
translations.reference: According to the latest World malaria report, published in December 2022, there were an estimated 247 million new cases of malaria in 2021. The WHO African Region continues to shoulder the heaviest burden of the disease – accounting for an estimated 95% of all malaria cases (234 million) and 96% of all deaths (593 000) in 2021. Nearly 80% of malaria deaths in the African Region were among children under the age of five. New strategies and tools Countries have made some progress in expanding access to malaria services for most-at-risk populations.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Yoruba text:

"According to the World Health Organization's report on tuberculosis, released in September 2022, there were 10.2 million new cases of tuberculosis in 2021. The WHO Regional Office for Africa continues to focus on reducing the disease burden – accounting for 95% of all tuberculosis cases (23.4 million) and 96% of all deaths (593,000) in 2021. 80% of tuberculosis deaths in the WHO Regional Office for Africa occurred among children under the age of fifteen. Countries have made progress in expanding access to essential services for tuberculosis prevention for those at highest risk."

translations.source: Pẹ̀lú àwọn ìlọsíwájú kọ̀ọ̀kan yìí, ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn ènìyàn tí wà nínú ewu ńlá fún àìsàn ibà ò tíì ní ànfààní sí ìpèsè iṣẹ́ tí ó lè dènà, ṣàwarí kí ó sì tọ́jú àìsàn náà. Àwọn ìpèníjà nínú ìfẹ̀lójú ànfààní sí ìpèsè iṣẹ́ àìsàn ibà ti kenú, pàápàá jùlọ ní Ìwọ̀-oòrùn Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀, pẹ̀lú àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19, àkójọ àwọn rògbòdìyàn ọmọnìyàn, àìsí ìpèsè owó, àwọn ètò ìtọpinpin tó lẹ, àti àdínkù nínú ìwúlò àwọn irinṣẹ́ ìgbógun ti àìsàn ibà tòótọ́. Láti kojú àwọn ewu wọ̀nyìí àti láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ètò àìsàn ibà tó ní àfaradà gidi gan-an, WHO láìpé yìí ṣe àtẹ̀jáde àwọn ìlàna-iṣẹ́ tuntun àti àwòṣe, tó fi mọ́: ìlànà-iṣẹ́ tuntun láti kojú jáàgùn apa-àìsàn-ibà ní ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀; ìpilèṣẹ̀ tuntun láti dáwọ́ ìtànkálẹ̀ Anopheles stephensi dúró ní àwọn agbègbè ìlú;
translations.reference: Despite some progress, many people at high risk of malaria still lack access to services that can prevent, detect and treat the disease. Challenges in expanding access to malaria services have been compounded, particularly in sub-Saharan Africa, by the COVID-19 pandemic, converging humanitarian crises, restricted funding, weak surveillance systems, and declines in the effectiveness of core malaria-fighting tools. To address these threats and support countries in building more resilient malaria programmes, WHO recently published new strategies and frameworks, including: a new strategy to contain antimalarial drug resistance in Africa; a new initiative to stop the spread of Anopheles stephensi in urban environments;
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Yoruba text:

"With each of these advancements, many people who were at risk of severe malaria have not benefited from the services that could cure them, and have instead succumbed to the disease. Advocates for increased access to malaria treatment services have noted, especially in the North-Eastern part of Nigeria, the resurgence of COVID-19, the shortage of health workers, inadequate health financing, and increased research into malaria control interventions. To address these challenges and to support countries in developing effective malaria control programs, WHO has recently developed new tools and guidelines, including: a new operational strategy to combat malaria in Nigeria; new guidelines to control the breeding of Anopheles stephensi in urban areas."

translations.source: ìlànà-iṣẹ́ tuntun, tí WHO àti UN-Habitat jọ ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ rẹ̀, láti tọ́ àwọn adarí ìlú sọ́nà nínú ìdènà àìsàn ibà ní ìlú. irinṣẹ́ tuntun láti ran àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè lọ́wọ́ láti ṣe àyẹ̀wò ètò ìtọpinpin àìsàn ibà wọn àti láti mọ àwọn agbègbè fún ìdókówò WHO tún ti ṣe àfikún sí kíkó àkóyawọ́, àyípadà àti ànfààní sí àwọn àbá lórí àìsàn ibà rẹ̀. Àwọn Ìtọ́nisọ́nà Ìṣọ̀kan WHO fún àìsàn ibà ti wà lórí àwọn ìkànnì àkósódù méjì: MAGICapp àti ohun èlò ìmúṣiṣẹ́ “Malaria Toolkit”. WHO ṣe ìwúrí fún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ṣe àwọn àbá náà ní ìbámu pẹ̀lú àwọn ààyè ààrùn lábẹ́lé fún ipa tó pọ̀.
translations.reference: a new framework, developed jointly by WHO and UN-Habitat, to guide city leaders in urban malaria control. a new toolkit to help countries assess their malaria surveillance systems and identify areas for investment WHO has also increased the transparency, flexibility and access to its malaria recommendations. The consolidated WHO Guidelines for malaria are now available through two digital platforms: MAGICapp and the “Malaria Toolkit” app. WHO encourages countries to tailor the recommendations to local disease settings for maximum impact.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Yoruba text:

"A new approach, co-created by WHO and UN-Habitat, to guide city leaders in addressing urban malaria. The new guidance helps countries assess their malaria control programs and identify areas for WHO support. WHO has also added to the surveillance, prevention, and treatment guidance for communities on malaria. WHO’s Unified Health Tools for malaria are available on two platforms: MAGICapp and the “Malaria Toolkit” digital tool. WHO encourages countries to implement these measures in conjunction with household interventions for maximum impact."

translations.source: Àwọn àfojúsọ́nà fún àwọn ìdásí tuntun Ìdókòwò onítẹ̀síwájú nínú àgbékalẹ̀ àti ìṣàmúlò àwọn àjẹsára àìsàn ibà tuntun àti àwọn irinṣẹ́ ìran-tó-ń-bọ̀ máa ṣe pàtàkì láti ṣe àṣeyọrí àwọn àfojúsùn àìsàn ibà àgbáyé ọdún 2030. Àjẹsára àìsàn ibà kejì, àjẹsára R21/Matrix-M (R21), tí ó bá gba àṣẹ, lè ṣe ìrànwọ́ láti dí àlàfo ńlá tó wà láàrin ìfiránṣẹ́ àti ìbéèrè kí ó sì tẹ̀síwájú láti mú àdínkù bá àìlera àwọn ọmọdé àti ikú látara àìsàn ibà. Kàn-án-pá ló jẹ́ fún WHO láti tẹ̀síwájú àtúnyẹwò fínífíní àti ti akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ tó péye fún àjẹsára àìsàn ibà R21 lọ́gán tí àlékún dátà ààbò pàtàkì àti ipa láti abala 3 ìdánwò R21 tó ń lọ lọ́wọ́ bá ti wà nílẹ̀ tí wọ́n sì pèsè rẹ̀ fún WHO. Ìpín sí ìsòrí àfihàn àjẹsára àìsàn ibà RTS,S ní àfikún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè ní ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ ni ìrètí wà pé yóò bẹ̀rẹ̀ ní ìbẹ̀rẹ ọdún 2024.
translations.reference: Prospects for new interventions Continued investment in the development and deployment of new malaria vaccines and next-generation tools will be key to achieving the 2030 global malaria targets. A second malaria vaccine, the R21/Matrix-M (R21) vaccine, if approved, could help close the sizable gap between supply and demand and further reduce child illness and death from malaria. It is a priority for WHO to continue the thorough and efficient expert review of the R21 malaria vaccine once additional key safety and efficacy data from the ongoing R21 phase 3 trial are available and provided to WHO. Phased introductions of the RTS,S malaria vaccine in additional countries in Africa are expected to begin in early 2024.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Vaccines for new diseases Advanced development in the deployment and scaling up of new disease vaccines and vector control interventions will be crucial to achieving the 2030 global disease elimination goals. The second disease vaccine, the R21/Matrix-M (R21) vaccine, if approved, could help fill a significant gap between demand and supply and further reduce childhood illness and deaths from malaria. The WHO is working to accelerate rigorous and credible evaluation for the malaria vaccine R21, following the release of critical data and impact from Phase 3 of the R21 trial, which is currently underway and has been submitted to the WHO. The rollout of the RTS,S malaria vaccine in additional countries in sub-Saharan Africa is expected to begin in early 2024."

translations.source: Ní ààyè ìdènà alákòóba ààrùn, àwọn ohun èlò 28 tuntun ló wà nínú ojú òpó R&D. Àwọn irinṣẹ́ tó wà lábẹ́ ìgbéléwọ̀n ni, fún àpẹẹrẹ, àwọn ẹ̀yà tuntun àwọ̀n apẹ̀fọn, àwọn ìjẹ aṣàfojúsùn tí ó máa ń pe ẹ̀fọn, àwọn apanirun ní ààyè kan, àwọn atanipa ilé tó lágbára (àwọn túúbù Eaves) àti ṣíṣe àyípadà jínnì àwọn ẹ̀fọn. Àwọn oníwádìí ń mú ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn ìtọ́jú aláìní-ACT ní ọ̀kùkúdùn ní ẹ̀ka àwọn oògùn apa-àìsàn-ibà nítorí ìjẹyọ àti ìtànkálẹ̀ àwọn jáàgùn artemisinin. Ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àwọn oògùn ìran-tó-ń-bọ̀, bíi "triple-ACTs," tí ó ń lo àpapọ artemisinin àti àwọn oògùn alájọṣepọ̀ méjì, ti wà lójú ọ̀nà láti mú àdínkù bá ewu jáàgùn. Kókó ọ̀rọ̀ fún olóòtú: Fún àlàyé síwájú sí i nípa ìpolongo Àyájọ́ Ọjọ́ Àìsàn Ibà Lágbàyé WHO, kàn sí https://www.who.int/campaigns/world-malaria-day/2023
translations.reference: In the vector control space, there are 28 new products in the R&D pipeline. Tools under evaluation include, for example, new types of insecticide-treated nets, targeted baits that attract mosquitoes, spatial repellents, lethal house lures (eaves tubes) and genetic engineering of mosquitoes. Researchers are prioritizing the development of non-ACT treatments in the field of antimalarial medicines due to the emergence and spread of partial artemisinin resistance. The development of next-generation medicines, such as "triple ACTs," which use a combination of artemisinin and two partner drugs, is underway to reduce the risk of drug resistance. Notes to the editor:For more information on the WHO World Malaria Day campaign, visit: https://www.who.int/campaigns/world-malaria-day/2023
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Yoruba document:

"In the era of combating malaria, 28 new drug candidates are in the R&D pipeline. Those under investigation include, for example, new strains of parasites, innovative drug formulations that target parasites, combinations in a single pill, powerful household insecticides (Eaves tubes), and the production of artemisinin derivatives. Researchers are accelerating the development of next-generation inhibitors of the malaria parasite's ACT pathway in the eastern region of the anti-malarial drug sector due to the scarcity and instability of artemisinin supplies. The development of fixed-dose combination drugs, such as "triple-ACTs," which use artemisinin in combination with two other drugs, is being explored as a way to increase protection against drug resistance. Key message for the reader: For more information, refer to the WHO's Global Malaria Day Action Plan, see https://www.who.int/campaigns/world-malaria-day/2023"

translations.source: Ètò Ìfiránṣẹ́ Ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA sún sí ìpele tí ó kàn nínú ìdàgbàsókè rẹ̀ Ìfilọ́lẹ̀ ilé ìwòsan Ibùdó ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA ní Afrigen, ní orílẹ̀-èdè South Africa. Àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ kórajọ sí Cape Town láti jíròrò lórí ìpele tí ó kàn nínú ìdàgbàsókè àjẹsára mRNA àti ìgbaraẹni nímọ̀ràn. Ó lé ní 200 àwọn akópa ilẹ̀-òkèèrè tí wọ́n ń ṣiṣẹ́ pẹ̀lú Ètò Ìfiránṣẹ́ Ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA ni wọ́n pàdé ní Cape Town ní ọ̀sẹ̀ yìí fún ìpàdé ìfojúrinjú àkọ́kọ́ wọn. Olùdarí Àpapọ̀ Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé (WHO), Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus àti Dr Joe Phaahla, Mínísítà fún Ètò Ìlera àti Mínísítà fún Kátà-kárà àti Ilé-iṣẹ́, Mr Ebrahim Patel ti orílẹ̀-èdè South Africa papọ̀ pẹ̀lú àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ onípele-gíga láti àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí wọ́n ń pèsè owó ni wọ́n darapọ̀ mọ́ wọn, ìpàdé aláìlẹ́gbẹ́ yìí ṣe àtúnyẹ́wò ìlọsíwájú láti ìgbà tí WHO àti Medicine Patent Pool (MPP) ṣe ìfilọ́lẹ̀ rẹ̀ ní oṣù Òkudù ní ọdún 2021.
translations.reference: mRNA Technology Transfer Programme moves to the next phase of its development Inauguration of the mRNA technology Hub facility at Afrigen, South Africa. Partners gather in Cape Town to discuss the next phase of the mRNA vaccine development and knowledge sharing. Over 200 international participants working with the mRNA Technology Transfer Programme met in Cape Town this week for their first face-to-face meeting. Joined by World Health Organization (WHO) Director General, Dr. Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus and Dr. Joe Phaahla, Minister of Health and Minister of Trade and Industry, Mr. Ebrahim Patel of South Africa together with high-level officials from funding countries, this unique meeting reviewed the progress since WHO and Medicines Patent Pool (MPP) launched it in June 2021.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the provided Yoruba text:

mRNA Technology Transfer Initiative Advances in its Implementation The African Medicines Regulatory Hub for mRNA technology at Afrigen, in South Africa, has reached a significant milestone. Partners gathered in Cape Town to discuss the progress of mRNA vaccine and therapeutics development. Around 200 country representatives working with the mRNA Technology Transfer Initiative met in Cape Town this week for their first introductory meeting. The Director-General of the World Health Organization (WHO), Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus, and Dr Joe Phaahla, Minister of Health and Minister of Trade, Industry and Competition of South Africa, together with high-level officials from donor countries, joined them. This crucial meeting reviewed progress since the WHO and Medicine Patent Pool (MPP) launched it in October 2021.

translations.source: Inú mi dùn láti wà ní Cape Town níbi pẹ̀lú àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ wa láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àwòṣe alálòpẹ́ fún ìfiránṣẹ́ ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA láti fún àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ìpawówọlé wọn kéré àti agbede-méjì ní ànfààní adọ́gba sí àwọn àjẹsára àti àwọn ohun èlò ìlera adóólà-ẹ̀mí mìíràn," Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ WHO Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "Ìwúrí ńlá gidi gan-an ni àṣeyọrí gbogbo àwọn tí wọ́n lọ́wọ́ sí iṣẹ́-àkànṣe yìí jẹ́ fún mi; ní nǹkan bí ọdún méjì dín a ti fi hàn pé a lè jọ ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀, a ṣe àṣeyọrí lápapọ̀." Àwọn tí wọ́n kópa nínú ìpàdé náà ni àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ tí wọ́n ń ṣe ìpèsè ajẹmọ́-ìbí láti orílẹ̀-èdè 15 nínú ètò náà, àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́ tó ń léwájú, ilé-iṣẹ́, àwọn aṣojú ẹgbẹ́ àwùjọ, àti àwọn olùpèsè owó. Ní àkókò ìpàdé ọlọ́jọ́-márùn náà, àwọn akópa máa sọ nípa ìlọsíwájú wọn ó sì jíròrò lórí àwọn aṣokùnfà pàtàkì fún ìtẹ̀síwájú Ètò náà bíi àwọn ìṣòro ohun-ìní ọlọgbọ́n àti àwọn tó jẹ́ ti òfin, bákan náà ni sáyẹ́nsì fún ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA àti àwọn ìṣàmúlò pàtàkì tó níṣe pẹ̀lú LMICs ní agbègbè àwọn ààrùn mìíràn bíi KASA àti ikọ́fe. Àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn COVID-19 náà ti fi hàn pé àìdọ́gba tó pọ̀ wà nínú ànfààní sí àwọn ohun èlò ìlera, pàápàá jùlọ àwọn àjẹsára.
translations.reference: “I am delighted to be here in Cape Town with our partners to support a sustainable model for mRNA technology transfer to give low- and middle-income countries equitable access to vaccines and other lifesaving health products,” said WHO Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus. “I am immensely proud of the achievement of all those involved in this project; in less than two years we have shown that when we work collaboratively, we succeed collectively.” The meeting participants include biomanufacturing partners from 15 countries in the Programme, leading experts, industry, civil society representatives, and funders. During the five-day meeting, participants will share progress and discuss critical enablers for the sustainability of the Programme such as intellectual property issues and regulatory aspects, as well as the science of mRNA technologies and key applications relevant to LMICs in other disease areas such as HIV and tuberculosis. The COVID-19 pandemic has underscored that gross inequity exists in access to health products, especially vaccines.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's the English translation of the Yoruba document:

"I am delighted to be in Cape Town with our partners to reaffirm commitment to the demonstration project for mRNA technology transfer to low-income and lower-middle-income countries to provide access to vaccines and other life-saving health products," WHO Director-General Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus said. "The success of all those involved in this initiative is truly remarkable for me; for nearly two years, we have shown that we can work together, we have achieved success together." Participants in the meeting included partners implementing vaccine research and development from 15 countries within the initiative, leading scientists, industry, civil society advocates, and funders. During the clinical research meeting, participants shared updates and focused on critical gaps for advancing the initiative, such as intellectual property hurdles and those related to regulations, as well as support for mRNA technology and essential tools working with LMICs in other areas such as TB and malaria. The COVID-19 pandemic has highlighted the significant inequity in access to health products, particularly vaccines."

translations.source: Títí di oṣù Èrenà ọdún 2023, ọdún mẹ́ta lé lẹ́yìn tí WHO kéde COVID-19 gẹ́gẹ́ bíi Pàjáwírì Ìlera Àwùjọ fún Ẹ̀dun Ọkàn Ilẹ̀-òkèèrè (PHEIC), 69.7% àwọn ènìyàn lágbàyé ti gba ó kéré jù oògùn àjẹsára COVID-19 kan. Pàtàkì jùlọ, ìpín yìí ṣì wà lábẹ́ 30% ní àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè tí ìpawówọlé wọn kéré (LCIs). Ètò yìí gbèrò láti ṣe àfikún sí ànfààní adọ́gba sí àjẹsára mRNA pẹ̀lú ṣíṣe àfikún sí pínpín ìkápá ìpèsè alálòpẹ́ jákèjádò LMICs, ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún àjọṣepọ̀ ní ẹkùn agbègbè àti ẹlẹ́kùnjẹkùn, àti ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ àti Ìrónilágbára fún àwọn òṣìṣẹ́ lábẹ́lé nípasẹ ìdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ àkànṣe àti aláfibọ̀ àti ìrànlọ́wọ́ àwọn akọ́ṣẹ́mọṣẹ́. Nǹkan tó dá yátọ̀ nínú Àwòṣe Ìfiránṣẹ́ Ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA ni ìgbésẹ̀ oríṣiríṣi tí yóò fi ààyè sílẹ̀ fún pínpín àwọn ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ fún oríṣiríṣi àwọn tí yóò gbà á kí ó ba lè dé ọ̀dọ àwọn tó nílò rẹ̀ nípasẹ àwọn ìpèsè lábẹ́lé àti ẹkùn agbègbè kíákíá. Dr Phaahla, Mínísítà fún Ètò Ìlera ní orílẹ̀-èdè South Africa, sọ wí pé: "Nǹkan tí a rí níbi lónì, jẹ́ àkókò kan nínú ìtàn, Ètò kan tí wọ́n gbèrò láti ró àwọn LMIC lágbára nípasẹ ojú òpó àjọṣepọ̀ àgbáyé.
translations.reference: As of March 2023, more than three years after WHO declared COVID-19 a Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC), 69.7% of the global population had received at least one dose of a COVID-19 vaccine. Notably, this proportion still remains below 30% in low-income countries (LICs) . This Programme aims to contribute to equitable access to mRNA vaccines by increasing the distribution of sustainable manufacturing capacity across LMICs, enhancing regional and inter-regional collaborations, and developing and empowering a local workforce through tailored and inclusive training and expert support. What is unique in the mRNA Technology Transfer model is the multilateral process that allows sharing of technologies to multiple recipients so that through local and regional production those in need can be reached rapidly. Dr Phaahla, Minister of Health South Africa, said: “What we see here today, is a moment in history, a Programme that is aimed at empowering LMICs through a global collaborative network.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"As of August 2023, three years after the WHO declared COVID-19 a Public Health Emergency of International Concern (PHEIC), 69.7% of the world's population had received at least one dose of a COVID-19 vaccine. More concerningly, this figure was below 30% in low-income countries (LCIs). This initiative aims to add to the expansion of access to mRNA vaccines with additional support for the rollout of primary vaccination in low- and middle-income countries (LMICs), strengthening partnerships at the regional and sub-regional levels, and establishing capacity building and sustainability for healthcare workers through training, mentorship, and support from healthcare professionals. A key differentiator in the mRNA Technology Transfer Initiative is the phased approach that will allow for the gradual scaling up of technologies for different manufacturers to reach those in need through primary care and sub-regional settings. Dr Phaahla, the Minister of Health in South Africa, stated: "What we are seeing today is a moment in history, an initiative designed to empower LMICs through a global partnership."

translations.source: Inú mi dùn láti rí ìtẹ̀síwájú tí ó ti di ṣíṣe láàrín àkókò díẹ̀ mo sì gba àtìlẹ́yìn láti ọ̀dọ ọ̀pọ̀lọpọ̀ àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè lóríṣiríṣi - àwọn orílẹ̀-èdè bíi South Africa tí ó ní ìkápá ìpèsè ajẹmọ́-ìbí tó ń ṣiṣẹ́ dáadáa tó sì lágbára tí wọ́n sì ti ṣetán láti ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀, kẹ́kọ̀ọ́ láti ọ̀dọ ara wọn kí wọ́n sì tún sọ ọ́ fún ará wọn." Mínísítà Blade Nzimande fún Ètò-ẹ̀kọ́ Ilé-ìwé Gíga àti Ìmọ̀ Sáyẹ́nsì, sọ wí pé: Tiwa ni àfojúsùn Ibùdó Ìfiránṣẹ́ Ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA tayọ COVID-19 nìkan. Àwọn ìkápá tí à ń ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ rẹ̀ ń wá láti ró wa lágbára láti kojú àwọn àjàkálẹ̀ ààrùn ọjọ́ iwájú mìíràn tí àjẹsára wọn ò lè lo àwọn ìkànnì ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA kan náà. Ó jẹ́ àfojúsùn kan tí ó fi tẹjú rẹ̀ mọ́ àwọn ààrùn tí wọ́n wọ́pọ̀ ní àwọn agbègbè wa bíi àìsàn ibà, ikọ́fe àti KASA." Ìjókòó náà tún sààmì àṣeyọrí pàtàkì fún ètò náà pẹ̀lú ìfilọ́lẹ̀ Ilé Ìwòsàn Ibùdó Ìfiránṣẹ́ Ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA ní Afrigen ní Cape Town ní ojú Dr Tedros àti Dr Matshidoso Moeti Olùdarí Ẹkùn Agbègbè WHO fún Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀, Charles Gore Olùdarí Aláṣẹ fún MPP àti Ọ̀narébù Mínísítà tí ó kópa nínú ayẹyẹ gígé ríbínì ní Afrigen.
translations.reference: I am thrilled to see the progress made in such a relatively short time and welcome the support from so many different countries - countries like South Africa that have a strong vibrant biomanufacturing capacity and that are willing to work together, learn from and share with each other.” Minister Blade Nzimande of Higher Education and Science, said: “Ours is the vision of the mRNA Technology Transfer Hub beyond just COVID-19. The capabilities we are building are looking to empower us to deal with other future pandemics whose vaccines could use the same mRNA technology platform. It is a vision that has set its eyes on diseases that are prevalent in our environments such as Malaria, Tuberculosis and HIV and AIDS.” This occasion also marks an important milestone for the Programme with the inauguration of the mRNA technology Hub facility at Afrigen in Cape Town in the presence of Dr. Tedros and Dr. Matshidiso Moeti WHO Regional Director for Africa, Charles Gore Executive Director of MPP and the Honorable Ministers who took part in a ribbon cutting ceremony at Afrigen.
translations.hypothesis:

"I am delighted to see the progress that has been made between a few months and I have received support from representatives of various countries - countries like South Africa which has a well-functioning intellectual property system and is strong and has agreed to work together, learning from each other and then sharing it with others." Minister Blade Nzimande for Higher Education, Science and Innovation said: "Our priority is the local production of the COVID-19 mRNA vaccine. The capacities we are developing will empower us to address future disease outbreaks where current mRNA technology cannot be used. It is a priority that addresses diseases that are prevalent in our regions such as tuberculosis, pneumonia and KASA." The event also highlighted the critical success of the program with the launch of the African Union’s first mRNA vaccine manufacturing facility, Afrigen in Cape Town, in the presence of Dr Tedros and Dr Matshidoso Moeti, Regional Director of WHO for Africa, Charles Gore, Executive Director for MPP and the Honorable Minister who participated in the grand opening ceremony at Afrigen. "

translations.source: Ọ̀jọ̀gbọ́n Petro Terblanche, Olùdarí Aláṣẹ fún Afrigen sọ wí pé: "Inú gbogbo ikọ̀ Afrigen dùn láti ṣe àṣeyọrí pàtàkì yìí pẹ̀lú píparí ìkànnì ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA náà. Ìkànnì yìí wà nínú ìdàgbàsókè àjẹsára mRNA láti ìbẹ̀rẹ̀-dé-òpin àti ìpèsè ilé ìwòsàn níbi tí àwọn olùdíje Ibùdó mRNA àjẹsára COVID-19 AfriVac 2121 ti wà ní abala ìfẹ̀lójú. Láti bíi oṣù 18 sẹ́yìn, Afrigen ti la àyípadà tó lápẹẹrẹ kọjá pẹ̀lú àtìlẹyìn ojú òpó àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ àti àwọn olùbánidámọ́ràn tí Ètò yìí fi ààyè gbà. A ti fi kún ìkápá àti ìkáátò wa láti dọ́gba pẹ̀lú àwọn òdiwọ̀n ojúlówó tó ga jùlọ fún àgbékalẹ̀ àjẹsára mRNA, tí ó ń ṣiṣẹ́ fún èròngbà láti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìkápá alálòpẹ́ nínú LMICs láti pèsè àwọn àjẹsára mRNA ". Afrigen ti ṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìlànà ìpèsè àjẹsára COVID-19 ní àṣeyọrí pẹ̀lú òdiwọn ààyè fún ìmọ̀ ìjìnlẹ̀ tí ó sì ń ṣe ìfẹ̀lójú ìlànà yẹn dé ìpele tí ó ṣe é lò fún ìgbà pípẹ́ fún ìpèsè àwọn abala àjẹsára tí yóò jẹ́ lílo nínú Abala I/II àyẹ̀wò ajẹmọ́-ìtọ́jú sí àwọn òdiwọ̀n GMP.
translations.reference: Professor Petro Terblanche, Executive Director of Afrigen said: “The entire Afrigen team are thrilled to reach this important milestone with the completion of the mRNA technology platform. This platform is housed within the end-to-end mRNA vaccine development and production facility where the mRNA Hub Covid 19 vaccine candidate AfriVac 2121 is currently in scale-up phase. Over the last 18 months, Afrigen has undergone an incredible transformation with the support of a network of partners and mentors enabled by this Programme. We have grown our capability and capacity to meet the highest quality standards of mRNA vaccine development, serving the objective to build sustainable capacity in LMICs to produce mRNA vaccines”. Afrigen has successfully established a COVID-19 vaccine manufacturing process at a laboratory scale and is currently scaling up that process to a level suitable for manufacturing vaccine batches to be used in Phase I/ II clinical trials to GMP standards.
translations.hypothesis:
Here's a translation of the Yoruba document into English:

"Mr. Petro Terblanche, the Managing Director of Afrigen, stated that: "The entire Afrigen team is delighted to successfully implement this critical mRNA technology transfer. This transfer is part of the mRNA vaccine manufacturing capacity building initiative, from bench-to-bedside and hospital readiness, where the developers of the COVID-19 mRNA vaccine, AfriVac 2121, have been in the development phase. Since around February 18, Afrigen has been working diligently with the support of partners and donors who have embraced this program. We have leveraged our expertise and experience to collaborate with leading experts in mRNA vaccine manufacturing, working towards establishing a robust system for manufacturing sterile injectable mRNA vaccines in LMICs (Low and Middle-Income Countries). Afrigen has successfully established a vaccine manufacturing process with a strong commitment to scientific excellence and is further developing that process to a level that will ensure its sustainability for the manufacturing of vaccine doses to be used in Phase I/II clinical trials to meet GMP (Good Manufacturing Practice) standards."

translations.source: Ní ìlànà adọ́gba, Afrigen máa tẹ̀síwájú láti ṣe ìdánilẹ́kọ̀ọ́ àti ìfiránṣẹ́ ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ fún àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ ojú òpó náà. Àwọn olùdásílẹ̀ náà kó ipa pàtàkì nínú ṣíṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún Ètò náà pẹ̀lú ìpèsè owó lápapọ̀ títí di òní ní US$ 177 mílíọ̀nù tí orílẹ̀-èdè France sì jẹ́ àkọ́kọ́ láti pèsè owó fún iṣẹ́ ìfiránṣẹ́ ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA náà. Àwọn tó péjú síbi ìpàdé náà ni àwọn aṣojú láti Àjọ Ilẹ̀ Yúróópù, Belgium, Germany bákan náà ni Norway, Canada, Àjọ Ìṣọ̀kan Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀, South Africa, àti ELMA Foundation. Caroline Delaney, Olùdarí Àpapọ̀ GlobalAffairs Canada fún Gúúsù àti Ìlà-oòrùn Ilẹ̀ Adúláwọ̀ sọ wí pé: "Orílẹ̀-èdè Canada ń ṣe ìfirinlẹ̀ ìtẹ̀síwájú àtìlẹyìn rẹ̀ fún Ètò Ìfiránṣẹ́ Ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA inú wa sì dùn láti kéde ìtẹ̀síwájú àfikún 15 mílíọ̀nù dọ́là owó orílẹ̀-èdè Canada níbi ìpàdé náà lónì. Èyí mú gbogbo owó tí orílẹ̀-èdè Canada ti fi dásí Ètò náà jẹ́ 45 mílíọ̀nù dólà owó orílẹ̀-èdè Canada.
translations.reference: In a parallel process, Afrigen will continue to carry out training and technology transfer to the network partners. The funders play a crucial role in supporting the Programme with total funding to date at US$ 117 million and France being the first to fund the mRNA technology transfer work. Present at the meeting were representatives from the European Commission, Belgium, Germany, as well as Norway, Canada, African Union, South Africa, and the ELMA Foundation. Caroline Delany, GlobalAffairs Canada’s Director General for Southern and Eastern Africa said: “Canada reaffirms its continued support of the mRNA Technology Transfer Programme and we are delighted to announce a further contribution of 15 million Canadian dollars at the meeting today. This brings Canada’s total contribution to the Programme up to 45 million Canadian dollars.

translations.source: Àwọn owó wọ̀nyìí ń ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún iṣẹ́ ṣíṣe ní Ibùdó náà ní orílẹ̀-èdè South Africa àti àwọn alájọṣepọ̀ ojú òpó káàkiri àgbáyé. A ní ìgbàgbọ́ tó rinlẹ̀ nínú pàtàkì ṣíṣe àgbékalẹ̀ ìkápá ní ìpele orílẹ̀-èdè àti ẹkùn agbègbè ". Mr Martin Seychell, Igbákejì Olùdarí Aláṣẹ Àjọ Ilẹ̀ Yúróópù, ló sọ bẹ́ẹ̀. "EC ti dá €40 mílíọ̀nù tẹ́lẹ̀ fún ìdásílẹ̀ Ibùdó Ìfiránṣẹ́ Ìmọ̀-ẹ̀rọ mRNA ó sì ti buwọ́lù owó ìrànwọ́ láìpẹ́ yìí pẹ̀lu EIB fún €15,5 mílíọ̀nù láti ṣe àtìlẹ́yìn fún ìfẹ̀lójú ìkápá ìpèsè àwọn àjẹsára. Èyí jẹ́ lára àpapọ ìdókówò lábẹ́ ìlànà-iṣẹ́ EU Global Gateway, níbi tí wọ́n ti ṣe àkójọ €1 bílíọ̀nù lé lábẹ́ Ikọ̀ Ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ Ilẹ̀ Yúróópù lórí Ìpèsè àwọn ohun èlò ìlera. Ó ti wá ṣe pàtàkì pàápàá báyìí láti kojú àwọn ìfúnpinpin òfin àti ìbéèrè tó ṣe é ṣe, láti rí i dájú pé kìí ṣe ìpèsè àjẹsára tí wọ́n jẹ́ ojúlówó tó dára jùlọ ní gbogbo ẹkùn agbègbè àgbáyé nìkan, ṣùgbọ́n pẹ̀lu ànfààní ojú ẹsẹ̀ àti adọ́gba sí àwọn àjẹsára wọ̀nyẹn."
translations.reference: These funds support the running of the Hub in South Africa as well as network partners around the world. We firmly believe in the importance of building capacity at local and regional level”. This is part of the overall investment under the EU Global Gateway strategy, where more than €1 Billion have been mobilised under the Team Europe Initiative on Manufacturing health products. It is now particularly important to also address possible regulatory and demand bottlenecks, to ensure not only production of vaccines of the highest standard in all regions of the world, but also rapid and equitable access to those vaccines.”

translations.source: Àwọn ìyàn sípò aṣíwájú pàtàkì tí wọ́n ṣe láti darí ìlànà-iṣẹ́ ìdarí àti ìpilẹ̀ṣẹ̀ WHO Àjọ Elétò Ìlera Àgbáyé (WHO) ti ṣe ìyànsípò àwọn lọ́gà-lọ́gà márùn-ún tuntun sí ikò adarí olú ilé-iṣẹ́ rẹ̀ ní Geneva. Àwọn ìyànsípò tuntun náà tẹ̀lé ìtúnni yàn sípò Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus sí sáà ọlọ́dún-márùn-ún kejì gẹ́gẹ́ bíi Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀. Àpapọ ikọ̀ àwọn olùdarí náà ni wọ́n ti ṣe láti dọ́gba pẹ̀lú àwọn ohun pàtàkì Àjọ náà fún ọdún márùn-ún tó ń bọ̀ tí wọn ó sì ṣiṣẹ́ papọ̀ pẹ̀lú Olùdarí-Àpapọ̀ láti mú ìtẹ̀síwájú bá àwọn nǹkan pàtàkì wọ̀nyìí àti èròngbà àyípadà àlá WHO. Láti mú kí ìlọsíwájú yá lórí ìṣàmúlò Ètò Àpapọ̀ fún Iṣẹ́ WHO 13th (GPW13), àti àṣeyọrí àfojúsùn "bílíọ̀nù mẹ́ta" rẹ̀ àti Àwọn Èròngbà Ìdàgbàsókè Alálòpẹ́ ajẹmọ́-ìlera náà, iṣẹ́ WHO fún ọdún márùn-ún tó ń bọ̀ dá lórí àwọn nǹkan pàtàkì márùn-ún:
translations.reference: Key leadership appointments made to drive WHO strategic direction and initiatives The World Health Organization (WHO) has appointed five new senior figures to its headquarters leadership team in Geneva. The new appointments follow the reappointment of Dr Tedros Adhanom Ghebreyesus to his second five-year term as Director-General. The overall leadership team has been consolidated to align with the Organization’s priorities for the next five years and will work closely with the Director-General to drive forward these priorities and WHO’s ambitious transformation agenda. To accelerate progress on implementation of WHO’s 13th General Programme of Work (GPW13), and achievement of its “triple billion” targets and the health-related Sustainable Development Goals, WHO’s work for the coming five years is focused on five priorities:

**Succession Plan for the Director-General of the World Health Organization**

The World Health Organization (WHO) has appointed seven new senior positions to the office of the Director-General in Geneva. translations.reference: making a paradigm shift towards promoting health and preventing disease by addressing its root causes; radically reorienting health systems towards primary health care; strengthening the systems for health emergency preparedness and response; harnessing the power of science, innovation, data and technologies to advance health; and strengthening WHO as the leading authority on global health and to achieve impact in countries.
